speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's been about a week — watery diarrhea that just won't quit, this awful smell, and lately some blood. I've got these gripping cramps and now my whole belly feels bloated and tight.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've always been healthy. No stomach problems before this, no surgeries.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The only thing I've taken lately was antibiotics for my acne — clindamycin, I think — a long course my dermatologist gave me. I finished it about three weeks ago. No allergies.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No, nobody in my family has Crohn's or colitis or anything like that.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No trips, no camping, nobody around me is sick, and I haven't been in the hospital. I eat normal food.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing and clammy, mildly lethargic but answering questions. Flushed and diaphoretic, looks his stated age. In evident discomfort, lying still with knees drawn up.
Distended and tympanitic to percussion, diffusely tender — greatest across the lower abdomen — with involuntary guarding but no frank rigidity. Bowel sounds markedly hypoactive.
Loose, watery brown stool in the vault with a strongly foul odor and streaks of fresh blood. No mass, fissure, or fluctuance. Stool strongly heme-positive.
Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill about 3 seconds, cool extremities and mild skin tenting — clinically volume-depleted.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Stool sent for C. difficile — PCR and toxin. Result's back on the panel.

labResults.name: C. difficile toxin B gene (tcdB) PCR
Stool bacterial culture
labResults.value: No Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, or Shiga-toxin E. coli isolated

diagnosticReports.title: Abdominal Radiograph (Supine and Upright)
diagnosticReports.body: Marked dilation of the transverse colon measuring up to 6.5 cm with nodular thumbprinting of the colonic wall and loss of normal haustral pattern; no free air identified. IMPRESSION: Colonic dilation with mucosal thumbprinting concerning for severe colitis; no perforation.

image.query: supine abdominal radiograph dilated transverse colon thumbprinting

diagnosticReports.title: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Diffuse circumferential wall thickening involving the entire colon with mural low-attenuation stratification (target/accordion appearance) and pronounced haustral thickening. Pericolonic fat stranding and a trace of free fluid. Maximal transverse colonic luminal diameter measures 6.2 cm. No pneumoperitoneum, discrete abscess, or evidence of perforation. IMPRESSION: Pancolitis with marked mural thickening and early colonic dilatation, concerning for a severe inflammatory/infectious process with early toxic dilatation. No free air.

image.query: CT abdomen pelvis pancolitis colonic wall thickening accordion sign
12-lead sinus tachycardia normal axis

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Oral vancomycin ordered and on its way up from pharmacy.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Vancomycin route/dose not specified — give 125–500 mg by mouth or enteral tube; IV vancomycin does NOT reach the colon and will not treat this.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fidaxomicin ordered as the oral agent.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Fidaxomicin dose not specified — 200 mg by mouth twice daily.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Noted — the clindamycin course is already finished, and I've flagged the chart so no further unnecessary antibiotics go in.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Two large-bore IVs in — bolusing balanced crystalloid now and I'll keep it running.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: ID here — metronidazole alone won't cut it for disease this severe. Make an oral agent your backbone; IV metronidazole is only an add-on if he's truly fulminant.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Whoa — don't slow the gut on a colitis like this. That's how you back everything up and tip a distended colon into ileus. Hold it.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Contact precautions up — gown, gloves, private room, and soap-and-water hand hygiene at the door.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — no antimotility or antidiarrheal agents, and I've flagged it so nothing gets ordered by mistake.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Surgery — thanks for the early heads-up. We'll follow him closely; if that colon keeps dilating or he doesn't turn around, we're talking about taking him to the OR.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: GI on the phone — with that recent antibiotic course and this picture, get the stool study and start enteral therapy; we'll co-manage on admission.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed is ready — admitting with surgery on board and fluids running. Handoff called.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity — early fulminant colitis progressing toward toxic megacolon and shock if targeted therapy, monitoring, and resuscitation are delayed

stages.event: Progressive abdominal distension and early hemodynamic drift
stages.nurse: Doctor, his belly is more swollen and firmer than when he rolled in, his fever's climbing and the pressure is starting to slip. I need orders.
stages.feedback: Early features of fulminant disease were emerging while targeted enteral therapy had not yet been started.

stages.event: Toxic megacolon with septic shock
stages.nurse: His abdomen is rigid and drum-tight now and I can't hold his pressure on fluids alone — he's crashing, I need decisions this second!
stages.feedback: Untreated, the colitis progressed to toxic megacolon and shock; definitive antibiotic therapy, aggressive resuscitation, and early surgical involvement were required to prevent collapse.

debrief.goodOutcome: Antibiotic-associated C. difficile colitis recognized and treated before it became fulminant; the patient was resuscitated, isolated, and admitted with surgery involved.
debrief.badOutcome: The recent antibiotic link was missed and targeted therapy delayed; the colitis advanced to toxic megacolon and shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You elicited the recent clindamycin course, sent C. difficile testing, started oral vancomycin while stopping unnecessary antibiotics, screened for fulminant disease with exam and imaging, avoided antimotility agents, placed contact precautions, and resuscitated and admitted with surgery on board — the dangerous complication averted.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter drifted without connecting the recent antibiotics to the diarrhea; targeted therapy and source control were delayed, antimotility agents or missed dilation went unchecked, and the patient progressed to toxic megacolon and septic shock.
learningPoints: Recent antibiotic use plus worsening, foul, or bloody diarrhea should prompt C. difficile testing and empiric treatment.
Severe C. difficile colitis is treated with ORAL (enteral) vancomycin or fidaxomicin — not IV vancomycin, and not metronidazole as first-line.
Fulminant C. difficile means hypotension/shock, ileus, or toxic megacolon; screen for it with serial exam, lactate, WBC, and abdominal imaging, and involve surgery early.
Avoid antimotility agents in colitis — they can precipitate ileus and toxic megacolon.
Institute contact precautions with soap-and-water hand hygiene to prevent spread.
missedOpportunities: Eliciting the recent clindamycin (antibiotic) exposure
Sending C. difficile stool testing (PCR/toxin)
Starting oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin
Assessing for toxic megacolon with abdominal imaging and avoiding antimotility agents
Instituting contact precautions and resuscitating/admitting with early surgical involvement

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: It started about five days ago with belly cramps, then diarrhea that turned bloody. It all began a few days after a family cookout — the burgers were a little pink. His little sister had a mild tummy bug too. Today he looks pale and he's barely peeing.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's a healthy kid, no medications, no allergies.

physicalExam.finding: Pale and tired-appearing, mild periorbital puffiness. Mucous membranes tacky. Non-toxic but clearly unwell.
Diffusely tender with voluntary guarding, no rebound, no mass. Hyperactive bowel sounds.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Stool sent for the full panel including Shiga toxin and E. coli O157.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Heme labs are back — hemoglobin's dropped to 8.9, platelets are only 62, and the smear shows schistocytes.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Chem shows an acute kidney injury — creatinine's up to 2.1 for a kid his size, potassium 5.4.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Careful isotonic fluids running with strict intake-and-output charting and frequent reassessment.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — no antibiotics for suspected Shiga toxin E. coli; they can worsen the HUS.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Agreed — no antimotility agents; they raise the risk of HUS.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Copy — anemia, low platelets, and acute kidney injury together. You're calling this evolving HUS.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — in suspected Shiga toxin E. coli, antibiotics increase the risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome. This is exactly the case where you do NOT give them.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Don't — antimotility agents also increase the risk of HUS in Shiga-toxin colitis. Avoid them.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Pediatric nephrology — agree, evolving HUS. Admit for close monitoring; we'll watch the platelets, creatinine, and urine output and be ready if he needs dialysis.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Admitted for HUS monitoring — nephrology on board, strict I/O and serial labs ordered.

deterioration.trajectory: STEC colitis evolving to HUS — worsens with antibiotics, antimotility agents, or under-resuscitation

stages.event: Worsening HUS with oliguria
stages.nurse: He hasn't made urine in over an hour and his blood pressure's creeping up — he looks worse.
stages.feedback: Careful isotonic fluid support is nephroprotective in evolving HUS; under-resuscitation accelerates the kidney injury.

debrief.goodOutcome: STEC colitis with evolving HUS recognized and managed supportively.
debrief.outcomeGood: You sent Shiga toxin/O157 stool studies, screened for HUS with a smear, renal function, LDH and haptoglobin, recognized the evolving HUS, correctly AVOIDED antibiotics and antimotility agents, gave careful isotonic fluids, and admitted with nephrology.
debrief.outcomePoor: Antibiotics or antimotility agents were given, or the HUS was under-supported.
debrief.badOutcome: The evolving HUS was worsened by a contraindicated antibiotic or antimotility agent, or by inadequate supportive care.
learningPoints: In suspected Shiga toxin-producing E. coli, AVOID antibiotics and antimotility agents — both increase the risk of HUS.
Screen for HUS with CBC and smear (schistocytes), platelets, creatinine, LDH, and haptoglobin.
Recognize evolving HUS as the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury.
Give careful isotonic IV fluids with strict input/output monitoring and involve nephrology early.
missedOpportunities: Sending Shiga toxin / O157 stool studies
Avoiding antibiotics and antimotility agents
Screening labs for HUS (smear, LDH, haptoglobin, creatinine)

speech.text: It started about five weeks ago. Now I'm going eight or nine times a day and there's blood every time. My stomach cramps and I'm so tired I can't get to school.

speech.speaker: patient

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Agreed — she's showing systemic toxicity: febrile, tachycardic, anemic, and volume down. I'm treating this as a severe, high-risk presentation until proven otherwise.

speech.speaker: nurse

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — no antidiarrheals, opioids, or antispasmodics on her orders. I'll flag it for the whole team.

speech.speaker: consultant

speech.speaker: consultant

speech.speaker: consultant

speech.speaker: nurse

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity — evolving toxic distension trending toward perforation and shock if not resuscitated, imaged, and escalated

stages.event: Worsening distension and hemodynamic stress
stages.nurse: Doctor, her belly's more swollen and tight and her heart rate keeps climbing — she looks worse. What are we doing for her?
stages.feedback: Systemic toxicity with volume depletion demands prompt recognition and resuscitation; delay lets the picture spiral.

stages.event: Progressive abdominal distension and rising fever
stages.nurse: Her abdomen is huge and rock-hard now and she's barely answering me. I need a plan.
stages.feedback: Without imaging the degree of colonic dilation goes unrecognized and the surgical clock keeps running.

stages.event: Colonic perforation with septic shock
stages.nurse: She's crashing — pressure's bottoming out and her belly's rigid. Get in here now!
stages.feedback: Uncorrected toxicity and hypovolemia culminated in perforation and shock — the core resuscitation was never delivered.

debrief.goodOutcome: Severe colitis with systemic toxicity recognized, imaged, and stabilized; infection excluded before immunosuppression; GI and surgery engaged early.
debrief.outcomeGood: You identified a severe, toxic colitis, resuscitated her and corrected the anemia and hypokalemia, imaged the colon, sent stool studies to exclude infection, kept her NPO, avoided antimotility agents, and got GI and surgery involved before starting steroids.
debrief.outcomePoor: Delay in recognizing the toxicity, missing the colonic dilation, or giving an antimotility/opioid agent let the distension progress toward perforation and shock.
debrief.badOutcome: The evolving toxic megacolon went unrecognized and unresuscitated — or was worsened by an antimotility agent — progressing to perforation and septic shock.
learningPoints: A chronic (weeks-long) course of bloody diarrhea with weight loss and anemia in an adolescent suggests new-onset IBD.
Toxic megacolon is a medical and surgical emergency — image for colonic dilation and involve surgery early.
Exclude infectious colitis (stool studies including C. difficile and STEC) before starting immunosuppression.
Avoid antimotility agents, opioids, and anticholinergics — they can precipitate toxic megacolon.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing systemic toxicity and resuscitating with fluids, potassium, and blood
Abdominal imaging to assess for colonic dilation
Stool studies to exclude infection before immunosuppression
Early pediatric GI and surgery consultation
Avoiding antimotility, opioid, and anticholinergic agents

deterioration.trajectory: hemodynamically stable, diagnostic case — the risk is a missed diagnosis, not deterioration

stages.event: Diagnostic anchoring — parent-driven narrative
stages.nurse: Doctor, Mom's getting anxious and doing all the talking — she keeps pushing the Crohn's angle. Are we any closer to a plan?
stages.feedback: Without a private, non-judgmental interview the sexual history stays hidden and the workup drifts toward IBD — the STI gets missed.

debrief.goodOutcome: STI proctitis identified and treated; confidentiality preserved.
debrief.outcomeGood: You interviewed her privately, uncovered the sexual history, tested and empirically treated for gonorrhea/chlamydia, and counseled on partners — the commonly-missed diagnosis, caught.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter ended without uncovering the sexual history, so the STI was missed and the workup anchored on IBD.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on the family history of Crohn's, the sexually transmitted infection went undiagnosed and untreated.
learningPoints: Interview adolescents privately — disclosure of sexual activity rarely happens in front of a parent.
STI proctitis is a commonly-missed cause of adolescent proctocolitis; a confidential sexual history is the key diagnostic step.
Send rectal NAAT for gonorrhea/chlamydia, screen for HIV/syphilis, and treat empirically with ceftriaxone + doxycycline.
A family history of IBD is a classic red herring — exclude STI before committing to an IBD diagnosis.
missedOpportunities: Interviewing the patient with her parent stepped out of the room
Rectal NAAT for gonorrhea and chlamydia
Empiric ceftriaxone plus doxycycline

Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds. No rebound or guarding. No acute abdominal source identified.

deterioration.trajectory: afebrile early sepsis progressing to septic shock if the atypical presentation goes unrecognized and untreated

stages.event: Escalating hypoxia and worsening mentation
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's more out of it than when she rolled in and her breathing's picking up — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: The subtle indicators of organ dysfunction were never actively screened for, and the absence of fever kept this from being treated with urgency.

stages.event: Refractory hypotension — evolving septic shock
stages.nurse: Doctor, her pressure keeps dropping and she's barely responding to me now — I need orders.
stages.feedback: Without prompt fluid resuscitation and antibiotics, the untreated infection progressed to overt septic shock.

stages.event: Cardiovascular collapse
stages.nurse: Doctor, I'm barely getting a pressure and she's not responding to anything — she's crashing!
stages.feedback: Septic shock left unresuscitated progressed to multi-organ failure and cardiovascular collapse.

debrief.goodOutcome: Afebrile sepsis recognized despite the absence of fever; source identified and controlled with fluids, antibiotics, and pressor support as needed — she stabilizes and is admitted.
debrief.outcomeGood: You didn't let a normal temperature rule out sepsis — the altered mentation, tachypnea, and relative hypotension were screened for and taken seriously. Lactate and cultures went out before antibiotics, fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics followed promptly, the pulmonary source was identified on exam and imaging, and she was admitted stable.
debrief.outcomePoor: The absence of fever anchored this toward simple dehydration or 'failure to thrive.' Organ dysfunction went unscreened, resuscitation was delayed, and she progressed to septic shock.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on the normal-to-low temperature, sepsis went unrecognized and untreated until she decompensated into septic shock.
learningPoints: Elderly and immunocompromised patients often mount a blunted febrile response — a normal or low temperature must never be used to exclude sepsis.
Early sepsis can present with subtle clinical and laboratory indicators of organ dysfunction, such as altered mentation, tachypnea, and relative hypotension, rather than overt fever.
Validated screening and scoring for early recognition of sepsis matter most in patients with vague, nonspecific symptoms of infection.
Hemodynamic optimization, cultures and lactate before antibiotics, prompt broad-spectrum antibiotics, and infection source identification/control remain the core of early sepsis management.
Unrecognized atypical sepsis progresses to septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction, while early recognition and treatment change the outcome.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing that a normal or low temperature does not exclude sepsis in an elderly patient
Actively screening for subtle organ dysfunction (altered mentation, tachypnea, relative hypotension) despite the absence of fever
Obtaining lactate and blood/sputum cultures before starting antibiotics
Prompt IV fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics
Identifying the pulmonary infection source on chest exam and imaging
Escalating to vasopressors if hypotension persisted despite fluids

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fingerstick glucose reads HIGH — meter says over 500.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Gas is back — pH 7.09, bicarb is 8.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Chem panel is up — sugar 512, potassium 5.2, bicarb low, creatinine a little up.

image.query: sinus tachycardia peaked T waves hyperkalemia ECG

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Isotonic fluids wide open — first liter going in. His heart rate is already settling.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Doctor — we don't have a potassium back yet. Do you want me to hold the insulin until we check it?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Insulin infusion started at 0.1 units/kg/hr — no bolus. Glucose trending down, we'll watch the potassium closely.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Insulin infusion ordered without a rate — specify 0.1 units/kg/hr, and NO IV bolus in a young patient.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Switching to dextrose-containing fluids so we can keep the insulin running as the sugar comes down.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: A1c is 12.8 — consistent with new-onset diabetes. Urinalysis shows ketones and glucose, no clear infection.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Hourly glucose and q2-hour electrolytes ordered, and I'll do frequent neuro checks watching for any headache or drop in mental status.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Endocrine here — agree with new-onset type 1 DKA. Continue the protocol, we'll take over insulin titration on the unit.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed confirmed — the DKA protocol travels with him.

deterioration.trajectory: high-anion-gap acidosis with dehydration; deteriorates if fluids/insulin are delayed or if insulin precedes potassium

stages.event: Worsening dehydration and acidosis
stages.nurse: He's breathing even harder and his pressure's drifting down — what do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Isotonic fluid resuscitation is the first step in DKA; delaying it worsens the dehydration and acidosis.

stages.event: Persistent severe acidosis
stages.nurse: He's getting more lethargic and the gap hasn't closed at all. I need a plan.
stages.feedback: Fluids plus a well-sequenced insulin infusion (after checking potassium) are required to close the anion gap.

debrief.goodOutcome: New-onset DKA managed in the correct order and admitted.
debrief.outcomeGood: You confirmed DKA, resuscitated with fluids, checked potassium before insulin, ran an insulin infusion without a bolus, added dextrose as glucose fell, and admitted to the ICU — textbook sequencing.
debrief.outcomePoor: Key steps were out of order or delayed, risking hypokalemic arrhythmia or cerebral edema.
debrief.badOutcome: DKA was managed with dangerous sequencing (insulin before potassium, or delayed fluids).
learningPoints: Confirm DKA with glucose, a venous gas/anion gap, and ketones.
Fluids first; check potassium BEFORE insulin and hold insulin if K < 3.3.
Insulin as an infusion with no IV bolus — especially in young patients — and add dextrose as glucose approaches ~250.
Monitor closely for cerebral edema in adolescents: new headache, altered mental status, bradycardia/hypertension are ominous.
missedOpportunities: Checking potassium before starting insulin
Adding dextrose to the fluids as the glucose fell
Identifying the precipitant (new-onset diabetes via A1c)

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: High-speed collision with a tree on a black-diamond run. Ski patrol found him down and unresponsive for about a minute before he came around.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: I saw the whole thing — he hit that tree going full speed.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's healthy — no medical problems that I know of, never been hospitalized.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: No medications, no allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ski patrol said he was out about a minute, came around to a GCS of 14. Over the last twenty minutes he's trended down to about 11.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He had a sandwich at the lodge maybe two hours ago. He'll have a beer at lunch sometimes but I didn't see him drink anything today.

physicalExam.finding: Restless and intermittently agitated, GCS estimated at 11 (down from 14 on EMS arrival), eyes tracking a beat slower than before; tachypneic and working to breathe upright.

Flattened, retruded 'dish-face' midface contour. Bilateral periorbital ecchymosis with lid edema so severe the eyes are nearly swollen shut. Brisk epistaxis with blood and secretions draining posteriorly.

Mouth opening approximately 1.5 cm (severe trismus) precluding Mallampati assessment; short, thick neck with limited extension; reduced thyromental distance (~2 fingerbreadths); active blood and secretions pooling in the oropharynx; midface grossly mobile and free-floating on gentle palpation.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: I can barely get him to open his mouth — maybe a finger's width — and I'm not liking how loose that midface feels.

physicalExam.finding: Rigid cervical collar in place per trauma protocol; short, thick neck circumference limiting palpation and visualization of landmarks; no crepitus or bony step-off appreciated through the collar.
Chest wall symmetric without flail segment or crepitus; breath sounds equal bilaterally but shallow; audible gurgling when reclined, clearer when upright; no wheeze.
Bilateral periorbital ecchymosis and lid edema nearly occluding the palpebral fissures; pupils equal and sluggishly reactive when visualized; extraocular movements not assessable due to swelling.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Blood gas back — he's not oxygenating well even on the non-rebreather.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Maxillofacial, Non-Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Comminuted fractures of the maxillary sinuses, nasal bones, and bilateral zygomaticomaxillary complexes with pterygoid plate involvement and separation of the midface from the skull base. Bilateral orbital floor fractures noted. Blood and secretions layer within the nasal cavity and nasopharynx. IMPRESSION: Comminuted panfacial fracture pattern with a mobile, unstable midface segment.

image.query: CT face maxillofacial fracture midface trauma

diagnosticReports.title: CT Cervical Spine, Non-Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: No acute fracture, subluxation, or malalignment of the cervical spine. Prevertebral soft tissues are within normal limits. Visualized lung apices are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: No cervical spine injury identified.

image.query: CT cervical spine trauma
portable trauma chest x-ray

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head, Non-Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: No acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, or midline shift. Ventricles and sulci are unremarkable for age. No skull fracture identified on this series. IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial abnormality; the patient's declining mental status is not explained by intracranial pathology on this study.

image.query: CT head non-contrast trauma

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Difficult airway cart's here — video laryngoscope, LMA, and the cric kit are all open and ready to go.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Anesthesia attending here — I'll take the first look with you once everything's ready.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Keeping him ramped and upright with high-flow nasal cannula running for apneic oxygenation — sats are climbing with him sitting up.

any: lower the head of the bed

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's desatting fast flat — 68%!

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Sit him back up now — he can't tolerate supine, we already know that.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Stop — hold off on anything nasal. With a midface fracture like this it could pass intracranially. Oral adjuncts only.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: I'm getting a big leak around the mask with his face like this — sats aren't holding even bagging him.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: RSI induction/paralytic dose not specified — e.g., ketamine 1.5 mg/kg IV + rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: Not yet — I want eyes on this airway and our backup already open before anyone pushes a drug.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ketamine and rocuronium in — video laryngoscope up, but it's a grade 3 view, blood and swelling obscuring the cords. Can't pass the tube.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: We haven't even attempted a look yet — let's not jump straight to a rescue device.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: LMA's in, good capnography waveform — sats climbing to the mid-90s.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: We're not at a scalpel yet — we haven't even attempted to intubate him.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Cricothyrotomy performed — surgical airway secured, good waveform, sats climbing.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: OMFS here — once the airway's secure we'll plan definitive repair. This isn't emergent surgery tonight, just needs a secured airway first.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: We can't move him anywhere until this airway's secure.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Airway's secure and he's stable for transfer — trauma ICU bed's ready, OMFS following for definitive repair.

deterioration.trajectory: anatomically and physiologically difficult airway with positional desaturation — deteriorates rapidly if treated as a routine intubation instead of an anticipated difficult airway

stages.event: Recurrent desaturation with recumbency and worsening agitation; GCS continues to drift down.
stages.nurse: He's satting worse every time we can't keep him upright and he's getting harder to redirect. What's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Positional desaturation is itself a physiologic difficult-airway predictor — without a deliberate airway assessment and optimized upright positioning/apneic oxygenation, the safety margin before any attempt keeps shrinking.

stages.event: Escalating hypoxia and oropharyngeal bleeding with no backup airway plan in place.
stages.nurse: He just went quiet on me and his jaw's still locked tight. I need direction here, now.
stages.feedback: Moving toward a first attempt without backup airway equipment and the most experienced operator already at the bedside is exactly the setup NAP4 identifies as the precursor to an unrecognized cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate event.

stages.event: Cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate progresses to hypoxic cardiac arrest.
stages.nurse: I've got no chest rise and the monitor just flatlined — I need hands on him right now!
stages.feedback: Without a rescue supraglottic airway or timely front-of-neck access once oxygenation could not be achieved, this progressed to hypoxic cardiac arrest — the outcome closed-claims data attribute to delayed escalation, not the difficult airway itself.

debrief.goodOutcome: Difficult airway anticipated and prepared for; airway secured safely via a structured escalation algorithm.
debrief.outcomeGood: You assessed for a difficult airway before pushing drugs, opened backup equipment and called the most experienced operator, optimized positioning and preoxygenation, and when the first attempt failed you bridged with a supraglottic airway (escalating to a surgical airway when needed) — Dean's airway was secured without a hypoxic arrest.
debrief.outcomePoor: Induction was pushed without a structured airway assessment or backup equipment in the room, the patient was forced supine, and a failed first attempt spiraled into an unrecognized cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate event with no rescue plan ready.
debrief.badOutcome: A predictable difficult airway was treated as a routine intubation; the failed first attempt was not bridged in time, and hypoxia progressed to cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: Difficult airway predictors (trismus, midface instability, short thick neck, blood/secretions in the airway) must be anticipated, not discovered mid-attempt
Closed-claims data show the most common preventable failures are failure to plan for a difficult airway, perseveration on a failed technique, and delayed calling for a surgical airway
NAP4 found most airway deaths and brain injuries occurred in seemingly low-risk patients — always prepare for difficulty regardless of how healthy the patient looks
A supraglottic airway should be used as a bridge/rescue device after a failed attempt, not skipped in favor of repeated laryngoscopy
Difficult airway management follows an algorithm — intubation, supraglottic airway, fiberoptic/awake intubation, and front-of-neck access — with equipment standardized and ready before induction
missedOpportunities: Systematic difficult-airway assessment before pushing induction drugs
Backup airway equipment (video laryngoscope, supraglottic airway, surgical kit) opened at the bedside before the first attempt
Calling the most experienced airway operator before attempting intubation
Optimized upright positioning and apneic preoxygenation instead of forcing the patient supine
Bridging a failed first attempt with a supraglottic airway instead of repeating the same technique
Immediate escalation to front-of-neck surgical airway once CICO developed

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had burning with urination and left-sided back pain for two days, then today she spiked a fever, got the shakes, and started saying confused things.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's diabetic and takes a pill for it. Otherwise pretty independent until today.

physicalExam.finding: Toxic-appearing, warm and flushed, confused (not oriented to place). Tachypneic, mottled knees.
Soft abdomen; marked left costovertebral-angle tenderness. No peritoneal signs.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Lactate is back at 4.6.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Two sets of blood cultures and a urine culture drawn and sent before any antibiotics.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Chem's back — she's in acute kidney injury, creatinine 2.3.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Broad-spectrum antibiotics hanging now — within the hour.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pushing 30 mL/kg of balanced crystalloid through two large-bore IVs — pressure's coming up a little.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Norepinephrine started and titrating — MAP is up to 68 now.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Norepinephrine ordered without a starting rate — specify (e.g., 0.05 mcg/kg/min) and titrate to MAP ≥ 65.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
diagnosticReports.body: Left hydronephrosis and hydroureter to the level of a 7 mm obstructing calculus at the distal ureter, with perinephric stranding. Right kidney normal. No free air or abscess. IMPRESSION: Obstructing left distal ureteral stone with hydronephrosis and features of pyelonephritis.

image.query: obstructing ureteral stone hydronephrosis CT abdomen pelvis

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Copy — you're calling this septic shock and hunting for the source.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Urology — an obstructed, infected system won't clear on antibiotics alone. We'll take her for emergent decompression, a stent or nephrostomy tonight.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU is expecting her — pressors and antibiotics continue in transit.

deterioration.trajectory: septic shock from an obstructed, infected kidney — worsens fast without timely cultures→antibiotics→fluids→pressors and source control

stages.event: Progressive septic shock
stages.nurse: Her pressure just dropped to 72 systolic and she's barely responding — I need a plan right now.
stages.feedback: Early antibiotics and aggressive fluid resuscitation are time-critical in septic shock.

stages.event: Refractory shock and cardiovascular collapse
stages.nurse: She's lost her pulse — she's arrested.
stages.feedback: Untreated septic shock progresses to cardiovascular collapse; the window for antibiotics, fluids, and pressors was missed.

debrief.goodOutcome: Urosepsis recognized and resuscitated; obstruction sent for decompression.
debrief.outcomeGood: You drew cultures and lactate, gave early broad-spectrum antibiotics, resuscitated with fluids and pressors, found the obstructing stone on imaging, and arranged urologic decompression and ICU care — complete source control.
debrief.outcomePoor: Resuscitation or source control lagged, and the shock progressed.
debrief.badOutcome: The septic shock and its obstructive source were not addressed in time.
learningPoints: In septic shock, draw lactate and cultures, then give broad-spectrum antibiotics within the hour.
Resuscitate with 30 mL/kg crystalloid and start norepinephrine for MAP < 65 despite fluids.
An obstructed, infected urinary system needs urgent decompression — antibiotics alone will not control the source.
Recognize septic shock early: hypotension plus an elevated lactate plus a source of infection.
missedOpportunities: Antibiotics within the first hour
30 mL/kg crystalloid and early vasopressor support
Imaging to find the obstructing stone and urologic decompression

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's been about four days of feeling short of breath and my legs swelling up, and today when I stood up I got so dizzy I almost fell.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I have pulmonary hypertension — high pressure in my lung arteries. I've had it about three years. I take a pill for it and a small water pill.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She takes tadalafil for her lung pressure and a low-dose diuretic. About a week and a half ago her insurance dropped coverage on the tadalafil and she hasn't been able to get it refilled since.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I ran out of my lung pill about eight days ago... I didn't think it would matter this fast.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: No family history of heart problems that we know of. She's never smoked, doesn't drink. She's been living with me since her diagnosis got worse.

physicalExam.finding: Anxious and uncomfortable, sitting bolt upright on the stretcher. Pale, cool, and diaphoretic. Alert but visibly frightened.
Jugular venous distension to the angle of the jaw. Loud, single-sounding second heart sound, no murmur or rub appreciated. Narrow pulse pressure, peripheral pulses thready.
Clear to auscultation bilaterally — no crackles, wheeze, or rales despite the degree of swelling. Tachypneic with mild accessory muscle use.
Mildly distended with tender hepatomegaly, liver edge palpable about 4 cm below the costal margin. Positive hepatojugular reflex. No rebound or guarding.
2+ pitting edema to the knees bilaterally. Distal extremities cool with delayed capillary refill (~4 s). Skin mottled at the ankles.

image.query: sinus tachycardia right axis deviation incomplete right bundle branch block deep S wave lead I Q wave and T wave inversion lead III T wave inversions V1 to V3 12-lead ECG
portable chest x-ray clear lung fields enlarged central pulmonary arteries prominent right heart border no infiltrate no effusion

diagnosticReports.title: Bedside Echocardiogram (Focused)
diagnosticReports.body: The right ventricle is markedly dilated and hypokinetic, larger than the left ventricle, with flattening of the interventricular septum producing a D-shaped left ventricle in short axis; severe tricuspid regurgitation and a dilated IVC without respiratory collapse are noted. IMPRESSION: Severe RV dilation/dysfunction with septal flattening and severe TR, consistent with acute-on-chronic right heart failure.

image.query: dilated hypokinetic right ventricle right ventricle larger than left ventricle septal flattening D-sign severe tricuspid regurgitation focused cardiac ultrasound

diagnosticReports.title: CT Pulmonary Angiogram
diagnosticReports.body: No filling defect identified within the main, lobar, or segmental pulmonary arteries. Main pulmonary artery is dilated at 34 mm. Right ventricle is dilated with reflux of contrast into the inferior vena cava and hepatic veins. IMPRESSION: No pulmonary embolism. Findings reflect right heart strain and elevated right-sided pressures rather than acute thromboembolic disease.

image.query: CT pulmonary angiogram dilated main pulmonary artery dilated right ventricle contrast reflux hepatic veins no filling defect

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — treating this as preload-dependent right heart failure, not undifferentiated shock. I'll hold off on a big fluid bolus.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Got it — no nitrates or vasodilators. I'll flag it given how dependent she is on her pressure right now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — norepinephrine over phenylephrine, and we'll avoid intubating unless she truly needs it given the PEEP effect on her right heart.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — you're calling the creatinine rise cardiorenal congestion, not prerenal failure. I won't push fluids for it.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fluid bolus is running wide open.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Stop that — she's preload-dependent right heart failure, not volume down. That bolus is going to overdistend her right ventricle and push the septum into the left side.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Nitro's on board.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — her pressure just cratered. She's hypotensive and preload-dependent; nitrates are dropping her coronary perfusion pressure too.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Phenylephrine's running.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: That's a pure alpha-agonist — it'll raise her pulmonary vascular resistance and push right ventricular afterload even higher. Wrong pressor for a failing right ventricle.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Norepinephrine started — titrating to her pressure now.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Norepinephrine dose/route not specified — start 0.05 mcg/kg/min IV and titrate to MAP >=65.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: Hold off on diuresis until her pressure's supported — pulling volume off a hypotensive, low-output right ventricle right now will drop her further.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Furosemide given now that her pressure's supported — she's starting to put out more urine.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Furosemide dose/route not specified — 40 mg IV, reassess urine output.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Intubating now.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Careful — positive pressure and high PEEP raise right ventricular afterload and drop preload. This could tip her into arrest. Only if she truly can't protect her airway.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Pulmonary hypertension and cardiology here — this fits a lapse in her vasodilator therapy precipitating acute-on-chronic right heart failure. We'll take her to the ICU, support her hemodynamics, and get her back on therapy once she's stable.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed's ready, pressors running, pulmonary hypertension and cardiology are following her up there.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity acute-on-chronic right heart failure — unsupported preload-dependent hypotension worsens to refractory shock and arrest without vasopressor support and specialist involvement

stages.event: Progressive right ventricular decompensation
stages.nurse: Doctor, her pressure keeps sliding and she's getting harder to rouse — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: Unsupported right ventricular failure will not correct itself; she needs hemodynamic support targeted at her physiology, not empiric shock management.

stages.event: Ongoing congestion without definitive source control
stages.nurse: Doctor, her belly's more distended and she's making even less urine — are we bringing anyone else in on this?
stages.feedback: Without identifying and addressing the precipitant and involving pulmonary hypertension/cardiology, her congestion and renal perfusion keep worsening.

stages.event: Refractory right heart failure, cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: She's lost her pulse — starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Unsupported, preload-dependent right ventricular failure progressed to cardiovascular collapse and arrest.

debrief.goodOutcome: Acute-on-chronic right heart failure recognized and treated with cautious volume management, vasopressor support, and urgent pulmonary hypertension/cardiology involvement; hemodynamics improve.
debrief.badOutcome: Empiric fluid boluses, nitrates, or phenylephrine given for presumed undifferentiated shock or 'CHF' overdistend the failing right ventricle, and she progresses to cardiovascular collapse.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized preload-dependent right heart failure, avoided large fluid boluses, nitrates, and phenylephrine, confirmed the diagnosis with ECG and echocardiography, supported her pressure with norepinephrine, identified the medication lapse driving this, and got pulmonary hypertension and cardiology involved urgently — her pressure, heart rate, and mentation improved.
debrief.outcomePoor: The preload-dependent physiology went unrecognized, fluids and/or nitrates were given for presumed shock or CHF, and she progressed to refractory right heart failure and arrest.
learningPoints: Right heart failure produces the same congestive signs as left heart failure (JVD, edema, hepatomegaly) but clear lungs and a preload-dependent, afterload-sensitive physiology change the entire treatment calculus

Because the right and left ventricles share the interventricular septum and pericardium (ventricular interdependence), large fluid boluses that overdistend the right ventricle can bow the septum into the left ventricle and paradoxically lower cardiac output
Chronic pulmonary vasodilator therapy is disease-modifying in pulmonary arterial hypertension, and abrupt discontinuation can rapidly precipitate acute-on-chronic right heart failure
Phenylephrine and high-PEEP positive pressure ventilation both raise right ventricular afterload and should be avoided when possible in right heart failure

Rising creatinine in right heart failure often reflects cardiorenal syndrome from venous congestion rather than prerenal hypovolemia, so treatment should target congestion rather than reflexive fluid administration
missedOpportunities: Recognizing preload-dependent right heart failure physiology and avoiding empiric large-volume fluid boluses
Avoiding nitroglycerin and other vasodilators
Obtaining echocardiography to confirm right ventricular size and function
Obtaining a 12-lead ECG to evaluate for right ventricular strain
Avoiding phenylephrine and unnecessary high-PEEP intubation
Identifying the medication lapse, involving pulmonary hypertension/cardiology urgently, and recognizing the rising creatinine as cardiorenal congestion

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: It's been three days of worse breathing and swelling, and the last two days he's barely wanted to eat or drink. This morning he stopped making sense.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm... just so tired... can't catch my breath... my legs feel so heavy...

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He has heart failure — his heart doctor said his pump is weak, only pumping about 20 percent, ever since his big heart attack a few years back.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He takes carvedilol, lisinopril, a water pill — furosemide — and spironolactone. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He quit smoking about fifteen years ago, maybe a pack a day before that. He doesn't drink. He's lived with me since his heart got bad.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's barely eaten or drunk anything the last two days, and I don't think he's urinated much at all since yesterday.

physicalExam.finding: Lethargic, cool and diaphoretic, mottled skin. Breathing fast and shallow with accessory muscle use. Non-toxic but clearly unwell.
Jugular venous pressure markedly elevated to the angle of the jaw. S3 gallop present. Narrow pulse pressure. Peripheral pulses thready. No murmur.
Bibasilar crackles two-thirds up both lung fields, with a faint expiratory wheeze. Tachypneic with accessory muscle use.
Cool, mottled, and diaphoretic extremities with delayed capillary refill (~4-5 s). 2+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally.
Lethargic and slow to respond, oriented to person only. Arousable to voice but drifts back to sleep. No focal deficits.
Mildly distended, tender hepatomegaly with a positive hepatojugular reflex. No rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds present.

image.query: 12-lead ECG old anterior Q waves nonspecific ST-T changes no acute ST elevation
portable chest x-ray bilateral pulmonary edema cardiomegaly

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Dobutamine infusion started — I'll watch his pressure and mentation closely.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Dobutamine/milrinone dose not specified — start dobutamine 2.5 mcg/kg/min (or milrinone load plus infusion) and titrate to perfusion.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: Hold off on diuresis for now — pulling more volume off him with pressures this low and no cardiac output support will drop him further.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Furosemide given now that he's better perfused — starting to put out more urine.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — he's hypotensive and preload-dependent right now. Nitrates will drop his pressure further, not help him.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Don't push morphine here — it'll drop his pressure and blunt his drive to breathe when he's already hypoperfused.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — you're treating this as cold, low-output cardiogenic shock, not a warm-and-wet flash pulmonary edema. I'll hold off on the usual nitro and diuretic bundle.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Cardiology here — agree with inotropic support and holding preload reduction. We'll follow him into the ICU and reassess his pump once he's stabilized.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed is ready with an arterial line and continuous telemetry — cardiology's aware and following him up there.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity cold-and-wet cardiogenic shock — hypoperfusion worsens without inotropic support, progressing to cardiac arrest

stages.event: Progressive hypoperfusion, worsening mentation
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's barely rousable now and his pressure keeps sliding — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: Without augmenting cardiac output, hypoperfusion keeps worsening and end-organ function will keep declining.

stages.event: Refractory cardiogenic shock, cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: He's lost his pulse — starting compressions now!
stages.feedback: Prolonged hypoperfusion without inotropic support in cold, low-output shock progressed to cardiac arrest.

debrief.goodOutcome: Cold-and-wet cardiogenic shock recognized and treated with inotropic support; hemodynamics stabilize under ICU-level care.
debrief.badOutcome: Reflexive vasodilator/diuretic therapy for presumed flash pulmonary edema worsens hypoperfusion, and the patient arrests.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the cold-and-wet, low-output phenotype, avoided nitrates, morphine, and premature diuresis, started inotropic support, obtained an ECG and chest x-ray, sent BNP, troponin, lactate, and renal function, and arranged ICU admission with cardiology following — mentation and perfusion improve.
debrief.outcomePoor: The low-output phenotype went unrecognized or was treated with the reflexive nitrate/morphine/diuretic bundle meant for flash pulmonary edema, and hypoperfusion progressed to cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: ADHF is a heterogeneous syndrome, not a single 'wet CHF' cookbook diagnosis — a one-size-fits-all approach can harm patients whose physiology differs from the classic hypertensive flash pulmonary edema case
A low-output, cold-and-wet or cold-and-dry patient can present with a deceptively normal heart rate if on a home beta-blocker, masking the severity of hypoperfusion
Nitrates, morphine, and aggressive diuresis are appropriate for warm-and-wet ADHF but can worsen hypotension and organ hypoperfusion in cold, low-output patients who instead need inotropic support
Worsening renal function in ADHF (cardiorenal syndrome) reflects poor renal perfusion from low cardiac output, not simply volume status, and should prompt reassessment of the hemodynamic profile
Because ADHF can be precipitated by silent or atypical myocardial infarction, a 12-lead ECG should be obtained early even when heart failure seems like the obvious primary diagnosis
missedOpportunities: Recognizing the cold-and-wet, low-output phenotype before treating
Early 12-lead ECG to exclude an acute coronary precipitant

deterioration.trajectory: type A dissection can extend to tamponade, stroke, or acute AR; uncontrolled rate/pressure or delay to OR is fatal

stages.event: Propagation of the dissection
stages.nurse: His pain is worse and the pressure's climbing again — heart rate's up too.
stages.feedback: Impulse control — lowering heart rate and shear with beta-blockade first — is the priority; uncontrolled rate propagates the dissection.

stages.event: Pericardial tamponade
stages.nurse: He's suddenly hypotensive with distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds — he's arresting.
stages.feedback: A proximal dissection ruptured into the pericardium causing tamponade; timely rate/pressure control and definitive surgery were the only path.

debrief.goodOutcome: Type A dissection recognized, medically controlled, and taken to the OR.
debrief.outcomeGood: You detected the pulse differential, controlled heart rate with beta-blockade before any vasodilator, treated pain, typed and crossed, confirmed with CT angiography, and got cardiothoracic surgery emergently — while correctly avoiding thrombolytics.
debrief.outcomePoor: Rate/pressure control or definitive surgery was delayed, or the dissection was mistreated as ACS.
debrief.badOutcome: The dissection propagated to tamponade before rate control and surgery were achieved.
learningPoints: Check blood pressure in both arms — a pulse/pressure differential is a key clue to dissection.
Control heart rate first with IV beta-blockade (target HR < 60) BEFORE any vasodilator to avoid reflex tachycardia and shear.
Confirm with CT angiography and get emergent cardiothoracic surgery for a type A dissection.
Do NOT give thrombolytics or anticoagulation — dissection can masquerade as STEMI/ACS.
missedOpportunities: Beta-blockade for rate control before any vasodilator
Emergent cardiothoracic surgery consultation
Avoiding thrombolytics/anticoagulation

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity — unstable bradycardia with failing escape rhythm; without pacing or chronotropic support, perfusion collapses toward arrest

stages.event: Worsening hypoperfusion, escape rhythm slowing
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's barely responding to me now and I can't get a pressure over 70 — I need you to do something.
stages.feedback: The escape rhythm is fading and perfusion is failing. Definitive rate support has not been established.

stages.event: Escape rhythm failing, near-arrest
stages.nurse: He's gone unresponsive and I can barely feel a pulse — I need help at the bedside right now!
stages.feedback: He is peri-arrest from an unsupported rate. Every minute without capture or a chronotrope worsens survival.

stages.event: Bradyasystolic arrest
stages.nurse: He's lost his pulse — he's arrested. Starting compressions!
stages.feedback: The unsupported bradycardia deteriorated into cardiac arrest. Early pacing and chronotropic support would have restored perfusion.

debrief.goodOutcome: Unstable complete heart block recognized and rate support established; perfusion restored and cardiology secured for transvenous pacing.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the AV dissociation, applied pads, attempted atropine, sedated and started transcutaneous pacing, added a chronotropic infusion as needed, worked up reversible causes, and got cardiology on board for a transvenous wire — perfusion restored.
debrief.outcomePoor: Rate support was never established, so the failing escape rhythm collapsed and the patient deteriorated into arrest.
debrief.badOutcome: Relying on atropine and delaying pacing, the unsupported bradycardia progressed to bradyasystolic arrest.
learningPoints: Atropine is often ineffective in high-grade/infranodal AV block — move quickly to pacing.
Transcutaneous pacing is a bridge; secure transvenous pacing and cardiology involvement.
Look for reversible causes: inferior MI, hyperkalemia, and AV-nodal blocking drugs.
Sedate and provide analgesia before transcutaneous pacing — it is painful.
missedOpportunities: Applying pacer pads and moving to transcutaneous pacing early

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity inferior STEMI with RV involvement — preload-dependent hypotension and progressive AV block worsen to cardiogenic shock and arrest without fluids and timely reperfusion

stages.event: Worsening preload-dependent hypotension
stages.nurse: Doctor, his pressure's sagging into the 80s and he still looks gray. What do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Preload-dependent RV infarct hypotension needs prompt IV fluid boluses — it will not correct on its own.

stages.event: Progressive conduction disease — high-grade AV block
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's dropped into complete heart block and his pressure's falling further — I need orders now!
stages.feedback: Ongoing untreated inferior ischemia progressed to complete heart block; the artery needs to be opened.

stages.event: Cardiogenic shock arrest
stages.nurse: He's lost his pulse — starting compressions!
stages.feedback: The infarct-related artery was never reperfused; complete heart block and cardiogenic shock progressed to arrest.

debrief.goodOutcome: Inferior STEMI with RV involvement recognized and reperfused; hypotension corrected with fluids, nitrates avoided.
debrief.badOutcome: Nitroglycerin dropped his preload-dependent pressure and reperfusion was delayed, progressing to heart block and cardiogenic shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the inferior STEMI, confirmed RV involvement with right-sided leads, gave aspirin and anticoagulation, supported his pressure with fluids instead of nitrates, and got him to the cath lab for timely primary PCI.
debrief.outcomePoor: The RV involvement went unrecognized, nitroglycerin was given for his chest pain and dropped his pressure, and reperfusion was delayed — he progressed to complete heart block and cardiogenic shock.
learningPoints: Inferior STEMI — get right-sided leads; RV involvement makes the patient preload-dependent.
Avoid nitroglycerin in RV infarction — it can cause profound hypotension; treat with fluids.
Primary PCI within the door-to-balloon window is the goal.
Watch for bradycardia and AV block with inferior MI.
missedOpportunities: Obtaining right-sided leads (V4R) to detect RV involvement
Treating hypotension with IV fluid boluses instead of nitrates
Activating the cath lab promptly for primary PCI
Anticoagulating and giving antiplatelet therapy per ACS protocol

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity obstructive shock — progressive RV failure to PEA arrest unless the obstruction is relieved and the RV supported

stages.event: Refractory hypotension — RV output falling
stages.nurse: Doctor — her pressure is down in the 70s and the sat won't come up. I need orders, what are we doing for her?
stages.feedback: Persistent hypotension in obstructive shock needs early vasopressor support and cautious volume — large boluses only worsen the failing right ventricle.

stages.event: Peri-arrest — worsening RV failure and shock
stages.nurse: She is mottling up past her knees and I can barely feel a pulse. This is slipping away — I need something now.
stages.feedback: Support alone is not enough once shock is this deep — the mechanical obstruction has to be relieved with thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy.

stages.event: PEA arrest
stages.nurse: I've lost her pulse — she's arrested! Starting compressions!
stages.feedback: The obstruction was never relieved; the overloaded right ventricle gave out and the patient arrested (PEA).

debrief.goodOutcome: High-risk PE recognized and lysed; obstructive shock reversed and RV supported.
debrief.badOutcome: Obstructive shock progressed to PEA arrest because the clot burden was never relieved and the RV was overloaded with fluid.
debrief.outcomeGood: You read the obstructive shock physiology, supported oxygenation and the pressure with high-flow oxygen and early norepinephrine, confirmed RV strain at the bedside, anticoagulated, and gave definitive thrombolysis for the unstable patient — she stabilized and went to the ICU.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter leaned on large-volume fluids and delayed definitive therapy; the failing right ventricle was overloaded and the patient deteriorated into arrest before the obstruction was relieved.
learningPoints: High-risk (massive) PE = PE with hypotension; treat with thrombolysis unless contraindicated.
The failing RV is preload-sensitive but intolerant of volume overload — modest fluids, early norepinephrine.
Bedside echo (RV dilation/strain) can guide therapy when the patient is too unstable for CTA.
Peri-intubation hypotension can precipitate arrest — resuscitate before you intubate.

Systemic thrombolysis for the hemodynamically unstable patient
