debrief.outcomePoor: Transcutaneous pacing was never established, so the failing paced rhythm collapsed and she arrested.
debrief.badOutcome: Trusting the implanted device and delaying pacing, the unsupported failure to capture progressed to cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: Failure to capture means the pacing artifact is present but not followed by a QRS — the device is firing but not depolarizing the myocardium.
A prior ECG showing a normally captured paced rhythm is the key comparison for identifying a new loss of capture.

An implanted pacemaker does not guarantee protection from life-threatening bradycardia — a malfunctioning device still needs standard unstable-bradycardia rescue with pacer pads and transcutaneous pacing.
Sedate and provide analgesia before transcutaneous pacing — it is painful.
Hyperkalemia can raise the capture threshold and distort paced-complex morphology, so check electrolytes in any patient with a pacing problem.
Causes of failure to capture include lead dislodgement or fracture, rising capture threshold (exit block), and battery depletion — all require device interrogation, not just resuscitation.
missedOpportunities: Applying pacer pads and starting transcutaneous pacing early
Sedating her before transcutaneous pacing
Comparing to a prior ECG to confirm this is a new loss of capture
Checking a basic metabolic panel for potassium and reviewing her medication list
Consulting cardiology/EP emergently for device interrogation

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Witnessed collapse while moving furniture — bystanders started CPR right away. We found him in PEA, intubated in the field, and he converted to V-fib once we got rolling. Down about fifty minutes total before he got a pulse back.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He grabbed his chest, coughed hard, threw up once, and then just went down. It happened so fast.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: No, he's never had heart problems that we know of. No diabetes, no high blood pressure that he's ever mentioned. He barely goes to the doctor.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He doesn't take anything regularly — maybe an aspirin once in a while. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: His father had a heart attack in his sixties. Ian smokes about half a pack a day and has a beer or two on weekends. He works construction — today he was just helping a neighbor move a couch.

physicalExam.finding: Intubated, motionless on the gurney. Skin cool and clammy with mild mottling over the knees; capillary refill delayed at roughly 4 seconds.

No eye opening, no verbal response possible (intubated), no purposeful movement to voice or a sternal rub — only slow, non-purposeful limb withdrawal to noxious stimulus. Pupils 4mm and sluggishly reactive bilaterally.
Tachycardic, regular rhythm on auscultation; heart sounds somewhat distant, no murmur or rub appreciated. Peripheral pulses weak and thready, central pulses easier to palpate.
Bilateral breath sounds equal over vent-generated breaths, no crackles or wheeze appreciated. ET tube secured at 22 cm at the teeth with good chest rise.
No overt shivering or myoclonic jerking noted at this moment, but he is not yet on active cooling — this will need frequent reassessment once cooling and sedation are underway.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: 12-lead's up — there's ST elevation in the inferior leads with reciprocal changes anteriorly.

image.query: post-ROSC 12-lead ECG inferior ST elevation reciprocal anterior ST depression acute injury pattern
portable AP chest ET tube tip above carina no pneumothorax no pulmonary edema

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: No acute intracranial hemorrhage or mass effect identified. No large territory hypodensity. Gray-white differentiation preserved. Ventricles and sulci normal in size. IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial abnormality; no radiographic contraindication to targeted temperature management.

image.query: noncontrast head CT post cardiac arrest no hemorrhage no mass effect

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Cooling device applied — target 32 to 33.9 degrees Celsius, temperature probe in and trending down.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Stop — below 30 degrees the adverse effects climb sharply with no added benefit. Keep him in the 32 to 33.9 range, not colder.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Arterial line placed in the right radial — continuous pressure tracing up on the monitor.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Holding the paralytic briefly for a quick neuro check — no seizure activity, no myoclonus, pupils unchanged, and he's still got a pulse. Resuming now.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Sudden collapse with chest tightness right before, now inferior ST changes on the 12-lead — that points to an acute coronary occlusion as the trigger, not a primary respiratory or metabolic cause.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fentanyl and propofol infusions running — he's comfortable and not fighting the cooling.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Sedation/analgesia dose not specified — e.g., fentanyl infusion plus propofol or midazolam titrated to comfort.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: Not yet — paralyzing him before he's adequately sedated would mask pain, awareness, and any seizure activity. Sedate him first.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Cisatracurium infusion started for refractory shivering — sedation's deep enough first, train-of-four monitoring is on.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Paralytic dose/infusion not specified — e.g., cisatracurium infusion titrated to train-of-four, only after adequate sedation.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: One liter of normal saline running in for the pressure.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Norepinephrine started, titrating to a MAP goal — pressure's coming up already.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Vasopressor dose not specified — e.g., norepinephrine infusion starting at 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP >=65.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: Get me the 12-lead first — I can't activate the cath lab without seeing what his rhythm and ST segments look like.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Interventional cardiology — inferior ST elevation, that's a culprit lesion. We'll take him up to the cath lab once he's stable enough to move.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neurology — far too early to prognosticate accurately under sedation and active cooling. Continuous EEG is reasonable if you're worried about seizures; we'll reassess formally after rewarming.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed's ready — cooling protocol running, cardiology's aware, pressors titrating. Nice work, Doctor.

deterioration.trajectory: postcardiac arrest syndrome — untreated, the brain-injury cascade, myocardial stunning, and the unaddressed coronary occlusion compound into recurrent hemodynamic instability and a second arrest

stages.event: Uncontrolled hyperthermia, persistent coma
stages.nurse: Doctor, his temperature keeps climbing and he still hasn't moved on his own — I need a plan from you.
stages.feedback: Without prompt cooling toward the 32-33.9C target, his temperature keeps climbing, adding thermal injury on top of the ischemia-reperfusion cascade already running in his brain.

stages.event: Worsening hypoperfusion, myocardial stunning unsupported
stages.nurse: His pressure just dropped again and he's not stable — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: The precipitating coronary lesion was never pursued and his stunned myocardium was left unsupported, so his perfusion kept eroding.

stages.event: Recurrent cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive with no pulse — he's arrested again!
stages.feedback: Untreated shivering, unsupported hypotension, and an unaddressed coronary occlusion let the post-arrest cascade progress to a second cardiac arrest that could not be reversed.

debrief.goodOutcome: Coma confirmed after ROSC from a shockable rhythm, active cooling to 32-33.9C started promptly, the coronary occlusion identified and taken toward the cath lab, shivering controlled with layered sedation and paralysis without losing the ability to detect re-arrest or seizure, and blood pressure supported through the myocardial stunning period.
debrief.outcomeGood: You confirmed he remained comatose after a shockable-rhythm arrest, started active cooling toward 32-33.9C without delay, got a 12-lead that showed the culprit lesion and moved him toward the cath lab, layered sedation before paralysis to control shivering while still checking under it for re-arrest or seizure, and supported his pressure through the myocardial stunning period — he had a real chance at neurologically intact survival.
debrief.outcomePoor: The workup stalled: cooling was never started or was delayed, the coronary occlusion driving the arrest went uninvestigated, and shivering or hypotension were left unaddressed while the postcardiac arrest cascade progressed unchecked.
debrief.badOutcome: Cooling was delayed, the coronary lesion driving the arrest was never pursued, and unaddressed shivering plus unsupported hypotension let postcardiac arrest syndrome progress to a second, fatal arrest.
learningPoints: Cardiac arrest survivors face 'postcardiac arrest syndrome' — a combination of brain injury, myocardial dysfunction, systemic ischemia-reperfusion injury, and the persistent underlying disease that caused the arrest.
Moderate therapeutic hypothermia targets 32-33.9C; temperatures below 30C carry substantially more adverse effects without added benefit.
Ischemic brain injury cascades continue for hours to days after ROSC, so the window to intervene with cooling is time-sensitive, not something to defer.
Restoring a pulse is only half the job — the cause of the arrest, often an acute coronary occlusion, must still be actively found and reversed alongside post-arrest stabilization.
Sedate adequately before adding paralysis for shivering, and keep reassessing underneath it — deep sedation can hide a silent re-arrest, seizure, or myoclonus.
missedOpportunities: Confirming the patient remained comatose after ROSC from a shockable rhythm
Initiating active cooling promptly toward 32-33.9C
Obtaining a 12-lead ECG and pursuing urgent cardiology/cath evaluation for the coronary occlusion
Controlling shivering with adequate sedation and analgesia, adding paralysis only after sedation was on board
Periodically reassessing under sedation so a re-arrest, seizure, or myoclonus would not go unnoticed
Supporting blood pressure through the myocardial stunning period rather than attributing hypotension solely to sedation

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I was getting up from my chair and it hit me like a hammer in my back and side. Everything went spinny and I woke up on the floor.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I blacked out for a bit. The pain bores straight through to my back and down toward my groin. I feel sick and sweaty.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: High blood pressure and high cholesterol. A scan a couple years back showed a little bulge in my aorta they were watching.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I take a water pill and something for cholesterol, and a baby aspirin. No allergies.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've smoked a pack a day since I was seventeen. A couple beers on the weekend, nothing more.

physicalExam.finding: Pale, gray, diaphoretic with cool mottled skin. Ill-appearing and anxious, slow to answer, mildly confused. Clearly in distress.
Distended, diffusely tender. A pulsatile, expansile mass is palpable above the umbilicus with guarding. Bowel sounds sparse.
Tachycardic, regular. Thready radial pulses. Femoral pulses palpable but diminished, right weaker than left. Cool extremities, capillary refill 4-5 seconds.
Left flank tender to light palpation. Faint bluish discoloration developing over the left flank and lower abdomen. Deep percussion deferred given instability.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Type and cross sent stat, blood bank notified.

labResults.name: Blood type
labResults.value: O positive; 4 units PRBC being crossmatched, O-negative available immediately

labResults.name: Urinalysis
labResults.value: Trace blood, 3-5 RBC/hpf, no nitrites, negative leukocyte esterase

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Probe's on his belly, here's your view of the aorta.

diagnosticReports.title: Bedside Aortic Ultrasound
diagnosticReports.body: Infrarenal abdominal aorta measures 6.4 cm in maximal diameter with a heterogeneous hypoechoic stripe adjacent to the aortic wall suggesting periaortic/retroperitoneal fluid. IMPRESSION: Large infrarenal aortic aneurysm with findings concerning for rupture.

image.query: abdominal aorta long axis dilated aneurysm ultrasound
sinus tachycardia 12-lead ecg no st changes
portable supine chest x-ray no acute infiltrate

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Vascular here, do NOT put him in the scanner. He's too unstable to leave the department and the answer is already at the bedside. Every minute in radiology is a minute he's bleeding.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Two 16-gauge lines in, one in each AC. Ready to resuscitate.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Massive transfusion protocol activated, hanging O-negative now with plasma and platelets on the way.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Good, keep him at a systolic in the 80s while he's talking to us. Don't chase a normal pressure before we have control.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Stop flooding him with saline. Driving his pressure up will pop the clot and worsen the bleed, and you're diluting what little clotting he has left. He needs blood, not crystalloid.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Small dose of IV fentanyl given and titrated, watching his pressure closely.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Analgesia dose not specified — titrate small IV fentanyl (e.g., 25-50 mcg) given hypotension.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Vascular surgery, on my way down now. Keep two units running and don't over-resuscitate. Book the OR and the hybrid suite, we're taking him immediately.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood, you're treating this as a ruptured aneurysm, not a kidney stone. Mobilizing the team now.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: The OR won't accept him without a surgeon accepting the patient, and no one from vascular has been called yet. Who am I paging?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vascular has him, OR is open and blood is going up with us. Rolling to the operating room now.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity hemorrhagic shock — ongoing intra-abdominal bleeding leads to exsanguination unless blood products and emergent operative control are delivered

stages.event: Ongoing hemorrhage — pressure crashing
stages.nurse: His pressure just dropped into the 60s and he's getting drowsy on me. Nothing I hang is keeping up — what do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Ongoing intra-abdominal hemorrhage is outpacing the circulation. Blood products, not more crystalloid, are needed to keep up with the loss.

stages.event: Refractory shock — no source control
stages.nurse: He's barely responding and the pressure won't come up no matter what I run. I need a decision right now.
stages.feedback: Transfusion buys minutes, not a cure. Without a surgeon engaged to control the source, the shock stays refractory.

stages.event: Exsanguination and cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: I've lost his pulse — he's not breathing, starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Definitive repair in the operating room is the only thing that stops the bleeding. Delay past this point is fatal.

debrief.goodOutcome: Ruptured AAA recognized and resuscitated with blood while the patient was rushed to definitive operative repair.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on renal colic and flooding the patient with crystalloid instead of blood and surgery, the ruptured aneurysm bled out.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the ruptured aneurysm despite the renal-colic mimicry, placed large-bore access and crossmatched, confirmed with bedside ultrasound, transfused blood under permissive hypotension, and got vascular surgery to take him emergently to the OR.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter drifted toward renal colic or a CT scan while crystalloid was poured in — the clot disrupted, the patient exsanguinated, and definitive repair never happened.
learningPoints: Consider ruptured AAA in any older patient with abdominal, flank, or back pain and hypotension — it classically mimics renal colic, and microscopic hematuria is a trap.
Resuscitate with blood products under permissive hypotension (SBP ~70-90 while mentating); crystalloid flooding raises pressure, disrupts the clot, and dilutes clotting factors.
Bedside ultrasound confirms the aneurysm in seconds; an unstable patient goes to the OR, not the CT scanner.
Immediate vascular surgery involvement and emergent operative or endovascular repair are the only definitive treatment.
missedOpportunities: Naming ruptured AAA instead of anchoring on renal colic
Two large-bore IVs with type and crossmatch
Bedside ultrasound to confirm the aneurysm
Massive transfusion with permissive hypotension rather than crystalloid
Emergent vascular surgery and transfer to the OR

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've been wiped out and achy for three or four days, and today going up the stairs I just went out for a second. Then this pressure hit my chest and I couldn't catch my breath.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I had colon cancer — surgery about eight months ago, finished chemo around six months ago. I also had a blood clot in my right leg last year, but they treated it and said it was gone, so I stopped the blood thinner.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Nothing regular right now — I actually stopped my blood thinner a few months back once the clot cleared up on the scan. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No family history of clotting problems that I know of. I don't smoke. Honestly I've been a lot less active since the surgery and chemo — mostly resting at home.

physicalExam.finding: Pale, diaphoretic, and visibly anxious, sitting upright on the stretcher; alert and oriented, mild respiratory distress but speaking in full sentences.

Regular tachycardic rhythm, no murmurs or rubs; S2 split with an accentuated pulmonic component; jugular venous pressure mildly elevated at roughly 9-10 cm; peripheral pulses intact, extremities warm and well-perfused.
Tachypneic with mild accessory muscle use; lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezes, rales, or rhonchi; no focal dullness to percussion.
Bilateral lower extremities without edema, erythema, palpable cords, or calf tenderness; no residual swelling or warmth at the site of his prior right leg DVT.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ECG's up on the monitor for you.

image.query: ECG sinus tachycardia right axis deviation S1Q3T3 diffuse anterior precordial T wave inversions right heart strain pattern

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Portable chest film is up.

image.query: portable chest x-ray clear lung fields no infiltrate no effusion normal cardiac silhouette

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Bedside echo images are up on the screen.

diagnosticReports.title: Bedside Cardiac Ultrasound (Focused)
diagnosticReports.body: Right ventricle is dilated with free wall hypokinesis and apical sparing; interventricular septum is flattened producing a D-shaped left ventricle in short axis. IMPRESSION: RV strain pattern with McConnell sign, concerning for pulmonary embolism.

image.query: parasternal short axis apical four chamber dilated hypokinetic right ventricle McConnell sign flattened interventricular septum D-sign

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's back from CT — images are up.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Pulmonary Angiography
diagnosticReports.body: Bilateral filling defects within the segmental and subsegmental pulmonary arteries, most confluent in the right lower lobe branches. Right ventricle to left ventricle short-axis diameter ratio measures 1.4. Reflux of contrast into the inferior vena cava and hepatic veins is noted, with mild interventricular septal flattening. No pneumothorax, consolidation, or large pleural effusion. IMPRESSION: Extensive bilateral pulmonary arterial filling defects with CT findings of right ventricular strain (RV:LV ratio greater than 1).

image.query: CT pulmonary angiography bilateral filling defects segmental pulmonary arteries right ventricular strain

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Agreed — those diffuse precordial T-wave inversions and the S1Q3T3 look more like right heart strain than an anterior MI. His story fits a clot, not plaque rupture.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: He's normotensive but with a positive troponin, elevated BNP, and RV strain on echo — that's submassive, intermediate-high risk. Anticoagulate now and keep thrombolysis in reserve unless he decompensates.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Oxygen's up to 4 liters by nasal cannula, sat's climbing.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Heparin bolus given and the drip is running, weight-based.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Heparin dose/route not specified — weight-based IV bolus plus infusion (or enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h) for therapeutic anticoagulation.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold off on nitro — his right ventricle is preload-dependent right now, and dropping his preload could crash his pressure. His pressure just fell after that dose.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: Let's get him therapeutically anticoagulated first — he's still perfusing well. I don't want to add bleeding risk from thrombolysis until we've committed to anticoagulation and see how he trends.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Given how he's trending, alteplase is reasonable now — infusing, and we'll watch closely for bleeding.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Alteplase dose/route not specified — 100 mg IV over 2 hours (or per catheter-directed protocol) for hemodynamic deterioration.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Cardiology here — that ECG is a strain pattern, not an anterior STEMI. Agree with anticoagulating and reassessing serially; we don't need the cath lab.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: PERT here — intermediate-risk PE with RV strain. Anticoagulate now, and we'll keep thrombolysis and catheter-directed therapy on standby if he decompensates.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed's ready, continuous telemetry running, and report's called — he's stable for transfer upstairs.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity case — untreated right ventricular strain from clot burden progresses to obstructive shock and cardiac arrest without prompt anticoagulation

stages.event: Team continues an ACS-focused workup; anticoagulation for the underlying clot has not been started.
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's breathing faster and his pressure's drifting down — are we actually doing anything for him or just watching the monitor?
stages.feedback: Time spent pursuing an ischemic workup without addressing the underlying clot burden let right ventricular strain progress.

stages.event: Escalating right ventricular strain with falling perfusion pressure.
stages.nurse: He's cold and clammy now and his pressure just keeps dropping, doctor — I need orders, now.
stages.feedback: Without anticoagulation the right ventricle kept straining against rising afterload, tipping him toward obstructive shock.

stages.event: Cardiac arrest from decompensated right ventricular failure.
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive and I can't get a pressure — starting compressions!
stages.feedback: The core resuscitation for right-heart strain — anticoagulation — was never given, and the right ventricle failed outright.

debrief.goodOutcome: Submassive PE recognized despite the ACS-mimicking ECG, confirmed on CTPA, and treated with prompt therapeutic anticoagulation while thrombolysis was held in reserve.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on an ACS workup, anticoagulation for his pulmonary embolism was delayed and his right ventricle decompensated into obstructive shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the ECG strain pattern as a PE mimic rather than an anterior MI, risk-stratified him with troponin, BNP, and bedside cardiac ultrasound, confirmed extensive clot burden and RV strain on CT pulmonary angiography, and started therapeutic anticoagulation promptly — his tachycardia and hypoxia trended back toward normal without ever needing thrombolysis.
debrief.outcomePoor: The ECG and mildly positive troponin kept the team chasing an ischemic workup, therapeutic anticoagulation was never started, and his right ventricle progressively failed against rising clot burden.
learningPoints: The classic PE triad of pleuritic pain, dyspnea, and hemoptysis is rarely all present; hemoptysis occurs in under 3% of PE patients
Right ventricular outflow obstruction from PE raises RV afterload, causing wall stress, dilation, and dysfunction that can progress to hypotension and hemodynamic collapse if untreated
A normal or only mildly elevated troponin and a preserved blood pressure do not exclude submassive PE with RV strain
Severity assessment with clinical findings, cardiac troponin, and focused cardiac ultrasonography guides the anticoagulation-versus-thrombolysis decision in intermediate-risk PE
Recent malignancy, major surgery, and a prior VTE history should raise pretest probability for PE even when the presentation mimics ACS
missedOpportunities: Recognizing the ECG's right-heart-strain pattern rather than anchoring on ACS
Risk-stratifying with troponin, BNP, and bedside cardiac ultrasound for RV strain
Obtaining CT pulmonary angiography to confirm and quantify clot burden
Starting therapeutic anticoagulation promptly instead of completing an ACS workup first
Reassessing vitals frequently enough to catch hemodynamic deterioration

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: For about a week I've been more and more short of breath and worn out. Today I stood up and nearly passed out, and my heart just races.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I had breast cancer two years ago with chemo and radiation. They said I was in remission. No heart or kidney problems that I know of.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Just a vitamin. No heart pills or water pills, and I'm not on any blood thinners. No allergies.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I don't smoke and rarely drink. No chest injuries. My family's healthy, no early heart trouble.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No sharp chest pain, more a heavy pressure. I've had some low fevers and lost weight this month. No cough and my legs aren't swollen.

physicalExam.finding: Anxious, leaning forward, tachypneic and diaphoretic. Pale, speaking in short sentences but protecting her airway.
Jugular venous pressure markedly elevated to the angle of the jaw at 45 degrees. The JVP does not fall with inspiration.
Tachycardic and regular. Heart sounds distant and muffled, no murmur or friction rub. Pulsus paradoxus measured at 16 mmHg. Peripheral pulses thready.
Tachypneic. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally — no crackles, no wheeze, no signs of consolidation.
Cool, mottled hands and feet with delayed capillary refill (~4 s). No peripheral edema. Distal pulses weak and thready.

image.query: 12-lead ECG low voltage QRS with electrical alternans
portable chest x-ray enlarged globular water-bottle cardiac silhouette clear lungs

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Probe's on. There's a big black stripe all the way around the heart, and the right side looks like it's getting squeezed.

diagnosticReports.title: Bedside Echocardiogram (Subxiphoid/Parasternal)
diagnosticReports.body: Large circumferential anechoic pericardial effusion with diastolic collapse of the right ventricular free wall and IVC plethora without respiratory variation. IMPRESSION: Large pericardial effusion with echocardiographic tamponade physiology.

image.query: bedside echocardiogram large circumferential pericardial effusion with diastolic right ventricular collapse

diagnosticReports.title: CT Pulmonary Angiogram, Chest
diagnosticReports.body: Large circumferential pericardial fluid collection measuring up to 3.1 cm, with flattening and compression of the right atrial and right ventricular free walls. Reflux of contrast into a dilated IVC and azygos vein. No filling defect within the pulmonary arteries to the segmental level. Lungs clear without consolidation or effusion. IMPRESSION: Large pericardial effusion with imaging features of right-heart chamber compression and elevated systemic venous pressures; no pulmonary embolus identified.

image.query: CT chest large pericardial effusion with right heart compression no pulmonary embolism

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Bolus running in wide open. Her pressure's coming up a little and she looks slightly more comfortable.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Bolus volume not specified — give a modest ~500 mL crystalloid bolus and reassess.

elseSpeech.speaker: consultant
elseSpeech.text: We don't have imaging confirmation yet — a quick bedside echo will show the effusion and guide the needle safely before you stick her.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: You aspirate a rush of bloody fluid and almost immediately her pressure jumps and her neck veins come down. She says she can finally breathe.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — you're treating this as obstructive shock from a pericardial effusion. The team's ready for your orders.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Got it — no diuretics or nitrates. I'll flag anyone who tries to give something that drops her preload.

any: keep her breathing on her own

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — we'll avoid positive-pressure ventilation and support her own breathing for now.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Do not diurese her. Pulling volume off a patient this preload-dependent will collapse her pressure.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Stop — nitrates will drop her preload and her pressure will bottom out. Not in this patient.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Careful — putting her on positive pressure now could drop her preload and tip her into arrest. Can we hold off?

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Cardiology and CT surgery are en route and standing by to help with definitive management. Call us the moment you need us.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU and the cath lab are ready — she's going up now with cardiology at the bedside.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity obstructive shock — preload-dependent physiology decompensates to PEA arrest without emergent drainage

stages.event: Worsening obstructive shock
stages.nurse: Doctor, her pressure's crashing and she's working really hard to breathe — I need something from you now.
stages.feedback: Obstructive shock is decompensating. A temporizing fluid bolus buys a little time, but only relieving the pressure around the heart fixes the physiology.

stages.event: PEA cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: She's lost her pulse — I'm starting compressions! What do you want to do?
stages.feedback: The effusion compressed the heart into a pulseless arrest. Definitive drainage was the only intervention that could have reversed it in time.

debrief.goodOutcome: Tamponade recognized and drained; obstructive shock reversed.
debrief.badOutcome: Preload-dependent physiology was mismanaged and the effusion compressed the heart into arrest.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized Beck triad and pulsus paradoxus, confirmed a pericardial effusion on bedside echo, supported preload with fluid while avoiding diuretics, nitrates, and positive-pressure ventilation, and drained the pericardium — reversing obstructive shock.
debrief.outcomePoor: The obstructive physiology went unrecognized or untreated. Preload-lowering interventions or delay to drainage let the effusion tamponade the heart into a PEA arrest.
learningPoints: Tamponade is preload-dependent — support volume, avoid nitrates/diuretics and, if possible, positive-pressure ventilation.
Beck triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled tones) + pulsus paradoxus; confirm with bedside echo.
Electrical alternans and low voltage on ECG suggest a large effusion.
Pericardiocentesis is the definitive, life-saving intervention.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing obstructive shock from Beck triad and pulsus paradoxus
Bedside echocardiogram to confirm the pericardial effusion
Emergent pericardiocentesis to relieve the tamponade
Avoiding diuretics, nitrates, and positive-pressure ventilation

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: (breathless) My heart's just... slamming... chest hurts... started maybe twenty minutes ago.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He was watching TV when it hit — no exertion, no trauma. He's gotten more confused in the last few minutes.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: (slowly) I had a heart attack... few years back. They said my heart... doesn't pump like it used to.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His wife says metoprolol, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and a daily aspirin. No known drug allergies.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity unstable wide-complex tachycardia — without synchronized cardioversion, perfusion collapses and the rhythm degenerates toward pulseless VT/VF

stages.event: Worsening hypoperfusion, persistent unstable wide-complex tachycardia
stages.nurse: Doctor, his pressure's in the 60s now and he's barely rousable — I need you to act.
stages.feedback: Prolonged unstable wide-complex tachycardia is collapsing his perfusion. Each additional minute without definitive rhythm conversion increases the risk of arrest.

stages.event: Degeneration to pulseless ventricular fibrillation
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive, no pulse — he's in V-fib! Starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Unstable VT left uncardioverted degenerated into pulseless ventricular fibrillation. Synchronized cardioversion was the definitive, immediate treatment and could not be substituted with drug trials or delay.

debrief.goodOutcome: Unstable wide-complex tachycardia recognized and treated as VT; synchronized cardioversion restored perfusion, with post-conversion workup and antiarrhythmic therapy in place.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the unstable wide-complex tachycardia as VT, sedated and synchronously cardioverted him, avoided AV-nodal blockers, obtained a post-conversion 12-lead, corrected his potassium and magnesium, started amiodarone, and got cardiology involved — perfusion restored.
debrief.outcomePoor: Definitive rhythm conversion was delayed or never performed, and the unstable VT degenerated into pulseless ventricular fibrillation.
debrief.badOutcome: Trialing drugs or verapamil instead of cardioverting an unstable wide-complex tachycardia let it degenerate into pulseless VT/VF and cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: Unstable wide-complex tachycardia → synchronized cardioversion, not drug trials.
In a patient with structural heart disease, treat wide-complex tachycardia as VT.
Verapamil/adenosine can be lethal if the rhythm is actually VT.
After conversion, correct K/Mg, evaluate for ischemia, and start amiodarone.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing unstable wide-complex tachycardia and treating it as VT
Synchronized cardioversion with pre-procedural sedation
Avoiding AV-nodal blockers/verapamil in a wide-complex tachycardia of uncertain origin
Correcting potassium/magnesium and starting amiodarone after conversion

deterioration.trajectory: hemodynamically stable at presentation but at real risk of decompensation if the wide complex tachycardia is not characterized and treated as VT — the trap is a false sense of security from her calm affect.

stages.event: Ongoing wide complex tachycardia without diagnostic clarity
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's still racing up there on the monitor and getting a little more short of breath. Are we doing anything about this rhythm?
stages.feedback: Without a 12-lead to characterize the wide complex rhythm and look for AV dissociation or fusion/capture beats, the workup is stalled.

stages.event: Increasing hemodynamic strain from sustained VT
stages.nurse: Her pressure's dropping and she says she's getting lightheaded. She needs something done, now.
stages.feedback: Sustained VT without an antiarrhythmic or cardioversion is progressing toward hemodynamic collapse.

stages.event: Degeneration to pulseless VT/ventricular fibrillation
stages.nurse: She's unresponsive, no pulse! Starting compressions, get the crash cart!
stages.feedback: Untreated sustained VT degenerated into a pulseless arrest — the core resuscitation, an antiarrhythmic or cardioversion for stable VT, was never delivered.

debrief.goodOutcome: Regular wide complex tachycardia recognized and treated as VT from the start; AV-nodal blockers avoided; a stable-VT antiarrhythmic (or cardioversion) restored sinus rhythm; cardiology consulted for EP evaluation.
debrief.badOutcome: The wide complex tachycardia was treated as a diagnostic trial with an AV-nodal blocking agent, or never definitively treated at all, and it degenerated into pulseless VT/VF arrest.
debrief.outcomeGood: You treated the wide complex tachycardia as VT until proven otherwise, obtained the 12-lead, asked about her prior MI and reduced ejection fraction, avoided verapamil/diltiazem, converted her with procainamide, amiodarone, or synchronized cardioversion, and looped in cardiology — Sharon left the department in sinus rhythm, accepted for admission and EP workup.
debrief.outcomePoor: An AV-nodal blocking agent was given as a 'diagnostic trial,' or a definitive antiarrhythmic/cardioversion was never chosen, and the rhythm progressed to hemodynamic collapse and cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: VT is the most common cause of a regular wide complex tachycardia and should be assumed first, not last.
Hemodynamic stability does not rule out VT — a calm, conversant patient can still be in VT and can still deteriorate suddenly.
Structural heart disease (prior MI, reduced ejection fraction) sharply raises the pretest probability of VT over SVT with aberrancy.
AV-nodal blocking agents like verapamil and diltiazem are dangerous 'diagnostic trials' when the rhythm turns out to be VT.
Stable VT is treated with procainamide or amiodarone (with pads on and cardioversion ready), not empiric rate control.
missedOpportunities: Treating the rhythm as VT until proven otherwise rather than assuming SVT with aberrancy
Obtaining a 12-lead ECG and assessing for AV dissociation and fusion/capture beats
Asking about prior MI, stents, and ejection fraction
Avoiding empiric AV-nodal-blocking agents such as verapamil or diltiazem
Choosing a stable-VT antiarrhythmic or synchronized cardioversion instead of a drug trial
Consulting cardiology given known ischemic cardiomyopathy

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity occlusion MI without STEMI criteria — delayed recognition and reperfusion allow the infarct to extend toward cardiogenic shock and arrest

stages.event: Persistent ischemic pain, non-diagnostic ECG anchoring
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's still clutching his chest and the monitor still isn't calling a STEMI — are we doing something or just watching?
stages.feedback: The occlusion pattern was already on the first ECG; recognizing it and getting the team moving couldn't wait on a millimeter cutoff.

stages.event: Extending infarct, early hypotension
stages.nurse: His pressure's slipping and he's gone quiet — I need a plan, now.
stages.feedback: Delayed reperfusion let the infarct extend toward cardiogenic shock; the artery still needed to be opened.

stages.event: Cardiac arrest — pulseless ventricular fibrillation
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive, no pulse — he's in V-fib! Starting compressions!
stages.feedback: An occluded artery left unopened for an hour led to extensive infarction and arrest; primary PCI needed to happen far earlier.

debrief.goodOutcome: Occlusion MI recognized despite non-diagnostic STEMI criteria; cath lab activated and he went for timely primary PCI.
debrief.badOutcome: The ECG was read as non-diagnostic and the workup anchored on trending troponins while the artery kept closing.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the occlusion pattern despite the ECG not meeting STEMI millimeter criteria, gave aspirin and ACS-protocol therapy, used serial ECGs and troponin as adjuncts without waiting on them, and activated the cath lab for emergent primary PCI.
debrief.outcomePoor: The ECG sat labeled as non-diagnostic while pain continued and troponins were trended — the occlusion went unrecognized and reperfusion was delayed until he progressed to cardiogenic shock and arrest.
learningPoints: Acute coronary occlusion can occur without meeting STEMI millimeter criteria — learn the occlusion patterns (hyperacute T waves, de Winter, Wellens, aVR elevation with diffuse depression).
Serial ECGs and comparison to prior tracings reveal dynamic occlusion changes that a single static tracing can miss.
A normal or only mildly elevated troponin should not delay reperfusion when the ECG pattern and symptoms are convincing.
Activating the cath lab and involving cardiology emergently is the definitive treatment — trending labs is not a substitute for reperfusion.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing the occlusion pattern on a non-diagnostic ECG
Obtaining serial ECGs and posterior/right-sided leads
Activating the cath lab for emergent primary PCI without waiting on troponin trends

stages.event: Untreated hypoglycemia worsens — decreased responsiveness with jerking movements of the extremities
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's stopped responding to my voice and her arms just started jerking — I need orders now!
stages.feedback: Untreated hypoglycemia in acute liver failure can precipitate seizures and further neurologic injury — checking and correcting glucose early is essential.

stages.event: Rising intracranial pressure — hypertension with reflex bradycardia and irregular respirations, sluggish pupil noted
stages.nurse: Doctor, her pressure's spiking, her heart rate's dropping, and her right pupil's looking sluggish — I need direction right now!
stages.feedback: Rising intracranial pressure from cerebral edema is the leading cause of death in acute liver failure; head elevation, minimizing stimulation, and hyperosmolar therapy for herniation cannot be delayed.

stages.event: Cardiopulmonary arrest from unmanaged cerebral herniation and refractory hypoglycemia
stages.nurse: Doctor, she has no pulse — we're starting compressions now!
stages.feedback: Without timely correction of hypoglycemia and aggressive management of raised intracranial pressure, cerebral herniation and cardiopulmonary arrest become unrecoverable — these core resuscitation steps cannot wait for transfer.

debrief.goodOutcome: Acute liver failure recognized early; NAC started, hypoglycemia corrected, cerebral edema managed, and the patient transferred to a transplant center in time.
debrief.badOutcome: Cerebral edema and hypoglycemia went unmanaged while the coagulopathy was reflexively chased with FFP — the patient herniated and arrested before transfer.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized acute liver failure, sent an acetaminophen level and started NAC, corrected the hypoglycemia, managed rising ICP, avoided reflexive FFP, and got her to a transplant center early — the best possible path for a patient like this.
debrief.outcomePoor: Hypoglycemia and rising intracranial pressure were never addressed, and the INR was chased with FFP instead of trended as a prognostic marker — the encephalopathy progressed to herniation and arrest.
learningPoints: Give N-acetylcysteine in acute liver failure — it helps even non-acetaminophen and late presentations.
Cerebral edema is the leading cause of death; treat hypoglycemia aggressively and monitor glucose closely.
Don't reflexively correct the INR with FFP — it's a key prognostic marker, not something to normalize unless the patient is actively bleeding or needs a procedure.
Refer early to a transplant center using King's College criteria — don't wait for decompensation.

Checking and treating hypoglycemia early and aggressively
Elevating the head of bed, minimizing stimulation, and treating rising ICP with hyperosmolar therapy
Avoiding empiric FFP and trending the INR instead of chasing it
Early hepatology consultation and transfer to a liver transplant center

speech.text: Synchronized cardioversion performed at 100 J — converted to sinus rhythm.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Cardiology here — rate's controlled, anticoagulation's addressed, echo's reassuring. Agree with admission to telemetry and outpatient rhythm-management follow-up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Bed's ready on telemetry — report called, orders reconciled.

deterioration.trajectory: stable but symptomatic AF with RVR — untreated, uncontrolled rate drives hemodynamic strain; unaddressed thromboembolic risk drives embolic complications; either path, left wholly unmanaged, ends in decompensation

stages.event: Uncontrolled rate — worsening dyspnea and discomfort
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's still racing in the 160s and looks more short of breath — what's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Ongoing uncontrolled rate is causing hemodynamic strain; rate control with an IV beta-blocker or diltiazem is needed.

stages.event: New focal deficit — concern for cardioembolic event
stages.nurse: Doctor, his right hand suddenly feels weak and his words are slurring — I need you at the bedside now.
stages.feedback: Unaddressed thromboembolic risk in AF — duration was never determined and anticoagulation was never started — allowed a cardioembolic event.

stages.event: Decompensation to cardiogenic shock
stages.nurse: Doctor, his pressure's crashing and he's barely responding — I need orders right now!
stages.feedback: Rate/rhythm control was never achieved; sustained uncontrolled AF with RVR led to hemodynamic collapse.

debrief.goodOutcome: Rate controlled, hemodynamic stability confirmed, duration determined, WPW excluded, anticoagulation addressed, precipitants ruled out, and admitted to telemetry.
debrief.badOutcome: Uncontrolled rate drove hemodynamic decompensation, or an unaddressed thromboembolic risk resulted in a cardioembolic event.
debrief.outcomeGood: You confirmed he was stable, controlled the rate with an IV beta-blocker or diltiazem, determined the AF had started only hours earlier, excluded pre-excitation, addressed his stroke risk with anticoagulation, and screened for precipitants — a well-managed new AF with RVR headed to telemetry.
debrief.outcomePoor: The rate was never adequately controlled, or duration and thromboembolic risk were never addressed — either path let the rhythm's consequences catch up with him.
learningPoints: Determine stability, duration, and preexcitation before choosing rate vs rhythm control.
Cardioverting AF of >48h/unknown duration without anticoagulation risks embolic stroke.
In AF with WPW (wide, irregular, very fast), AV-nodal blockers are contraindicated — use procainamide or cardiovert.
AF may be compensatory for sepsis/PE/thyrotoxicosis — treat the underlying cause, don't just slow the rate.
missedOpportunities: Assessing hemodynamic stability and confirming the rhythm on a 12-lead ECG
Rate control with an IV beta-blocker or diltiazem
Determining AF duration before considering cardioversion
Assessing CHA2DS2-VASc and starting anticoagulation
Recognizing the absence of WPW/pre-excitation before using AV-nodal blockers
Screening for reversible precipitants (thyroid, ischemia, infection)

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's been in jail three days with nothing to drink. Started shaking yesterday, and today he's seeing things and doesn't know where he is.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Get them off me... there's bugs everywhere. Where am I?

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's never really seen a doctor. I think he has some liver problems from the drinking, but nothing's ever been treated.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He doesn't take any regular medicines. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He drinks hard liquor every day, close to a pint of vodka, has for years. This is the longest he's gone without a drink in a long time.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's had the shakes before when he tried to stop, and a few years back he had a seizure a couple of days after his last drink.

physicalExam.finding: Diaphoretic and visibly tremulous with a coarse hand tremor, agitated and picking at the IV line and bedsheets; unkempt, poor dentition.
Disoriented to place and date, inattentive, describes seeing insects crawling on the sheets; pressured speech, coarse tremor, mild hyperreflexia, no focal weakness.
Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs; skin warm and diaphoretic, pulses bounding.
Soft, mild epigastric tenderness without rebound or guarding; liver edge palpable 2 cm below the costal margin, no ascites appreciated.
Diaphoretic and flushed; spider angiomata on the chest and palmar erythema noted; no jaundice, no bruising or trauma.

labResults.name: Urine Drug Screen
labResults.value: Negative for opioids, benzodiazepines, amphetamines, cocaine

labResults.name: Urinalysis
labResults.value: No pyuria, nitrites, or leukocyte esterase — unremarkable

image.query: sinus tachycardia rate 130 no ST changes normal QRS interval

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: No acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, or midline shift. Ventricles and sulci are within normal limits for age. No acute infarct. IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial abnormality.

image.query: noncontrast head CT normal ventricles no hemorrhage no mass effect

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Benzodiazepine dose not specified — titrate to a validated symptom-triggered scale (e.g., diazepam 10-20 mg IV or lorazepam 2-4 mg IV, repeat and escalate as needed).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: First dose given IV — I'll keep scoring him and flag you if he needs more.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Dose not specified for refractory sedation — e.g., phenobarbital 10 mg/kg IV load, or a dexmedetomidine/propofol infusion with airway control.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Reasonable for benzodiazepine-refractory DTs — escalating sedation with close monitoring of his airway and respiratory status.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Thiamine dose/route not specified — 500 mg IV, given before any glucose.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Thiamine 500 mg IV given now, before any glucose.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Hold on, Doctor — let's get thiamine on board first before we push any sugar.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Dextrose given — thiamine was already on board, so we're covered.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Liter of normal saline running.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Magnesium, potassium, and phosphate repletion running per protocol.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold off — antipsychotics alone don't treat the underlying withdrawal and can lower his seizure threshold. He needs benzodiazepines, not haloperidol.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Starting CIWA-Ar scoring now, Doctor — I'll flag you every time he trends up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Full head-to-toe done — no wounds or bruising, abdomen only mildly tender, guaiac negative. Cultures and UA are pending, lipase back and unremarkable.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's not just drunk, Doctor — his last drink was three days ago per his brother, and his pressure and heart rate keep climbing. This won't sleep off on its own.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Tox/critical care here — keep escalating benzodiazepines on the CIWA scale, add phenobarbital if he's refractory, and let's get him monitored. We'll help watch for a co-ingestion or withdrawal seizure.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed is ready, Doctor — we'll transport him on the monitor with the CIWA protocol continuing.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity alcohol withdrawal — untreated autonomic hyperactivity progresses to withdrawal seizures, hyperthermia, and cardiovascular collapse without escalating symptom-triggered benzodiazepines

stages.event: Autonomic hyperactivity intensifies — tremor and diaphoresis worsen, agitation escalates, heart rate and blood pressure climb further.
stages.nurse: He's soaked through the sheets and picking at the IV line again, Doctor — his pressure keeps climbing. What do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: Escalating symptom-triggered benzodiazepines are overdue — untreated autonomic hyperactivity is progressing toward seizure.

stages.event: A witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure occurs at the bedside, followed by brief post-ictal desaturation.
stages.nurse: He just seized right in front of me — I need orders now!
stages.feedback: A withdrawal seizure occurred because escalating benzodiazepines were never given — this is preventable with timely, symptom-triggered dosing.

stages.event: Malignant hyperthermia and cardiovascular collapse follow repeated seizures; the monitor shows a lethal arrhythmia and pulses are lost.
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive, no pulse — he's coding!
stages.feedback: Untreated delirium tremens progressed to cardiac arrest. Escalating benzodiazepines, with phenobarbital for refractory symptoms, were the definitive therapy and were never given.

debrief.goodOutcome: Delirium tremens recognized and treated with escalating symptom-triggered benzodiazepines, thiamine before glucose, electrolyte repletion, and a workup for a concurrent precipitant, with ICU admission.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized delirium tremens, escalated symptom-triggered benzodiazepines (adding phenobarbital when needed), gave thiamine before any glucose, corrected his fluids and electrolytes, ruled out a concurrent illness or injury, and admitted him to a monitored ICU bed.
debrief.outcomePoor: Benzodiazepines were delayed or never escalated, and untreated autonomic hyperactivity progressed to a withdrawal seizure and cardiovascular collapse.
debrief.badOutcome: Without escalating benzodiazepines, his autonomic hyperactivity progressed through a witnessed seizure to a lethal arrhythmia and cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: Symptom-triggered escalating benzodiazepines are the cornerstone of DT management; add phenobarbital (or dexmedetomidine/propofol with airway control) for refractory cases.
Give thiamine before glucose to avoid precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy.
Delirium tremens typically appears 48-96 hours after the last drink and can be fatal if undertreated.
Always search for a concurrent, precipitating illness or injury — infection, trauma, GI bleed, or pancreatitis.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing delirium tremens and starting a symptom-triggered protocol
Escalating benzodiazepines (and phenobarbital if refractory)
Thiamine before glucose
Correcting magnesium, potassium, and phosphate
Workup for a concurrent illness or injury
ICU/monitored admission

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was totally fine, then out of nowhere he grabbed his head, said it was the worst pain of his life, and went down. His left side's been weak ever since, and he's thrown up twice.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's got atrial fibrillation — that's why he's on the warfarin — and high blood pressure. No prior strokes or bleeds that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Just the warfarin, five milligrams every evening, and a pill for his pressure. No aspirin, no other blood thinners. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's retired, lives with me, doesn't smoke, has a glass of wine now and then. No family history of bleeding problems that I know of.

any: how long has this been going on

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was talking to me normally maybe forty-five minutes ago. This all happened fast — headache, then he grabbed his head and went down within a couple minutes.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, intermittently opens eyes to voice but drifts off between questions, not consistently following commands. Vomited once on arrival.

GCS 9 (eyes open to voice, incomprehensible sounds, localizes on the right/withdraws on the left). Right pupil 4 mm and sluggishly reactive, left pupil 3 mm and brisk. Left arm and leg 1/5 strength, right side full strength. Positive Babinski on the left.
No scalp laceration, hematoma, or bruising. No tongue trauma or other seizure stigmata. Ears and nose clear, no CSF otorrhea or rhinorrhea.
Irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with known atrial fibrillation. No murmurs. Peripheral pulses intact and symmetric.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Coags are back — INR's up at 3.7, PT's prolonged, PTT's normal. He's supratherapeutic on the warfarin.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage centered in the right basal ganglia/thalamus measuring approximately 4.2 x 3.5 cm, with surrounding vasogenic edema and 6 mm rightward-to-leftward midline shift. Small amount of intraventricular extension into the right lateral ventricle. No extra-axial hemorrhage. Calvarium intact. IMPRESSION: Acute right basal ganglia intraparenchymal hemorrhage with mass effect and midline shift — recommend urgent neurosurgical evaluation.

image.query: noncontrast axial head CT showing right basal ganglia intraparenchymal hemorrhage with midline shift
12-lead ECG showing atrial fibrillation, irregularly irregular rhythm without discrete P waves

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: On it — pharmacy's already pulling reversal agents based on his med list.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Hold on, Doctor — we don't have his coags back yet. Want me to send an INR/PT first before we reverse?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: 4-factor PCC and IV vitamin K given — repeat INR pending recheck.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: PCC and vitamin K dosing not specified — 4-factor PCC dosed to weight and INR (typically 25-50 units/kg) plus vitamin K 10 mg IV.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: FFP will help some, but it's slow to thaw, needs cross-matching, and it's a big volume load for an elderly man — PCC corrects the INR faster with less volume. Add PCC if you haven't.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pharmacy checked — he's on warfarin, not a DOAC. His INR fits that; if it were dabigatran we'd reach for idarucizumab, and a factor Xa inhibitor would call for andexanet alfa or PCC.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Stop — sublingual nifedipine drops the pressure too fast and unpredictably. In a fresh bleed like this that risks cerebral hypoperfusion. Use a titratable IV agent instead.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Nicardipine drip started, titrating toward a systolic in the 140s to 150s — not lower.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Nicardipine dose/rate not specified — start 5 mg/hr IV, titrate to a systolic target of about 140-160 mmHg, avoiding an excessive drop.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Head of bed up to 30, hypertonic saline running, and we're set up for RSI given how he's protecting his airway.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Hyperosmolar/airway dosing not specified — mannitol 1 g/kg IV or 3% saline bolus, plus RSI medications dosed to weight for a GCS this low.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neurosurgery here — we've seen the scan and the coags. We'll trend serial exams while reversal finishes and decide on OR versus medical management; neurocritical care's aware too.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Neuro ICU bed's ready and neurosurgery wants a repeat head CT in six hours.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity: anticoagulated ICH — hematoma expansion drives deterioration, so delayed agent-specific reversal, uncontrolled blood pressure, and unmanaged ICP/airway compromise progress to herniation and death

stages.event: Increasing left-sided weakness and decreasing responsiveness
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's harder to arouse than he was fifteen minutes ago and that left side isn't moving at all now — are we any closer to a plan?
stages.feedback: Without prompt agent-specific reversal of the coagulopathy, the hematoma keeps expanding and the exam keeps worsening.

stages.event: Anisocoria and early Cushing physiology — impending herniation
stages.nurse: His right pupil just blew and he's not withdrawing on that left side anymore — this is getting worse fast.
stages.feedback: Uncorrected coagulopathy plus uncontrolled, markedly elevated blood pressure lets the hematoma keep expanding, driving intracranial pressure toward herniation.

stages.event: Brainstem herniation — fixed and dilated pupils, apnea, cardiovascular collapse
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's not breathing on his own and I don't have a pulse.
stages.feedback: Without both reversal of the coagulopathy and aggressive management of raised ICP and the airway, hematoma expansion progresses to fatal herniation.

debrief.goodOutcome: Anticoagulation-associated intracerebral hemorrhage recognized and reversed before herniation.
debrief.outcomeGood: You obtained emergent head CT and coagulation studies, reversed the warfarin with PCC and IV vitamin K, controlled his blood pressure to target without over-correcting, protected his airway and managed rising ICP, and got neurosurgery and neurocritical care involved urgently — the sequence that limits hematoma expansion and prevents herniation.
debrief.outcomePoor: Reversal, blood pressure control, and ICP/airway management weren't completed in time, and hematoma expansion progressed to herniation and death.
debrief.badOutcome: Delayed or incomplete agent-specific reversal and blood pressure control allowed the hematoma to expand into a fatal herniation.
learningPoints: In anticoagulated ICH, rapid agent-specific reversal limits hematoma expansion.
Warfarin: PCC plus IV vitamin K (PCC is faster and preferred over FFP).
Dabigatran: idarucizumab; factor Xa inhibitors: andexanet alfa (or PCC).
Control blood pressure and involve neurosurgery emergently.
missedOpportunities: Emergent noncontrast head CT and coagulation studies
Agent-specific reversal: PCC plus IV vitamin K for warfarin
Blood pressure control to a moderate target, avoiding an excessive drop
Management of raised ICP and airway protection for declining consciousness
Urgent neurosurgery and neurocritical care consultation

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It started as a dull ache around my belly button yesterday, then last night it moved down and settled on the right side. Now it's sharp and it hurts to move.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No real medical problems. I've never had surgery or been in the hospital for anything.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I don't take any medications, and I don't have any allergies.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: A couple beers on weekends, don't smoke, no other drugs. I work in a warehouse.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I had breakfast around 8 this morning. Nothing since — I haven't wanted to eat.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I threw up once earlier. No appetite at all. No diarrhea — still passing gas, last bowel movement was yesterday morning, normal.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No burning, no blood, peeing normally.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable-appearing, lying still with knees drawn up, avoids sudden movement. Alert and non-toxic. Low-grade fever on exam, mildly tachycardic.

Focal tenderness at McBurney's point in the right lower quadrant with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness. Positive Rovsing's sign (RLQ pain elicited with LLQ palpation) and positive psoas sign. Bowel sounds hypoactive. No palpable mass, no distension.
Testes descended bilaterally, non-tender, normal lie, no swelling or high-riding testis. Cremasteric reflex intact bilaterally. No inguinal hernia palpated.
Normal sphincter tone, no mass. Mild right-sided tenderness on rectal exam. Stool heme-negative.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: The appendix is dilated to 11 mm with wall thickening and surrounding periappendiceal fat stranding in the right lower quadrant. A small calcified appendicolith is present at its base. No free air, drainable fluid collection, or abscess identified; trace free fluid in the pelvis. IMPRESSION: Dilated, thick-walled appendix with adjacent inflammatory fat stranding and an appendicolith, representing acute inflammation of the appendix without evidence of perforation or abscess at this time.

No appendicolith identified. IMPRESSION: Dilated noncompressible appendix, compatible with acute appendicitis.

diagnosticReports.body: Supine and upright views show a nonspecific, nonobstructive bowel gas pattern without dilated small or large bowel loops. No free intraperitoneal air. No abnormal calcification. IMPRESSION: Nonspecific bowel gas pattern; no free air or obstruction.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: IV placed, normal saline running.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's made NPO — nothing by mouth in case he goes to the OR.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fentanyl 100 mcg IV given — his pain's down from 8 to 3.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Analgesic dose/route not specified — fentanyl 50-100 mcg IV or morphine 4 mg IV.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV given.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Antibiotic dose/route not specified — piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV (or ceftriaxone 1 g IV plus metronidazole 500 mg IV).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Agreed — that pattern fits. I'll let surgery know we're thinking that way and keep him NPO for now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Good call — we're watching closely for signs of rupture or abscess, and keeping an atypical course in mind given his exam.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Doctor, are you sure? He still has a fever and his exam hasn't changed — he's still guarding on the right side.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Surgery here — exam and imaging line up. We'll take him back for an appendectomy. Keep him NPO and fluids running until we're ready.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Doctor, surgery hasn't seen him yet — we can't take him up without them on board.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: OR's ready, anesthesia's aware — he's headed up now.

deterioration.trajectory: an unrecognized or untreated appendix progresses from localized inflammation to perforation, peritonitis, and septic shock

stages.event: Pain worsening, tenderness spreading beyond the right lower quadrant
stages.nurse: Doctor, his pain's worse and he's more tender everywhere now, not just the right side — are we doing anything for him?
stages.feedback: Without recognizing the migratory pain and peritoneal signs early, the workup stalls while the inflamed appendix continues toward perforation.

stages.event: Signs of perforation — diffuse guarding, spiking fever
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's guarding all over his belly now and his fever's climbing — what's the plan?
stages.feedback: Delaying imaging and antibiotics lets a contained appendix perforate and seed generalized peritonitis.

stages.event: Septic shock from perforated appendicitis with diffuse peritonitis
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's unresponsive and I can barely get a pressure — we need to act now!
stages.feedback: Without antibiotics and an emergent surgical consult, a perforated appendix with diffuse peritonitis progresses to fatal septic shock.

debrief.goodOutcome: Acute appendicitis recognized from the migratory pain and peritoneal signs, worked up with labs and appropriate imaging, treated with analgesia, IV fluids, NPO status, and antibiotics, and taken to the OR by surgery before it could perforate.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the migratory RLQ pain and peritoneal signs, sent labs, confirmed the diagnosis on CT, treated his pain without hesitation, started antibiotics, kept him NPO, and got surgery on board for an appendectomy.
debrief.outcomePoor: The classic pattern wasn't acted on quickly enough — recognition, imaging, antibiotics, or the surgical consult were delayed, and the appendix progressed to perforation and septic shock.
debrief.badOutcome: Delay in recognizing the appendicitis pattern and involving surgery allowed the appendix to perforate, leading to diffuse peritonitis and fatal septic shock.
learningPoints: Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with anorexia and peritoneal signs (McBurney's tenderness, guarding, rebound, Rovsing's sign) suggests appendicitis; a normal WBC doesn't exclude it.
Ultrasound is preferred first-line in children and pregnancy to avoid radiation; CT is appropriate when the diagnosis is uncertain in adults.
Analgesia does not obscure the surgical exam — treat pain promptly rather than withholding it.
Give IV fluids, keep the patient NPO, start antibiotics, and consult surgery for appendectomy once the diagnosis is suspected.
Watch for atypical presentations (retrocecal, pelvic, pregnancy, extremes of age) and signs of complicated disease (perforation, abscess).
missedOpportunities: Recognizing the migratory pain and peritoneal exam findings as appendicitis
Sending labs (CBC, inflammatory markers) and choosing appropriate imaging
Treating pain with analgesia rather than withholding it
Starting IV fluids, keeping the patient NPO, and giving antibiotics
Consulting surgery for appendectomy before perforation

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's been yellow since she was a few days old and it just never went away. Last week or so her poop turned pale, almost gray, and her pee looks dark.

speech.speaker: family

speech.speaker: family

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's exclusively breastfed, feeding well every two to three hours, and gaining weight fine. Plenty of wet diapers, no vomiting.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: I brought a diaper to show you — the stool's been pale and chalky-looking for about a week, and her urine stains the diaper dark yellow-brown.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: No medications and no allergies. She gets her daily vitamin D drops, that's it.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: No liver problems that we know of in the family. She's our first baby, and my husband and I aren't related.

physicalExam.finding: Awake, active, well-hydrated 5-week-old with a deep yellow-green tinge to the skin and sclerae. Non-toxic, feeding interest normal, no respiratory distress.
Jaundice extends to palms and soles with a slightly greenish hue rather than the classic lemon-yellow of unconjugated jaundice. Scleral icterus present.
Mildly distended, non-tender. Liver edge firm and palpable ~3 cm below the right costal margin. Spleen tip not definitively palpable today.
Pale, putty-colored (acholic) stool visible in the diaper; urine staining the diaper a dark yellow-brown.
Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur. Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no distress.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Total bilirubin's back — 9.5. Want that fractionated into total and direct?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fractionated bilirubin is back.

labResults.value: Negative (toxoplasma, rubella, CMV, HSV IgM non-reactive)

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Agreed — jaundice this far out with pale stools isn't something to just watch.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: We're still waiting on the fractionated panel — total and direct are pending.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Right — any direct component this high is never just physiologic.

any: this is not normal newborn jaundice

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: So it's not just from the breastfeeding, like we were told?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Correct — pale stools and a direct bilirubin like this rule out simple breast-milk jaundice.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Vitamin K dose/route not specified — 1 mg IM for an infant this size.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitamin K 1 mg IM given.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold off — phototherapy treats unconjugated bilirubin. It won't touch a conjugated elevation like this and can cause bronze baby syndrome. Not indicated here.

diagnosticReports.title: Abdominal/Hepatobiliary Ultrasound
diagnosticReports.body: Gallbladder not visualized after adequate fasting interval. Echogenic triangular/tubular cord-like density noted anterior to the right portal vein. Liver parenchyma mildly coarse. No intra/extrahepatic ductal dilation. IMPRESSION: Nonvisualized gallbladder with triangular cord sign, concerning for an obstructive cholestatic process.

image.query: infant abdominal ultrasound nonvisualized gallbladder triangular cord sign

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Pathology's back on the biopsy.

responders.narrative: Percutaneous liver biopsy: portal tract expansion with bile ductular proliferation and canalicular bile plugs, mild portal fibrosis — pattern favors biliary obstruction over neonatal hepatitis.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Peds GI/hepatology here — with pale stools and a conjugated bilirubin like that, we need an urgent ultrasound and a surgery consult. Let's not sit on this.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Peds surgery — if the ultrasound doesn't show a normal gallbladder, we'll take her to the OR for a cholangiogram and surgical correction if the ducts are obstructed. The earlier we operate the better her outcome, so let's move fast.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Bed's ready on the inpatient peds/GI service — transport's set if we need to transfer for surgery.

deterioration.trajectory: hemodynamically stable, diagnostic case — the risk is a missed or delayed diagnosis, not acute decompensation

stages.event: Diagnostic anchoring — family pushing for discharge
stages.nurse: Doctor, the family's ready to head home — their pediatrician can follow up the jaundice next week. What's the plan?
stages.feedback: Without fractionating the bilirubin, the workup drifts toward reassurance — the conjugated component goes unrecognized and the surgical window for a timely Kasai narrows.

debrief.goodOutcome: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia recognized and urgently worked up; GI/hepatology and surgery involved for timely Kasai evaluation.
debrief.badOutcome: Cholestatic jaundice was dismissed as breast-milk jaundice, delaying the workup and risking a missed surgical window and progression to biliary cirrhosis.
debrief.outcomeGood: You fractionated the bilirubin, recognized the conjugated elevation as pathologic, checked LFTs and coagulation studies, gave vitamin K, and got GI/hepatology and surgery involved urgently — preserving the window for a timely Kasai portoenterostomy.
debrief.outcomePoor: The bilirubin was never fractionated, so the conjugated elevation went unrecognized, the family was reassured it was breast-milk jaundice, and the visit ended without urgent GI/surgery referral — losing time in the narrow surgical window.
learningPoints: Any jaundice persisting beyond ~2 weeks of age must be fractionated — a conjugated (direct) elevation is always pathologic.
Pale, acholic stools plus prolonged jaundice point to biliary atresia — a time-critical surgical diagnosis.
Check LFTs and coagulation studies and give vitamin K — cholestasis impairs vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.

deterioration.trajectory: pediatric airway/esophageal emergency — alkali burns progress from stridor toward complete airway obstruction, with a secondary risk of esophageal or gastric perforation and mediastinitis if the airway is neglected or oral intake/decompression is forced

stages.event: Airway edema progressing without a secured airway
stages.nurse: Doctor, his stridor's a lot louder now and he's tugging at his neck to breathe — sats are dropping and I need direction.
stages.feedback: Alkali burns cause airway edema that worsens over the first hour — without early, controlled airway management he can obstruct completely.

stages.event: Fever and a firm, tender abdomen develop — possible esophageal or gastric perforation
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's spiked a fever and his belly's gotten firm and tender — something's changed and I need a plan now.
stages.feedback: Ongoing oral intake with no imaging to look for injury risks a missed perforation progressing silently to mediastinitis.

stages.event: Complete airway obstruction and cardiopulmonary arrest
stages.nurse: Doctor — he's not breathing, he's unresponsive!
stages.feedback: Without a controlled, prepared airway, the edema progressed to complete obstruction and arrest — every minute without airway control mattered.

debrief.goodOutcome: Airway secured early in a controlled, prepared fashion; harmful home-remedy actions avoided; the child kept NPO with fluids and analgesia; imaging obtained to exclude perforation; and GI, surgery, and toxicology all looped in — stable transfer to the PICU pending endoscopy.
debrief.badOutcome: The airway was never secured in a controlled fashion, and progressive edema led to complete airway obstruction and cardiopulmonary arrest.
debrief.outcomeGood: You anticipated a difficult airway and secured it with backup ready, avoided emesis, charcoal, neutralization, and a blind NG tube, kept him NPO with fluids and analgesia, imaged for perforation, and brought in GI, surgery, and toxicology — a well-run caustic ingestion resuscitation.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without early, controlled airway management, the airway edema progressed unchecked to complete obstruction.
learningPoints: Alkali ingestions cause deep liquefactive esophageal injury — protect the airway early and anticipate a difficult intubation.
Do not induce emesis, give charcoal, attempt neutralization/dilution, or blindly pass an NG tube — all of these worsen the injury.
Keep the patient NPO with IV fluids and analgesia while arranging imaging to evaluate for perforation or mediastinitis.
Early endoscopy (12-24h) grades the injury and guides further management; steroids and antibiotics are selective, specialist-guided decisions, not routine empiric therapy.
Involve toxicology/poison control and surgery early, and keep surgery in the loop for any sign of perforation.
missedOpportunities: Early, controlled airway management with backup equipment ready before edema progressed
Avoiding induced vomiting, activated charcoal, neutralization/dilution, or a blind NG tube
NPO status with IV fluids and analgesia
Upright chest imaging or CT to evaluate for perforation/mediastinitis
Early GI consultation for endoscopy to grade the injury
Surgery and toxicology/poison control involvement
Deferring steroids/antibiotics to specialist guidance rather than giving them empirically

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity case — an unconfirmed, untreated clot with rising intracranial pressure progresses toward herniation

stages.event: Increasing somnolence with recurrent vomiting and brief seizure-like twitching
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's more drowsy than before and just vomited again — are we doing anything else for her right now?
stages.feedback: Ongoing seizure activity and rising intracranial pressure were left unaddressed.

stages.nurse: Her pressure's climbing and her heart rate is dropping — she's barely rousable and not moving her right side. I need a plan right now.

stages.event: Herniation
