stages.feedback: Refractory adrenal crisis, never treated with hydrocortisone, progressed to cardiovascular collapse and arrest.

debrief.goodOutcome: Adrenal crisis recognized and treated with immediate hydrocortisone, isotonic fluids with dextrose, hyperkalemia management, and treatment of the precipitating infection.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized refractory hypotension in a steroid-dependent patient, gave hydrocortisone without waiting on labs, corrected the hypoglycemia and hyponatremia, treated the hyperkalemia, and identified the pyelonephritis that triggered the crisis — she stabilized and went to the ICU.
debrief.outcomePoor: Hydrocortisone was never given, so the shock never resolved despite fluids and pressors — she progressed to hyperkalemic arrhythmia and arrest.
debrief.badOutcome: Treating this as fluid-refractory sepsis alone and escalating pressors while omitting hydrocortisone left the underlying cortisol deficiency unaddressed, and she arrested.
learningPoints: In refractory shock in a steroid-dependent patient, give hydrocortisone before it resolves — don't wait for cortisol or ACTH results.
Classic labs: hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia.
Abrupt cessation of chronic steroids plus a stressor (illness, infection) precipitates crisis.
Fluids and pressors alone won't fix the hypotension without cortisol replacement.
Look for and treat the precipitant — here, a superimposed urinary infection — alongside the steroid replacement.
missedOpportunities: Immediate IV hydrocortisone
Isotonic fluids with dextrose for the hypoglycemia
Treatment of significant hyperkalemia
Identifying and treating the precipitating infection

debrief.goodOutcome: Thyroid storm recognized and treated with the full four-pronged regimen in the correct order; precipitant identified and treated.
debrief.outcomeGood: You started propranolol and a thionamide, followed with iodine only after the thionamide was on board, added stress-dose steroids, cooled her with acetaminophen while avoiding aspirin, resuscitated with fluids, and treated the underlying pneumonia — the storm broke.

learningPoints: Order matters: beta-blocker + thionamide first, then iodine at least an hour later, plus steroids.
PTU is preferred in storm because it also blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion.
Avoid aspirin for fever — salicylates displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins.
Always look for and treat the precipitant (commonly infection).

stages.event: Persistent hyperthermia with new seizure activity

debrief.goodOutcome: Exertional heat stroke recognized and rapidly cooled with core temperature monitoring; complications screened and treated.
debrief.outcomeGood: You measured core temperature, began immediate aggressive cooling with cold-water immersion, stopped cooling near target to avoid overshoot, resuscitated with IV fluids, avoided antipyretics, screened for and treated rhabdomyolysis/hyperkalemia/AKI/hypoglycemia, and admitted him for monitoring.
debrief.outcomePoor: Cooling was delayed or antipyretics were relied upon instead of active cooling, and complications went unrecognized.
debrief.badOutcome: Delayed cooling allowed hyperthermia to progress to seizure and, ultimately, a fatal arrhythmia from unrecognized hyperkalemia and rhabdomyolysis.
learningPoints: Cooling speed determines outcome — cold-water immersion is the most effective method.

stages.event: Ongoing exposure without active rewarming

stages.event: Myocardial irritability from persistent core cooling
stages.nurse: He just went out on the monitor — I need direction right now!
stages.feedback: A cold, irritable myocardium fibrillates without aggressive rewarming; severe or refractory cases need extracorporeal rewarming escalation.

stages.event: Refractory arrest without extracorporeal rewarming or appropriately paced resuscitation
stages.nurse: Still no pulse. Are we calling it?
stages.feedback: He is not dead until warm and dead — resuscitation should continue with escalating rewarming (including ECMO) rather than premature termination.

debrief.goodOutcome: Severe hypothermia recognized and reversed with gentle handling and aggressive rewarming; the patient survived to ICU admission.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized severe hypothermia and Osborn waves early, handled him gently, actively rewarmed with warm air, fluids, and oxygen, escalated to ECMO when needed, and worked up a precipitating cause — he survived to ICU care.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without gentle handling and aggressive rewarming, the cold myocardium turned irritable, degenerated into a lethal arrhythmia, and resuscitation efforts came too late.
debrief.badOutcome: Rough handling or delayed, inadequate rewarming let a cold, irritable heart fibrillate and arrest, and the patient died before being adequately rewarmed.
learningPoints: 'Not dead until warm and dead' — continue resuscitation and rewarming in hypothermic arrest.
Handle gently; the cold myocardium is irritable and prone to ventricular fibrillation.
In severe hypothermic arrest, limit shocks and drugs until the core temperature rises (~30°C).
Osborn (J) waves are a classic ECG finding; escalate to extracorporeal rewarming when severe.
Coagulation studies are run warmed to 37°C in the lab and can look falsely reassuring despite real clinical coagulopathy.
missedOpportunities: Handling the patient gently rather than jostling or rapidly repositioning him
Beginning active external and internal rewarming (forced warm air, warmed IV fluids, warm humidified oxygen) rather than passive coverage alone
Escalating to extracorporeal (ECMO) rewarming for this degree of hypothermia
Holding repeated defibrillation and vasoactive drugs until the core temperature approached 30°C
Checking glucose/electrolytes and screening for a precipitating cause (alcohol, sepsis, thyroid, trauma)

deterioration.trajectory: hypoactive delirium from an evolving urinary source — worsens toward septic shock without recognition, fluid resuscitation, and empiric antibiotics

stages.event: Early sepsis progression, still unrecognized

stages.event: Progression to septic shock and cardiovascular collapse

stages.feedback: Unrecognized, untreated urosepsis progressed to septic shock and arrest; the window for fluids and empiric antibiotics was missed.

debrief.goodOutcome: Delirium recognized as distinct from her baseline dementia; the underlying UTI was found and treated, and she was reoriented without unnecessary sedation.
debrief.outcomeGood: You obtained collateral history establishing an acute, fluctuating change from baseline, applied the CAM, and systematically worked up the cause — the UTI was found on urinalysis, treated with IV fluids and empiric antibiotics, and she was reoriented with nonpharmacologic measures instead of being sedated.
debrief.outcomePoor: The confusion was managed as a progression of her known dementia rather than investigated as a new problem, so the underlying infection went untreated.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on 'her dementia getting worse,' the urinary source of her delirium was missed and left untreated, risking progression toward septic shock and a lasting drop in her cognitive baseline.
learningPoints: Delirium is defined by acute onset and/or fluctuating disturbance in attention and cognition, distinct from the gradual, attention-preserved decline of dementia
Missed diagnosis rates for delirium in the ED run 54%-89%, often because it is misattributed to baseline dementia or psychiatric illness
Delirium is a syndrome, not a diagnosis in itself - it signals an underlying medical condition such as infection, hypotension, hypoxia, or a toxin/medication effect that must be found and treated
Delirium has hypoactive, hyperactive, and mixed psychomotor subtypes; the quiet, lethargic hypoactive form is the one most easily overlooked at the bedside
Delirium carries significant morbidity and mortality - identifying and treating the underlying cause is the emergency, not sedating the behavior
missedOpportunities: Obtaining collateral history from family/staff to establish the acute, fluctuating timeline
Applying a bedside delirium screen such as the CAM
Sending a urinalysis/urine culture to identify the infectious source
Starting IV fluids and empiric antibiotics for the UTI
Using nonpharmacologic reorientation instead of sedating medications

debrief.goodOutcome: Eleanor's private disclosure, exam findings, and the EMS/caregiver discrepancy were documented; APS was notified and social work built a safety plan before disposition.
debrief.badOutcome: Eleanor was treated as a routine mechanical fall and discharged back into her son's custody without further intervention.
debrief.outcomeGood: You separated Eleanor from her son, found injuries inconsistent with a single fall, documented the EMS-versus-caregiver discrepancy verbatim, obtained CT and coagulation studies given her laceration and apixaban, and reported to Adult Protective Services with social work building a safety plan before she went anywhere.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter closed without ever getting Eleanor alone, the mismatched fall story went undocumented, and she was discharged back to the same caregiver and situation that brought her in.
learningPoints: Elder abuse is underreported by at least two orders of magnitude — a fall may be the patient's only opportunity to be identified and helped
Physical abuse is often mistaken for accidental trauma or the normal skin fragility of aging, especially in patients on anticoagulants
Cognitive impairment and dependency on a caregiver are major risk factors, and spouses and adult children are the most common perpetrators
A history that shifts between EMS, caregiver, and patient, or a caregiver who answers for the patient, is itself a red flag
The single most important management step is making the diagnosis and reporting suspicion to Adult Protective Services
missedOpportunities: Interviewing Eleanor privately, away from her son
A full skin and body exam for occult injuries
Documenting the EMS-versus-caregiver discrepancy verbatim
Reporting suspected abuse to Adult Protective Services
Engaging social work for a multidisciplinary safety plan before disposition

stages.event: Anchoring on a resolved, 'minor' bleed
stages.nurse: Doctor, the family's asking why we're still watching a stool that already stopped — are we sending him home or what's the hold up?
stages.feedback: A resolved herald bleed in a patient with a prior aortic graft is not reassuring — this needs to be treated as a high-risk bleed early, before any imaging is even back.

stages.event: Deferred workup — no imaging, no surgical involvement
stages.nurse: He just told me he feels a wave of queasiness and looks grayer than he did an hour ago — are we getting imaging or a surgeon involved anytime soon?
stages.feedback: Without emergent CT angiography and simultaneous vascular and GI/general surgery consultation, the graft-enteric communication is never identified before the next, much larger bleed.

stages.event: Massive hematemesis — hemorrhagic shock
stages.nurse: He just vomited a huge volume of blood all at once and his pressure's crashing — I need help in this room right now!
stages.feedback: The herald bleed was the warning sign of the secondary hemorrhage to come; without large-bore access, airway readiness, and blood typed and crossed, Walter exsanguinated when the graft fully eroded into the duodenum.

debrief.goodOutcome: Secondary aortoenteric fistula recognized from the graft history and treated as a surgical emergency — resuscitated, imaged, and taken urgently to the OR before the catastrophic secondary hemorrhage.
debrief.badOutcome: The herald bleed was dismissed as a resolved, minor event; without recognizing the aortic graft as a red flag, Walter later hemorrhaged massively and could not be saved.
debrief.outcomeGood: You specifically asked about his prior aortic graft, treated the resolved bleed as a possible graft-enteric fistula despite reassuring vitals, secured large-bore access and prepped for airway compromise, obtained an emergent CT angiogram while consulting vascular surgery and general surgery/GI simultaneously, and typed and crossed blood in anticipation of a secondary hemorrhage — he went to the OR for graft excision and bowel repair before he could rebleed.
debrief.outcomePoor: The graft history was never specifically probed, the resolved bleed was treated as routine, and imaging or surgical consultation was delayed — Walter appeared stable until he suddenly hemorrhaged massively and could not be resuscitated.
learningPoints: Aortoenteric fistula must be considered in any GI bleed occurring in a patient with a history of aortic graft or endograft repair, no matter how minor the bleed looks.
A herald bleed is a self-limited warning hemorrhage that classically precedes a much larger, often fatal, secondary hemorrhage — reassuring current vitals do not exclude it.
A benign-appearing GI bleed with a history of aortic graft surgery is a distinct high-risk category, not equivalent to routine peptic ulcer or diverticular bleeding.
Standard upper GI bleed resuscitation — large-bore IV access, airway readiness, and transfusion preparation — proceeds in parallel with the urgent aortoenteric fistula workup, not after it.
Emergent CT angiography plus simultaneous vascular and GI/general surgery consultation is the correct next step; delay converts a survivable herald bleed into fatal exsanguination.

Treating the resolved bleed as a possible aortoenteric fistula rather than a routine GI bleed

Airway protection readiness with suction at bedside
Emergent CT angiography with simultaneous vascular surgery and GI/general surgery consultation
Type and crossmatch blood with massive transfusion readiness

diagnosticReports.title: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Sigmoid colon demonstrates multiple diverticula with circumferential wall thickening and adjacent fat stranding. A 5.2 cm rim-enhancing, thick-walled fluid collection is present in the pelvis abutting the thickened sigmoid loop, most consistent with a diverticular abscess. A tiny focus of extraluminal gas is seen adjacent to the collection, without large-volume free intraperitoneal air. A small amount of free fluid layers in the pelvis. Remaining bowel loops are unremarkable; no obstruction. IMPRESSION: Sigmoid diverticulitis with a 5.2 cm pelvic abscess and a focus of contained extraluminal gas concerning for a contained perforation; no large-volume free air identified.

stages.event: Tachycardia and fever persist despite initial evaluation
stages.nurse: Doctor, her heart rate's still sitting in the 120s and she says the pain's worse now than when she came in — are we doing anything for her yet?

stages.event: Tenderness spreading, peritoneal signs worsening
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's guarding across her whole lower belly now and flinching before I even touch her, and her pressure's drifting down too. What's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Without CT confirmation and a surgical consult, the abscess and contained perforation are progressing toward diffuse peritonitis unrecognized.

stages.event: Diffuse peritonitis and septic shock
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's barely rousable and I can hardly get a pressure — I need help in here now.
stages.feedback: The contained perforation progressed to free perforation with diffuse peritonitis and septic shock — without emergent surgical source control, she did not survive.

debrief.goodOutcome: Complicated diverticulitis recognized despite her reassurance; CT confirmed the abscess and contained perforation, IV fluids and antibiotics were started, surgery was consulted, and she was admitted for drainage and source control.
debrief.badOutcome: The anchoring 'same as last time' story was accepted at face value; without IV resuscitation, imaging, or surgical involvement, the contained perforation progressed to diffuse peritonitis and septic shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You didn't take her word that this was 'the same as last time' — reassessing her, getting the CT, starting IV fluids and antibiotics, consulting surgery, and admitting her for drainage caught complicated diverticulitis before it became a free perforation.
debrief.outcomePoor: Treating this as her prior uncomplicated flare, the case closed without IV resuscitation, imaging, or surgical involvement, and the contained perforation progressed toward diffuse peritonitis and septic shock.
learningPoints: Diverticulitis most commonly arises in the sigmoid (left) colon in Western patients, from perforation of a diverticulum through the colonic wall.

Uncomplicated diverticulitis in reliable, non-toxic patients can often be managed with oral or even no antibiotics as an outpatient — but that pathway depends entirely on first excluding complicated disease.

Complicated diverticulitis (abscess, free perforation, diffuse peritonitis, obstruction, or fistula) is a different disease requiring CT confirmation, IV antibiotics, and surgical consultation, not the outpatient oral-antibiotic pathway.

A presentation that 'feels like' a prior benign episode can mask silent progression to perforation; persistent tachycardia, spreading peritoneal signs, and an elevated lactate should never be dismissed just because the patient says it's the same as last time.
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast is the imaging modality of choice to distinguish uncomplicated from complicated diverticulitis and to guide whether percutaneous drainage or surgery is needed.
missedOpportunities: Reassessing with a full exam and vitals instead of accepting 'same as last time'
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
IV fluid resuscitation and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics
Emergent surgical consultation
Admission with IR drainage or operative source control

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity vascular emergency — deceptively benign early exam masks progressive bowel infarction; delay is fatal

stages.event: Continued unrecognized pain out of proportion to exam
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's still writhing in pain but her belly and labs look fine so far — are we sending her home or what's the hold-up?
stages.feedback: Pain out of proportion to a deceptively benign abdominal exam in an elderly, AFib, non-anticoagulated patient is mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise — the soft belly bought time that is now being spent.

stages.event: Bowel hypoperfusion progresses, uncharacterized
stages.nurse: Her heart rate's climbing and she's spiking a temperature now — I need a real plan for this patient.
stages.feedback: Without urgent CT angiography the SMA occlusion goes uncharacterized while ischemic bowel progresses toward infarction and perforation.

stages.event: Bowel infarction, perforation, and septic shock
stages.nurse: She's hypotensive and barely responsive — I need you at the bedside right now!
stages.feedback: Without antibiotics, anticoagulation, and emergent surgical revascularization, infarcted bowel perforates and septic shock follows — this stage is not survivable without the core resuscitation bundle.

debrief.goodOutcome: SMA embolus identified on CTA and treated with antibiotics, anticoagulation, and emergent vascular/general surgery revascularization.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized pain out of proportion to exam in an elderly, non-anticoagulated AFib patient, sent lactate and labs while resuscitating, started broad-spectrum antibiotics and heparin, obtained urgent CT angiography, and got vascular and general surgery to the bedside emergently — the SMA embolus was found and treated before infarction set in.
debrief.outcomePoor: The deceptively soft abdomen and early normal-appearing labs were taken as reassuring, so the SMA embolus was never worked up until the bowel infarcted, perforated, and she went into septic shock.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on a benign-feeling belly and unremarkable early labs, the mesenteric ischemia went unrecognized until bowel infarction and septic shock had already set in.
learningPoints: Pain out of proportion to exam in an elderly, AFib, or vascular-risk patient is mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise.
Early exam and labs can be deceptively normal — a normal lactate or soft abdomen does not exclude bowel ischemia.
CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis is the test of choice; involve vascular and general surgery emergently rather than waiting for confirmation to worsen.
Avoid vasopressors when possible — they worsen splanchnic perfusion and can push marginal bowel into infarction; prioritize volume, antibiotics, anticoagulation, and revascularization.
Mortality is very high — the diagnosis hinges on suspicion and speed, not on waiting for a dramatic exam.
missedOpportunities: Suspecting mesenteric ischemia despite a soft, benign-feeling abdomen
Sending lactate and resuscitating with IV fluids
Starting broad-spectrum antibiotics
Starting heparin anticoagulation
Obtaining urgent CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis
Consulting vascular and general surgery emergently

debrief.goodOutcome: Perforated peptic ulcer recognized as a surgical emergency, resuscitated, decompressed, covered with a PPI and broad-spectrum antibiotics, imaged, and taken to the OR.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the rigid abdomen as peritonitis, resuscitated with fluids, placed an NG tube, gave a PPI and broad-spectrum antibiotics, confirmed free air on imaging, and got surgery to the bedside for emergent repair.
debrief.outcomePoor: The rigid abdomen wasn't acted on quickly enough — resuscitation, antibiotics, imaging, or the surgical consult were delayed, and the perforation progressed to generalized peritonitis and septic shock.
debrief.badOutcome: Delay in recognizing the surgical abdomen and calling surgery allowed the perforation to progress to generalized peritonitis and fatal septic shock.
learningPoints: A rigid, board-like abdomen with guarding and rebound is peritonitis until proven otherwise — resuscitate and call surgery immediately.

speech.text: Correct call — she's ruptured and unstable, so this is surgical, not medical management. Methotrexate isn't appropriate here.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity hemorrhagic shock — ongoing intraperitoneal bleeding from a ruptured ectopic leads to exsanguination unless she is resuscitated with blood products and taken emergently to the OR

stages.event: Ongoing hemorrhage — pressure crashing
stages.nurse: Her pressure just dropped into the 60s and she's more tachycardic — nothing I'm hanging is keeping up. What do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Ongoing hemorrhage is outpacing crystalloid alone — she needs large-bore access and blood typed and crossed now.

stages.event: Refractory shock — inadequate resuscitation
stages.nurse: She's barely rousable and the pressure won't come up no matter what I run — I need a decision right now.
stages.feedback: Crystalloid can't keep pace with ongoing intra-abdominal hemorrhage; blood products are the definitive resuscitation while surgery is arranged.

stages.event: Exsanguination and cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: I've lost her pulse — she's not breathing, starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Only emergent operative control of the bleeding source stops this — without OB/GYN and the OR, delay past this point is fatal.

debrief.goodOutcome: Ruptured ectopic recognized early with a positive pregnancy test and free fluid on bedside ultrasound; she was resuscitated with blood products and taken emergently to the OR.
debrief.badOutcome: Recognition was delayed — the diagnosis was missed or the case anchored on a benign gynecologic or urinary cause, and she exsanguinated before reaching the OR.
debrief.outcomeGood: You checked a pregnancy test, got large-bore IV access and type and crossmatch, confirmed free fluid with no intrauterine pregnancy on bedside ultrasound, resuscitated with blood products, and got OB/GYN to the OR without waiting on a quantitative hCG or formal imaging — while correctly avoiding methotrexate in an unstable, ruptured ectopic.
debrief.outcomePoor: Either the pregnancy test was never sent, the diagnosis anchored on another cause, or the trip to the OR was delayed waiting on labs or formal imaging — she progressed to hemorrhagic shock and cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: Check a pregnancy test in every reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain or syncope.
Free pelvic fluid with no intrauterine pregnancy on bedside ultrasound is a ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.
An unstable ruptured ectopic goes to the OR — don't wait for a quantitative beta-hCG or formal radiology imaging.
Resuscitate hemorrhagic shock with blood products, not crystalloid alone, and get OB/GYN involved emergently.
Methotrexate is for stable, unruptured ectopics only — it is contraindicated once she is unstable or ruptured.
missedOpportunities: Sending a urine or serum pregnancy test
Bedside ultrasound looking for free fluid and an intrauterine pregnancy
Type and crossmatch with early blood product transfusion
Emergent OB/GYN consultation and expedited transfer to the OR

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: (weak, retching) It just... came up all at once, so much blood... I've been passing black tarry stools all day too.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was diagnosed with cirrhosis about three years ago. He's had a scope before that showed varices — they banded a couple of them last year.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was supposed to be on a beta-blocker for the varices but he ran out of it a while back and never refilled it. No blood thinners, no allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He drinks every day, has for years — vodka mostly, more than half a bottle some days. We've tried to get him to stop.

physicalExam.finding: Pale, diaphoretic, and visibly jaundiced. Retching intermittently into a basin. Drowsy but arousable to voice, oriented to person only.

Markedly distended with a positive fluid wave and shifting dullness consistent with ascites. Caput medusae visible around the umbilicus. Spleen tip palpable below the left costal margin. Mild diffuse tenderness, no rebound or guarding.
Scleral icterus and diffuse jaundice. Multiple spider angiomata across the chest and upper arms. Palmar erythema bilaterally. Mucous membranes dry and pale.
Black, tarry, foul-smelling melena on the glove. No bright red blood per rectum, no external hemorrhoids or masses.
Drowsy and slow to respond, arousable, oriented to person only. Mild flapping tremor (asterixis) with arms outstretched. No focal motor or sensory deficits.
Tachycardic, regular. Thready peripheral pulses with delayed capillary refill (~4 seconds). No murmurs appreciated. Cool, clammy extremities.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Type and cross sent stat, blood bank notified.

labResults.name: Blood Type
labResults.value: O positive; 4 units PRBC crossmatched, O-negative available immediately

image.query: sinus tachycardia 12-lead ecg no ischemic changes
portable supine chest x-ray no infiltrate no aspiration

diagnosticReports.title: Abdominal Ultrasound
diagnosticReports.body: Liver demonstrates coarsened, nodular echotexture with irregular surface contour, splenomegaly measuring 15 cm, and moderate perihepatic and pelvic free fluid. IMPRESSION: Cirrhotic liver morphology with splenomegaly and ascites, compatible with portal hypertension.

image.query: cirrhotic liver nodular contour ascites splenomegaly abdominal ultrasound

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: He's actively hemorrhaging and too unstable for the scanner — if this is variceal bleeding, the answer is an emergent scope, not a trip to CT. Every minute down there is a minute he's bleeding.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Two 16-gauge IVs in, one in each AC. Fluids running, ready to give blood.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Transfusing PRBCs now, targeting a restrictive hemoglobin around 7 — not chasing normal.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Transfusion units/target not specified — restrictive strategy, transfuse PRBCs to a target hemoglobin of ~7 g/dL.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — chasing a normal hemoglobin here raises his portal pressure and drives rebleeding. Stay restrictive, target around 7, not higher.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: FFP and platelets going in for the coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Blood product dose not specified — FFP 2 units and/or platelets per coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia severity.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Ease off the crystalloid — flooding him with saline raises portal pressure and dilutes what little clotting factor he has left. He needs blood, not saline.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Octreotide bolus given and the drip is running — should cut down the splanchnic flow.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Octreotide dose not specified — 50 mcg IV bolus followed by 50 mcg/hr infusion.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ceftriaxone 1 gram IV given — prophylaxis for the cirrhosis.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Ceftriaxone dose not specified — 1 g IV daily for cirrhotic GI bleed prophylaxis.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pantoprazole bolus and infusion started.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: PPI dose not specified — pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus followed by 8 mg/hr infusion (or 40 mg IV twice daily).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's intubated for airway protection — sat's coming up, no more soiling with the next emesis.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: GI here, on my way with the scope. Keep the blood and octreotide running — we'll band whatever's bleeding.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: GI hasn't been called yet — I can't get him to the endoscopy suite without a consultant to do the scope.

responders.narrative: GI performs bedside-to-suite emergent upper endoscopy: large esophageal varices with active bleeding from one column are identified and controlled with band ligation.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Found the bleeding varix and banded it — flow's stopped. He tolerated the scope well.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Placing the balloon tamponade as a bridge — it's tamponading the bleed until we can get definitive endoscopic or TIPS control.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — you're treating this as a variceal hemorrhage from portal hypertension. What do you want first?

any: admit to the intensive care unit

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU has the bed. He's stabilized on the drip and going up now.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity hemorrhagic shock from bleeding esophageal varices — ongoing hematemesis, aspiration risk, and exsanguination unless resuscitation, vasoactive/antibiotic therapy, and emergent endoscopic control are delivered

stages.event: Ongoing hematemesis — pressure crashing
stages.nurse: He just brought up another huge volume of blood and his pressure tanked into the 60s — I need orders now.
stages.feedback: Ongoing massive hematemesis in a hypotensive cirrhotic needs large-bore access and blood products right away — crystalloid alone won't keep up with the loss.

stages.event: Airway soiling — aspiration
stages.nurse: He's vomiting again and coughing on it — his sat just dropped into the 70s and he's barely rousable.
stages.feedback: Massive hematemesis with a depressed mental status needs the airway secured before the next emesis is aspirated.

stages.event: Exsanguination — refractory hemorrhage
stages.nurse: I've lost his pulse — he's not breathing, starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Medical therapy alone doesn't stop a bleeding varix — without emergent endoscopic banding (or rescue tamponade/TIPS), the hemorrhage continued until he exsanguinated.

debrief.goodOutcome: Bleeding esophageal varices recognized and controlled — resuscitated with restrictive blood products, octreotide, and antibiotics, then treated definitively with emergent endoscopic band ligation.
debrief.badOutcome: Under-resuscitation, an unprotected airway, or delayed endoscopy let the variceal hemorrhage continue until the patient exsanguinated.
debrief.outcomeGood: You placed large-bore IVs and crossmatched blood, transfused under a restrictive strategy while correcting the coagulopathy, started octreotide and prophylactic ceftriaxone, added a PPI, protected the airway, and got GI to band the bleeding varices emergently — he stabilized and went to the ICU.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter leaned on crystalloid and over-transfusion instead of restrictive blood products and vasoactive/antibiotic therapy, the airway was never secured, and endoscopy was delayed — the hemorrhage continued until he arrested.
learningPoints: In cirrhotic GI bleeds, prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) reduce mortality — don't forget them.
Use a restrictive transfusion target (~7 g/dL); over-transfusion and large-volume crystalloid raise portal pressure and worsen rebleeding.
Octreotide reduces splanchnic/portal flow; endoscopy provides definitive band ligation, with balloon tamponade or TIPS as rescue.
Protect the airway early in massive hematemesis with altered mental status to prevent aspiration.
An elevated BUN out of proportion to creatinine is a classic clue to a large upper GI blood load.
missedOpportunities: Two large-bore IVs with type and crossmatch
Restrictive transfusion strategy with correction of coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia
Octreotide to reduce splanchnic/portal flow
Prophylactic ceftriaxone
Airway protection in massive hematemesis with altered mental status
Emergent endoscopy for band ligation, with tamponade/TIPS as rescue

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It started about eight hours ago in my left side, shooting down toward my groin. I've had fevers and shaking chills the last two days too, and it burns when I go — I figured it was just another bladder infection.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've had two kidney stones before, both passed on their own. And I get bladder infections a lot — probably four or five this year.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Just a multivitamin and something for my blood pressure. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She lives alone and barely drinks any water, honestly. No family history of anything like this that I know of. She doesn't smoke or drink.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've been queasy and haven't wanted to eat since yesterday, threw up once. No discharge, my bowels are normal.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable, restless, shifting position on the stretcher and unable to find a comfortable position. Diaphoretic, flushed. Non-toxic but clearly unwell.
Marked left costovertebral-angle tenderness to percussion. Abdomen soft, mild left lower quadrant tenderness without rebound or guarding, no palpable mass. Bowel sounds present.
Tachycardic, warm and well-perfused peripherally, capillary refill under 2 seconds. No pulsatile abdominal mass, femoral pulses 2+ and symmetric bilaterally.
No adnexal tenderness or masses, no cervical motion tenderness, no vaginal discharge. External genitalia unremarkable.
Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Anxious and uncomfortable but appropriate, following commands normally.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Lactate's back at 2.8.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Urine culture and two sets of blood cultures drawn and sent before we start anything.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Abdomen and Pelvis, Noncontrast
diagnosticReports.body: FINDINGS: Moderate-to-severe left hydronephrosis and hydroureter down to a 7 mm calculus impacted at the distal left ureter. Perinephric and periureteral fat stranding present. Right kidney and ureter unremarkable. No free air, no drainable perinephric abscess, no aortic aneurysm or dissection. Appendix normal. IMPRESSION: Obstructing left distal ureteral stone with moderate-to-severe hydronephrosis and periureteral inflammatory changes.

image.query: obstructing distal ureteral stone moderate hydronephrosis perinephric stranding noncontrast CT axial

diagnosticReports.title: Renal and Bladder Ultrasound
diagnosticReports.body: Left kidney shows moderate-to-severe hydronephrosis with dilated calyces and echogenic debris/layering within the collecting system; right kidney is normal. IMPRESSION: Left hydronephrosis with internal debris concerning for infected obstructed system (pyonephrosis) - urologic emergency.

image.query: left hydronephrosis dilated renal pelvis and calyces right kidney normal grayscale ultrasound

any: is this just a kidney stone
worried this is more than a stone

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her temp's still up at 39.3 and her heart rate never came down after the morphine — I don't love that.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: No pulsatile mass, no peritoneal signs, no adnexal tenderness — AAA, appendicitis, and ovarian torsion all look unlikely so far.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Analgesic dose/route not specified — e.g., morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV plus ketorolac 15-30 mg IV.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Morphine and ketorolac given together — she's settling, says the pain's down to a 4.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Hold on — no cultures have gone yet on this patient.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics hanging now — cultures were already sent.

any: aggressive iv fluids to pass the stone
iv hydration to pass the stone
push fluids to pass the stone

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Aggressive hydration won't push an obstructing stone past a swollen, infected ureter — pumping more fluid behind a blocked, pus-filled kidney doesn't fix this. She needs decompression, not a bigger bolus.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Urology here — an infected, obstructed kidney doesn't clear on antibiotics alone. We're taking her back for a stent or a nephrostomy tonight.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Admitting order's in — urology's aware and she's headed up with antibiotics running.

deterioration.trajectory: infected, obstructed collecting system — worsens toward urosepsis without prompt antibiotics and emergent urologic decompression

stages.event: Fever persists, tachycardia unresponsive to analgesia
stages.nurse: She's spiking again and her pressure's drifting down since we got here — what's the plan?
stages.feedback: Fever with persistent tachycardia despite adequate analgesia should raise concern for an infected, obstructed system — cultures and broad-spectrum antibiotics are time-critical here.

stages.event: Progression toward septic shock from the unrelieved obstruction
stages.nurse: Her pressure just dropped again and she's getting confused — I need you at the bedside now.
stages.feedback: An infected, obstructed kidney will not improve on antibiotics alone; without emergent urologic decompression the obstruction drives ongoing sepsis.

stages.event: Refractory septic shock and cardiovascular collapse from the unrelieved infected, obstructed kidney
stages.nurse: She's lost her pulse — she's arrested.
stages.feedback: The obstructed, infected kidney was never decompressed and antibiotics were never started — untreated pyonephrosis progressed to fatal septic shock.

debrief.goodOutcome: Infected, obstructed collecting system recognized and treated; decompressed by urology and admitted on antibiotics.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized that fever and persistent tachycardia meant more than routine colic, obtained the noncontrast CT, sent cultures before starting broad-spectrum antibiotics, controlled pain with combined opioid/NSAID therapy, and got urology to the bedside for emergent decompression — the urologic emergency was caught and treated.
debrief.outcomePoor: The fever and tachycardia were treated as routine renal colic, antibiotics and urologic decompression were delayed, and the infected, obstructed system progressed toward septic shock.
debrief.badOutcome: Managed like uncomplicated renal colic, the infected obstructed kidney was never decompressed and she deteriorated into septic shock.
learningPoints: Fever with an obstructing ureteral stone signals an infected, obstructed collecting system (pyonephrosis) — a true urologic emergency, not routine renal colic.
Noncontrast CT is the imaging modality of choice for suspected urinary stones.
Draw urine and blood cultures before starting broad-spectrum IV antibiotics — antibiotics alone cannot adequately penetrate an obstructed, purulent collecting system.
An infected, obstructed kidney needs emergent urologic decompression (stent or percutaneous nephrostomy); it will not clear on antibiotics alone.
Combine opioids with NSAIDs for analgesia — aggressive IV hydration has not been shown to speed stone passage and is not a substitute for source control.
missedOpportunities: Emergent noncontrast CT abdomen/pelvis to confirm the obstructing stone and grade hydronephrosis
Urine and blood cultures sent before starting broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
Emergent urology consultation for decompression rather than outpatient stone management
Combined opioid plus NSAID analgesia instead of relying on aggressive IV hydration

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It came on all of a sudden a few hours ago — this awful stabbing pain low on my right side that comes in these unbearable waves. It eases a little between them but never really goes away. I've thrown up twice.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I don't have any medical problems. I do have a cyst on my right ovary — my doctor's been watching it for a few months.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm not on any medications, and I don't take anything for the cyst. No allergies.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Not that I know of — nobody in my family's had ovarian problems. My mom had a cyst once too, I think, but nothing serious.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My last period started about two weeks ago and was normal. My cycles are regular, every 28 days or so, so I think I'm right around when I'd be ovulating.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I have a boyfriend, we're sexually active. We use condoms most of the time, not always. I'm not on birth control.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable and pale, doubled over intermittently with waves of severe pain, retching into an emesis basin between waves. Tachycardic-appearing and mildly diaphoretic, non-toxic.

Suprapubic and right lower quadrant tenderness with voluntary guarding during a pain wave; no rebound or rigidity. A vague, tender fullness is palpable in the right lower quadrant. Bowel sounds present.

Bimanual exam reveals marked right adnexal tenderness with a palpable, tender adnexal fullness; left adnexa non-tender. Cervix non-tender to motion, os closed, no active bleeding or abnormal discharge.
No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally; flanks non-tender to palpation and percussion.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Urine pregnancy test is back.

responders.narrative: Pelvic ultrasound with Doppler performed, transabdominal and transvaginal views obtained.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Radiology's on the phone — the right ovary is enlarged with a whirlpool sign of the twisted pedicle, but they're seeing some arterial and venous flow on Doppler.

diagnosticReports.title: Pelvic Ultrasound with Doppler
diagnosticReports.body: Right ovary is enlarged to 5.2 cm with peripherally displaced follicles and edematous stroma; twisted, coiled appearance of the vascular pedicle is seen with absent intraovarian arterial and venous Doppler flow. IMPRESSION: Findings compatible with right ovarian torsion.

image.query: enlarged right ovary whirlpool sign twisted vascular pedicle transvaginal doppler ultrasound

diagnosticReports.title: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: FINDINGS: Right ovary is enlarged and edematous with peripheral follicles, measuring approximately 6 cm, displaced anteriorly and medially, with mild surrounding free fluid in the pelvis. Appendix is normal in caliber without wall thickening or fat stranding. Kidneys and ureters are unremarkable without hydronephrosis or calculus. IMPRESSION: Enlarged, edematous right ovary with adjacent free fluid, nonspecific but concerning for adnexal pathology; appendix and urinary tract are normal.

image.query: enlarged edematous right ovary free fluid pelvis axial CT

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fentanyl given — she says the pain's easier to ride out between waves.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Analgesic ordered without a dose — specify (e.g., fentanyl 50-100 mcg IV or morphine 4 mg IV).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ondansetron given IV — she says her stomach's settling and the retching's stopped.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Antiemetic ordered without a dose — specify (e.g., ondansetron 4 mg IV).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: IV's in, she's made NPO in case she goes to the OR, and a type and screen is sent.

any: doppler flow doesn't rule out torsion
normal flow does not exclude torsion
still concerned for torsion despite normal doppler
don't be reassured by the doppler

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Copy — noted, you're not letting the flow on ultrasound talk you out of it. I'll let GYN know you still want them at bedside.

any: high suspicion for torsion over appendicitis

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — you're keeping torsion at the top of the list instead of settling on the known cyst, appendicitis, or a stone.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: GYN here — with this exam and the whirlpool sign, I agree, time matters for the ovary. We're booking the OR now for laparoscopic detorsion.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: General surgery here — her appendix looks fine and this exam doesn't fit appendicitis. This really sounds gynecologic; get GYN involved.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Doctor, GYN hasn't seen her yet — I can't book the OR without a surgical consult on board.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: OR is ready and GYN is scrubbing in — we're taking her up right now for detorsion.

any: doppler is normal so no torsion

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — preserved flow on Doppler does not exclude torsion; the ovary has a dual blood supply. Sending her home on that alone risks losing the ovary.

deterioration.trajectory: surgical urgency — intermittent torsion with a compromised but not yet infarcted ovary; delay progresses toward irreversible ischemic necrosis and loss of the ovary, not immediate hemodynamic collapse

stages.event: Uncontrolled pain and ongoing vomiting
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's still doubled over and vomiting into the basin every few minutes — are we doing anything for her pain?
stages.feedback: Severe pain and vomiting were left unaddressed; analgesia and antiemetics should be given early while the workup proceeds.

stages.event: Diagnosis still uncharacterized, pain persisting in waves
stages.nurse: Doctor, the pain keeps coming back in these waves and we still don't have imaging back — what's the hold-up?
stages.feedback: Without a pelvic ultrasound with Doppler the adnexa goes uncharacterized while the twisted ovary continues losing blood supply.

stages.event: Progression toward ovarian ischemia
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's spiking a fever now and the pain is unrelenting — I need a real plan, not more tests!
stages.feedback: Every hour without emergent gynecologic surgery for detorsion increases the risk of irreversible ovarian necrosis and loss of the ovary.

debrief.goodOutcome: Ovarian torsion recognized despite preserved Doppler flow, and the ovary detorsed emergently by gynecology before it infarcted.
debrief.badOutcome: Reassured by preserved Doppler flow or anchored on the known ovarian cyst, appendicitis, or renal colic, the torsed ovary was never detorsed emergently and progressed to ischemic necrosis, costing her the ovary and her fertility on that side.
debrief.outcomeGood: You obtained a pregnancy test, treated her pain and nausea, got a pelvic ultrasound with Doppler, recognized that the preserved flow did not exclude torsion, avoided anchoring on the known cyst or other diagnoses, and got gynecology to the bedside emergently for laparoscopic ovary-sparing detorsion before the ovary lost its blood supply for good.
debrief.outcomePoor: The preserved Doppler flow or the known ovarian cyst was taken as reassuring, so gynecology was never consulted emergently and the torsed ovary progressed to necrosis.
learningPoints: Ovarian torsion can have preserved arterial and venous Doppler flow because the ovary has a dual blood supply (ovarian and uterine arteries) — normal flow does NOT rule it out.
Sudden severe, often intermittent, unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting and an adnexal mass or known cyst should raise suspicion for torsion, especially around ovulation or with a cyst.
Diagnosis is clinical — don't let a falsely reassuring ultrasound or anchoring on a known cyst, appendicitis, or renal colic delay a time-critical gynecologic surgical consult.
Emergent laparoscopic detorsion (ovary-sparing where possible) is the definitive treatment and the only way to salvage the ovary and preserve fertility.
Always send a pregnancy test in a reproductive-age woman with pelvic pain.
missedOpportunities: Obtaining a pregnancy test
Treating pain and nausea promptly
Pelvic ultrasound with Doppler
Recognizing that preserved Doppler flow does not exclude torsion
Emergent gynecology consultation for laparoscopic detorsion
Avoiding anchoring on the known ovarian cyst, appendicitis, or renal colic
Expediting surgery once clinical suspicion was high

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It woke me straight out of sleep about two hours ago — sudden, awful pain in my left testicle. I've thrown up twice since it started.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No real medical problems. This has actually happened before — a couple of times over the last year I've had sudden pain down there for a few minutes that went away on its own. Never like this though.

speech.speaker: patient

speech.speaker: patient

speech.speaker: patient

physicalExam.finding: Pale, diaphoretic, anxious adolescent male lying still and guarding his groin, wincing with any movement. Afebrile. Vomited once more in triage.
Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses, no rebound. Inguinal canals intact bilaterally, no hernia, no inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Left hemiscrotum swollen and erythematous. The left testis is firm, exquisitely tender to any palpation, and riding HIGH in the scrotum with a horizontal (transverse) lie. Right testis normal size, non-tender, normal vertical lie. No skin lesion.
Cremasteric reflex is ABSENT on the left (no elevation of the left testis with stroking of the ipsilateral inner thigh). Present and normal on the right.
Elevating the left testicle does NOT relieve the pain (Prehn's sign negative/unreliable) — findings are not reassuring for epididymitis.

stages.event: Persistent ischemic pain, no surgical plan in motion
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's still doubled over and just vomited again — what's the plan for him?
stages.feedback: A sudden, severely painful, high-riding testis is a time-critical surgical emergency — every minute without an urgent urology call narrows the window to save it.

stages.event: Salvage window closing
stages.nurse: Doctor, it's been a while now and he's still in agony — is anyone else getting involved in his care?
stages.feedback: Testicular salvage falls sharply after about six hours from symptom onset; without an emergent urology consult for surgical exploration, the testis is progressing toward infarction and becoming unsalvageable.

debrief.goodOutcome: Testicular torsion recognized clinically, analgesia given, and urology consulted emergently for surgical exploration and detorsion — the testis salvaged.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized torsion from the sudden onset, high-riding testis, and absent cremasteric reflex, treated the pain, and got urology to the bedside emergently without letting imaging delay the OR — he went back for exploration and detorsion in time.
debrief.outcomePoor: Recognition or the emergent urology consult was delayed, or the picture was worked up and treated as epididymitis instead.
debrief.badOutcome: Diagnosis or the urology consult was delayed past the salvage window, and the ischemic testis progressed to infarction.
learningPoints: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis — sudden severe pain, a high-riding transverse testis, and an absent cremasteric reflex are enough to call urology emergently.
Don't let imaging delay the OR: obtain a Doppler ultrasound only if it will not postpone surgical exploration.
Manual (open-book) detorsion is a reasonable temporizing bridge if urology is delayed, but it does not replace formal surgical exploration.
Testicular salvage falls sharply after about six hours from symptom onset — recognize the time-critical window.
Don't anchor on epididymitis — a normal cremasteric reflex would be reassuring, but its absence plus sudden onset should prompt urgent surgical evaluation, not empiric antibiotics.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing testicular torsion clinically from the exam findings
Emergent urology consultation for surgical exploration
Analgesia for the patient's pain
Not letting Doppler ultrasound delay the urology consult or OR

debrief.goodOutcome: Acute chest syndrome recognized and treated — hypoxia corrected, antibiotics with atypical coverage given, incentive spirometry encouraged, fluids kept judicious, and transfusion arranged with hematology on board.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized acute chest syndrome, corrected his hypoxia, dosed analgesia carefully to avoid hypoventilation, encouraged incentive spirometry, covered typical and atypical organisms, kept fluids judicious, and arranged transfusion with hematology involved — a leading cause of sickle cell mortality, controlled.
debrief.outcomePoor: Hypoxia and the underlying process went unaddressed, and acute chest syndrome progressed to respiratory failure and death.
debrief.badOutcome: Treated as a routine pneumonia or pain crisis, oxygenation was never corrected and transfusion was never arranged — acute chest syndrome progressed to respiratory failure.
learningPoints: Acute chest syndrome = new infiltrate plus respiratory symptoms in sickle cell disease — a leading cause of death.
Cover atypical organisms; incentive spirometry helps prevent progression.
Exchange transfusion is indicated for severe or rapidly progressive disease.
Avoid over-hydration and opioid-induced hypoventilation, which worsen it.
missedOpportunities: Supplemental oxygen to correct hypoxia
Broad-spectrum antibiotics with atypical coverage (cephalosporin + macrolide)
Transfusion (simple or exchange) with hematology involvement
Avoiding fluid overload and opioid over-sedation

deterioration.trajectory: expanding cerebral infarct from untreated sickle cerebral vasculopathy — progresses to herniation without emergent recognition, imaging, and exchange transfusion

stages.event: Team continues routine pain-crisis orders; the neuro exam hasn't been reassessed since triage.
stages.nurse: Doctor, mom's asking when his next pain dose is due — are we doing anything different than his usual crisis visits, or just working through the same plan?
stages.feedback: Without recognizing the facial droop and unilateral weakness as new focal deficits rather than sedation, the stroke evaluation never starts and time is lost.

stages.event: Right-sided weakness is more pronounced on nursing reassessment and Marcus is harder to rouse.
stages.nurse: His grip on the right is weaker than when we got him settled, and he's drowsier than before — charge is asking me what the plan is.
stages.feedback: Delaying the non-contrast head CT delays every downstream stroke-directed action, including the emergent exchange transfusion, while the infarct territory continues to expand.

stages.event: Marcus becomes unresponsive with irregular, gasping respirations as cerebral edema from the expanding infarct raises his intracranial pressure.
stages.nurse: He's not responding to my voice and his breathing just changed completely — I need you at the bedside right now.
stages.feedback: Without emergent exchange transfusion to bring HbS below 30% and halt the vaso-occlusive process in the cerebral vessels, the infarct expanded, precipitating rising intracranial pressure, herniation, and death.

debrief.goodOutcome: New stroke recognized emergently in a child with sickle cell disease; head CT excluded hemorrhage, exchange transfusion delivered, neurology involved, and Marcus stabilized without progression of his deficit.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the new focal neurologic deficit as stroke rather than sedation, obtained an emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage, activated hematology for emergent exchange transfusion targeting HbS below 30%, and involved neurology for MRI/MRA — Marcus stabilized with no further progression of his weakness.
debrief.outcomePoor: The facial droop and right-sided weakness were treated as part of the usual pain crisis and sedation from his home opioid. Without an urgent head CT and emergent exchange transfusion, the infarct expanded and Marcus herniated.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on 'another pain crisis' and a cold, the new stroke went unrecognized, imaging and exchange transfusion were delayed, and the infarct progressed to herniation.
learningPoints: New neurologic symptoms in a patient with sickle cell disease are stroke until proven otherwise, not just fatigue from a pain crisis.
Stroke in SCD arises from a distinct large-vessel intracranial vasculopathy, different from the microvascular vaso-occlusion that causes ordinary pain crises.
The definitive emergency treatment for acute stroke in SCD is exchange transfusion to reduce HbS below 30%, not a routine simple transfusion.
Hemoglobin electrophoresis cannot reliably confirm SCD genotype if the patient has been transfused within the prior 90 days.
Optimal stroke management strategies in SCD are age-dependent, so care must be tailored rather than applying one protocol to every age group.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing the new focal neurologic deficit as stroke rather than sedation or fatigue
Emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage
STAT CBC, reticulocyte count, type & crossmatch, and confirming no transfusion within the last 90 days
Emergent hematology/blood bank activation for exchange transfusion targeting HbS below 30%
Neurology consultation and MRI/MRA once stabilized
Avoiding additional sedation, hypoxia, and hypotension that could worsen cerebral ischemia

deterioration.trajectory: acute microangiopathic hemolysis with thrombocytopenia — progresses to widespread microthrombosis (stroke, cardiac ischemia, death) without urgent plasma exchange

stages.event: Anchoring on isolated thrombocytopenia — hemolysis workup not pursued

stages.event: Progressive microthrombosis — new focal neurologic deficit

debrief.goodOutcome: TTP recognized and treated with urgent plasma exchange and corticosteroids; platelet transfusion avoided.
debrief.badOutcome: Plasma exchange was delayed or platelets were transfused, and the untreated microangiopathy progressed to stroke or fatal cardiac ischemia.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with thrombocytopenia, confirmed hemolysis with a normal coagulation profile, sent ADAMTS13, arranged urgent plasma exchange with hematology, started corticosteroids, and avoided platelet transfusion — she stabilized and went to the ICU for ongoing exchange.
debrief.outcomePoor: The MAHA-plus-thrombocytopenia picture wasn't acted on quickly enough, or platelets were transfused for the low count — the untreated microthrombosis progressed to neurologic and cardiac injury.
learningPoints: MAHA + thrombocytopenia without another cause = treat as TTP; the classic pentad is usually incomplete.
Urgent plasma exchange is life-saving — involve hematology immediately.

Normal PT/PTT distinguishes TTP from DIC.
missedOpportunities: Confirming hemolysis with LDH, haptoglobin, and a normal coagulation profile
Sending ADAMTS13 activity
Arranging urgent plasma exchange with hematology
Avoiding platelet transfusion

debrief.goodOutcome: Silent hypoxemia recognized despite a reassuring, well-appearing patient; isolated and treated as COVID-19 pneumonia despite a negative initial swab, escalated promptly to helmet CPAP, and admitted for his oxygenation rather than his comfort.
debrief.badOutcome: His calm demeanor and a negative rapid swab were trusted over the monitor; escalation was delayed and he progressed toward severe hypoxic respiratory failure.
debrief.outcomeGood: You trusted the repeat 84 percent reading over his reassuring affect, isolated him and treated as COVID-19 pneumonia despite the initial negative swab, obtained a lung ultrasound showing diffuse B-lines, escalated to helmet CPAP when low-flow oxygen wasn't enough, and admitted him based on the severity of his hypoxia — the commonly-missed diagnosis, caught before he crashed.
debrief.outcomePoor: The low pulse oximetry reading was dismissed as probe error or anxiety because he looked and felt well, escalation beyond low-flow oxygen never happened, and his silent hypoxemia progressed toward respiratory failure.
learningPoints: COVID-19 pneumonia can cause "silent" or "happy" hypoxemia — patients often do not feel dyspneic until they are profoundly hypoxic, so pulse oximetry, not patient-reported breathlessness, should drive triage and escalation
A single negative nasal swab does not rule out COVID-19: if the clinical picture fits, isolate and treat as COVID-19 pneumonia and repeat testing in 3 days
Lung ultrasound is more sensitive than chest x-ray for COVID-19 pneumonia, and a diffuse B-line pattern correlates with a good response to PEEP
Most hypoxic COVID-19 patients are PEEP responders — helmet CPAP/noninvasive ventilation can safely buy time and bridge patients to an ICU bed, with intubation reserved for NIV failure
During a COVID-19 surge, admission decisions should be driven primarily by the degree of hypoxia, not by how unwell the patient feels or appears

debrief.goodOutcome: Fournier's gangrene recognized early; broad-spectrum antibiotics and fluid resuscitation started without delay, emergent urology/surgery consulted, and the patient taken to the OR for debridement without waiting on imaging or a next-day evaluation.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on epididymitis or cellulitis and deferring surgery, Harold developed refractory septic shock from unchecked necrotizing fasciitis and died.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized that his pain was out of proportion to the exam findings in a poorly controlled diabetic, found crepitus and skin necrosis on a full genital and perineal exam, refused to anchor on epididymitis despite a reassuring cremasteric reflex, started broad-spectrum antibiotics and fluids, and got him to the OR emergently — the time-critical intervention that determines outcome in necrotizing fasciitis.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter drifted toward a diagnosis of simple epididymitis or cellulitis; without emergent surgical consultation and debridement, the infection spread unchecked along the fascial planes.
learningPoints: Fournier's gangrene should be considered in elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised men with perineal pain 'out of proportion' to physical exam findings.
It is a polymicrobial necrotizing infection of the genital and perineal fascia, distinct from and far more dangerous than epididymitis or a simple scrotal abscess.
Fever, dysuria, and a preserved cremasteric reflex favor epididymitis, but none of these findings excludes an early necrotizing infection in a high-risk host.
Antibiotics alone are not definitive treatment — emergent surgical debridement is the time-critical intervention that determines outcome.
Deferring to a 'see them in the morning' or outpatient plan is dangerous; early recognition and aggressive management are critical to maximizing outcomes.
missedOpportunities: A full genital and perineal exam checking for crepitus, dusky/necrotic skin, and malodor
Recognizing pain out of proportion to exam findings as a red flag beyond simple epididymitis
Emergent urology or surgery consultation for probable necrotizing infection rather than an outpatient plan
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and sepsis resuscitation (fluids, labs, lactate) without delay
Immediate surgical debridement rather than awaiting imaging or a next-day/courtesy evaluation

debrief.goodOutcome: PJP recognized as the AIDS-defining presentation of previously undiagnosed HIV; treated empirically with TMP-SMX and adjunctive steroids, HIV confirmed with CD4/viral load sent, TB actively excluded with isolation and AFB smears, and ID/HAART linkage arranged.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized hypoxia wildly out of proportion to a near-normal lung exam, got the CXR and ABG showing a widened A-a gradient, sent a 4th-generation HIV test despite no volunteered history, isolated for TB while sending AFB smears, and started empiric TMP-SMX with adjunctive steroids. Once HIV came back reactive, ID confirmed an AIDS-defining CD4 and arranged HAART linkage — the commonly-missed diagnosis, caught.
debrief.outcomePoor: The mismatch between his hypoxia and his benign exam was never questioned, HIV testing was never sent, and empiric CAP antibiotics were given instead of PJP-directed treatment — his hypoxic respiratory failure progressed unchecked.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on typical CAP or anxiety, the undiagnosed HIV and its AIDS-defining opportunistic infection were never pursued, and he progressed to hypoxic respiratory failure.
learningPoints: Hypoxia markedly out of proportion to a benign lung exam is the classic tip-off for PJP — don't let a clear-sounding chest reassure you.
Send a 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab test based on risk factors and clinical pattern, even when the patient hasn't volunteered a history — ask directly and privately.
The average time from HIV acquisition to an AIDS-defining CD4 under 200 is 8-10 years, often with a clinically silent chronic phase.
TB shares risk factors and a subacute presentation with PJP — isolate and send AFB smears while the workup proceeds, don't wait to rule it out first.

Standard short-course CAP antibiotics do not cover Pneumocystis; empiric TMP-SMX plus steroids (if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >35) is the correct initial therapy, followed by emergent ID consultation and HAART linkage.

learningPoints: Acute pharyngitis typically resolves within 3 to 5 days; symptoms that improve and then dramatically worsen should broaden the differential to life-threatening complications.

debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized that a normal ear exam couldn't explain his pain, took a focused head and neck history, examined beyond the ear, worked through the referred-otalgia differential, avoided another empiric ear treatment, and arranged urgent ENT evaluation — the commonly-missed diagnosis, caught.
debrief.outcomePoor: The workup stayed anchored on the ear. Without a focused head and neck history and exam, the referred pain from an occult malignancy was never explained, and he was sent out for routine follow-up instead of urgent evaluation.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on 'another ear infection,' a third empiric treatment was prescribed and he was discharged to routine follow-up while the occult malignancy went undiagnosed.
learningPoints: Otalgia is often not an ear problem — in one cited study, 28% of ear pain cases in non-ED settings were due to referred (secondary) otalgia.

The ear is innervated by cranial nerves V, VII, IX, and X plus the cervical plexus, so TMJ disease, dental disease, parotitis, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, and head and neck cancer can all present as ear pain.

Persistent unilateral otalgia with an unremarkable otoscopic exam — especially in an older adult with a tobacco/alcohol history — is a red flag for occult head and neck cancer, not just another ear infection.
Pain that is out of proportion to ear exam findings should redirect the workup toward the rest of the head and neck rather than toward a repeat course of otic treatment.
Because significant ear and referred disease can be occult, a thorough exam is warranted in any patient with otalgia, including vulnerable populations who cannot reliably report symptoms.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing that a normal ear exam does not explain severe otalgia
Taking a focused head and neck history for odynophagia, hoarseness, weight loss, and tobacco/alcohol use
Performing a full head and neck exam beyond the ear — oral cavity/oropharynx inspection and neck palpation
Avoiding a third empiric course of ear antibiotics or drops
Arranging urgent otolaryngology referral for endoscopic evaluation and biopsy instead of routine follow-up

speech.speaker: patient

physicalExam.finding: Alert, mildly anxious, cradling the right hand against her chest; non-toxic-appearing, ambulatory, speaking in full sentences.

Two tiny, nearly re-epithelialized puncture marks on the volar surface of the right index finger near the proximal phalanx-MCP crease. No active drainage, no surrounding necrosis — the entry wound itself looks deceptively unremarkable given the degree of finger swelling.

Right index finger held in slight flexion at rest; fusiform ('sausage-digit') swelling from the MCP to the PIP joint; exquisite tenderness along the course of the flexor tendon sheath; pain markedly worsened with passive extension of the finger. Skin intact over the swelling, no fluctuance.
Finger well-perfused, capillary refill under 2 seconds, radial pulse 2+, sensation intact to light touch throughout the digit. No signs of compartment syndrome.
No proximal lymphangitic streaking along the forearm. No epitrochlear or axillary lymphadenopathy appreciated at this time.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Two sets of blood cultures drawn before antibiotics, sent to the lab.

responders.narrative: The puncture wound is nearly closed and too small to swab meaningfully — a useful deep culture would have to come from surgical drainage material, not a surface swab.

speech.text: Hand films are up.

diagnosticReports.body: Fusiform soft tissue swelling overlying the volar proximal index finger with no radiopaque foreign body and no acute fracture or dislocation; bones and joint spaces are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: Soft tissue swelling of the index finger without fracture or radiopaque foreign body.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate started — that covers Pasteurella well.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Amoxicillin-clavulanate dose/route not specified — 875/125 mg PO BID, or IV ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h if admitted.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — a narrow anti-staph agent alone won't reliably cover Pasteurella multocida, which is the classic cat-bite pathogen. She needs amoxicillin-clavulanate coverage instead.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ibuprofen given for pain and swelling.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Wound left open, not sutured — lightly packed and dressed, as expected for a bite puncture like this.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Don't close a bite puncture on the hand — sealing the entry point traps bacteria against the tendon sheath and can turn this into an abscess.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Tdap given — her tetanus status is now up to date.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Since this stray can't be observed for ten days, she meets criteria for rabies postexposure prophylaxis — vaccine series plus rabies immunoglobulin at the wound site.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hand surgery here — these are early Kanavel signs. We'll watch closely on IV antibiotics, but she needs admission tonight and may still need the OR if it doesn't settle.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Admission arranged to the hand surgery service for IV antibiotics and close monitoring.

deterioration.trajectory: early septic flexor tenosynovitis — if unrecognized, progresses from tendon sheath involvement toward the deep palmar space over hours, risking tendon necrosis and bacteremia

stages.event: Swelling extends further up the finger toward the palm; she now resists any attempt to flex or extend it, and is guarding the hand tightly.
stages.nurse: Doctor, her finger's more swollen than when she came in and she won't let anyone near it — are we doing anything for this today?
stages.feedback: Kanavel signs were never assessed — early flexor tenosynovitis is progressing while it's still salvageable without an operative washout.

stages.event: Erythema and swelling now extend into the palm and toward the wrist; she looks increasingly uncomfortable and is starting to sweat.
stages.nurse: Doctor, her whole hand looks tight now and she's guarding it against her chest — the pain's a lot worse. What's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Without empiric anti-Pasteurella antibiotics and an emergent hand surgery consult, the infection has tracked from the tendon sheath toward the deep palmar space — exactly the progression this pathogen is known for.

debrief.goodOutcome: Septic flexor tenosynovitis recognized early despite the deceptively small wound; wound left open, x-rayed, treated with amoxicillin-clavulanate, and hand surgery consulted before deep space spread.
debrief.badOutcome: The tiny puncture wound was dismissed as trivial — no Kanavel exam, no anti-Pasteurella antibiotics, and no hand surgery consult — allowing progression toward the deep palmar space and possible tendon necrosis.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized that the tiny puncture wound was high-risk despite its benign appearance, examined for and found early Kanavel signs, left the wound open, obtained a hand x-ray, started amoxicillin-clavulanate, and got hand surgery involved emergently — averting progression to a deep space infection.
debrief.outcomePoor: The wound's small size led to underestimating the risk. Without a focused hand exam, imaging, anti-Pasteurella antibiotics, or a hand surgery consult, the tenosynovitis was left to progress toward the deep palmar space.
learningPoints: Cat bites cause narrow, deep puncture wounds that inoculate bacteria into joints and tendon sheaths — a tiny, nearly closed entry wound can hide a serious deep infection.
Bite-related puncture wounds to the hand should not be closed primarily; leave them open given the high infection risk.
Pasteurella multocida is the classic rapid-onset cat-bite pathogen and needs an antibiotic active against it, such as amoxicillin-clavulanate — not a narrow anti-staphylococcal agent alone.
Any hand bite requires a deliberate exam for Kanavel signs of flexor tenosynovitis, a surgical emergency requiring hand surgery consultation.
Every mammalian bite needs an explicit tetanus and rabies postexposure prophylaxis assessment based on the wound, immunization history, and whether the biting animal is available for observation.
missedOpportunities: Examining the hand specifically for Kanavel signs of flexor tenosynovitis
Hand x-ray to exclude a retained tooth fragment, fracture, or joint penetration
Empiric amoxicillin-clavulanate for anti-Pasteurella coverage
Leaving the puncture wound open rather than closing it
Emergent hand surgery consult with tetanus and rabies PEP assessment

speech.speaker: consultant

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Malaria smear's back negative, and blood cultures aren't growing anything so far.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: ID here — with the travel history, rash, thrombocytopenia and hemoconcentration I'd admit for serial CBCs, strict fluid balance, and NSAID avoidance. We'll trend her every 6 to 12 hours.

deterioration.trajectory: fever defervescing while clinically worsening — early plasma leakage that progresses to decompensated shock if fluids and admission are never initiated

stages.event: Defervescence mistaken for improvement
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's asking again if she can go home — her fever's coming down so the family thinks she's turning the corner. What's the plan?
stages.feedback: A falling temperature paired with rising heart rate, ongoing vomiting, and vague abdominal discomfort is a red flag here, not reassurance — trending labs and reassessing for warning signs is overdue.

stages.event: Progressive plasma leakage, narrowing pulse pressure
stages.nurse: Her pressure's slipping and her heart rate won't stop climbing. I need direction, now.
stages.feedback: Without volume support, worsening tachycardia and a narrowing pulse pressure reflect ongoing capillary leak — cautious isotonic fluids and admission for monitoring are needed before compensation fails.

stages.event: Decompensated shock, unresponsive to voice
stages.nurse: She's unresponsive and her pressure's barely reading — I'm calling it!
stages.feedback: Compensatory mechanisms failed. Without fluid resuscitation and inpatient monitoring, unchecked plasma leakage progressed to decompensated shock.

debrief.goodOutcome: Warning-sign dengue recognized and treated appropriately; patient stabilized and admitted for monitoring before progressing to shock.
debrief.badOutcome: Fever mistaken for resolving illness, NSAIDs given for pain, and warning signs missed — the patient progressed toward hemorrhagic complications and shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You obtained the travel and mosquito-exposure history, avoided NSAIDs/aspirin, found the positive tourniquet test and petechiae, trended the hematocrit and platelets, recognized the WHO warning signs, and started cautious fluids with admission for monitoring — the critical-phase presentation was caught before decompensation.
debrief.outcomePoor: The defervescence was read as improvement, NSAIDs or aspirin were given for pain, and the warning signs — vomiting, abdominal pain, rising hematocrit, falling platelets — went unaddressed; she was not admitted for monitoring and progressed toward shock.
learningPoints: Dengue is the most rapidly spreading mosquito-borne viral illness worldwide and should be considered in any febrile returning traveler.
The critical phase of dengue often begins as fever defervesces — clinical worsening at this point is a red flag, not reassurance.
NSAIDs and aspirin are contraindicated in suspected dengue because of thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk.
WHO warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, rising hematocrit with falling platelets) mandate hospital admission for monitoring.
No pathognomonic findings exist for mosquito-borne illness, so a thorough travel history is essential to differentiate dengue from malaria and other travel-related fevers.
missedOpportunities: Obtaining a detailed travel and mosquito-exposure history
Avoiding NSAIDs/aspirin given thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk
Performing a tourniquet test and examining for petechiae or mucosal bleeding
Trending hematocrit and platelet counts to catch hemoconcentration
Recognizing WHO warning signs as an indication for admission
Starting cautious isotonic IV fluids and admitting for serial monitoring

deterioration.trajectory: septic shock from a rapidly advancing necrotizing soft-tissue infection — worsens fast without resuscitation, toxin-suppressing antibiotics, and emergent surgical debridement

stages.event: Progressive septic shock
stages.nurse: Doctor, his pressure just dropped to 74 over 42 and he's more diaphoretic — I need a plan right now.
stages.feedback: Aggressive fluid resuscitation for septic shock is time-critical in necrotizing fasciitis — delaying it lets him spiral toward refractory shock.

stages.event: Advancing soft-tissue necrosis and deepening shock
