stages.event: Refractory shock and cardiac arrest

stages.feedback: Without emergent surgical debridement, necrotizing fasciitis progresses to overwhelming sepsis and death — antibiotics alone are never sufficient; the OR is the only life-saving intervention, and delay is fatal.

debrief.goodOutcome: Necrotizing fasciitis recognized early; the patient was resuscitated, started on broad-spectrum antibiotics with clindamycin, and taken emergently to the OR for debridement.
debrief.badOutcome: The diagnosis was missed or surgery was delayed, and the necrotizing infection progressed to overwhelming septic shock and death.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the red flags of necrotizing fasciitis, resuscitated aggressively with fluids, sent supportive labs and cultures before starting vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, and clindamycin, and got surgery to the bedside emergently for debridement without waiting on imaging — the definitive, life-saving treatment was delivered in time.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter ended without emergent surgical debridement — antibiotics and supportive care alone were never enough, and the infection progressed unchecked.
learningPoints: Pain out of proportion with systemic toxicity is necrotizing fasciitis until proven otherwise.
Definitive treatment is emergent surgical debridement — antibiotics alone are not enough.
Add clindamycin to suppress toxin production alongside broad-spectrum + MRSA coverage.
A reassuring early skin exam and a low LRINEC do not exclude it — don't delay surgery.
Draw cultures before starting antibiotics, but never let culture collection delay the surgical page.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing pain out of proportion as a red flag rather than reassuring skin findings
Aggressive early fluid resuscitation for septic shock
Blood cultures sent before starting vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, and clindamycin
Emergent surgical consultation for debridement without waiting on imaging

stages.feedback: Untreated skull-base osteomyelitis tracked intracranially. Without IV antipseudomonal antibiotics and emergent ENT involvement for source control, this progressed to fatal intracranial extension.

debrief.goodOutcome: Necrotizing otitis externa recognized and treated — cranial nerve exam, inflammatory markers, and CT-confirmed skull-base involvement led to IV antipseudomonal antibiotics and ENT admission before permanent damage occurred.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized this wasn't simple otitis externa, screened cranial nerves, sent inflammatory markers and blood cultures, confirmed skull-base involvement on CT, started IV antipseudomonal antibiotics, and got ENT involved for admission — the invasive infection was caught and treated before it caused permanent neurologic damage.
debrief.badOutcome: The two-week failure of topical therapy in a diabetic host was anchored on as simple otitis externa; without IV antipseudomonal antibiotics and ENT involvement, the skull-base infection progressed to facial nerve palsy and ultimately fatal intracranial extension.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter never escalated beyond topical therapy for what looked like a routine ear infection, so the invasive skull-base process went unrecognized and untreated.
learningPoints: Suspect necrotizing otitis externa in any diabetic or immunocompromised patient with otitis externa that fails to improve on topical therapy.
Granulation tissue at the osseocartilaginous junction of the ear canal, with pain out of proportion to exam, is the classic can't-miss finding.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the usual causative organism; management requires IV antipseudomonal antibiotics, not topical drops alone.
New cranial neuropathy, especially facial nerve palsy, signals skull-base spread and a worse prognosis.
Advanced imaging (CT temporal bone ± MRI) and emergent ENT consultation are indicated whenever this diagnosis is clinically suspected.
missedOpportunities: A full cranial nerve exam screening for facial nerve and lower cranial nerve involvement
Inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP), blood glucose, and blood cultures
CT temporal bone imaging (with MRI if intracranial extension was suspected)
IV antipseudomonal antibiotics rather than continued topical drops
Emergent ENT consultation for admission and definitive management

No rash on palms or soles. No lesions, no lymphadenopathy on cursory survey. Skin warm and dry.

Mild-to-moderate diffuse cortical volume loss for stated age, with mildly prominent ventricles. IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial process; nonspecific diffuse atrophy. Clinical correlation recommended.

Scattered patchy leptomeningeal enhancement over the cerebral convexities. No restricted diffusion, acute infarct, or mass lesion. IMPRESSION: Diffuse cortical atrophy with subtle leptomeningeal enhancement; nonspecific but compatible with a chronic meningoencephalitic process. Recommend correlation with CSF and serologic studies.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold off — IM benzathine penicillin doesn't reliably cross into the CNS. If we're worried about central nervous system involvement, that dosing form won't be adequate; we need IV penicillin instead.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: With the ongoing benzathine shortage — or if she can't tolerate penicillin — IV ceftriaxone for 10 to 14 days is a reasonable alternative, though penicillin desensitization is still preferred when it's feasible.

speech.speaker: consultant

speech.speaker: nurse

deterioration.trajectory: hemodynamically stable, subacute cognitive case — the risk is a missed diagnosis and lost follow-up, not physiologic collapse

stages.event: Family growing insistent on a straight dementia workup
stages.nurse: Doctor, the daughter's asking why we're doing blood work instead of just getting her in to see a neurologist. Where are we headed with this?
stages.feedback: Without RPR/VDRL screening, the workup keeps drifting toward a pure dementia pathway and a treatable cause stays hidden.

stages.event: Family requesting discharge for outpatient neurology follow-up

stages.feedback: Discharging before treatment is initiated risks losing her to follow-up while an active infection goes untreated.

debrief.goodOutcome: Neurosyphilis recognized and treated with IV penicillin G; partner testing arranged for her husband.
debrief.outcomeGood: You took a private, nonjudgmental sexual history despite her initial denial, sent RPR/VDRL alongside the dementia workup, confirmed with a treponemal test, started IV penicillin G, and arranged partner notification — the great imitator, caught before further decline.
debrief.outcomePoor: The workup stayed anchored on a primary dementia and depression evaluation; RPR/VDRL was never sent, so the treatable cause of her decline went unrecognized.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on dementia and depression, the neurosyphilis went undiagnosed and untreated, leaving her at risk for further cognitive decline and late complications like tabes dorsalis.
learningPoints: Syphilis is called the great imitator because its presentation varies widely by stage and can mimic dementia, depression, or other psychiatric illness
Treponema pallidum's lack of surface proteins allows early invasion of the CNS, so neurologic and psychiatric involvement can occur even without a recognized prior syphilis history
Denial of current sexual activity should not exclude syphilis testing, since risk can stem from remote or lifetime exposure rather than current behavior
RPR/VDRL is a screening test that requires reliable follow-up for confirmatory treponemal testing when positive
Persistent penicillin G benzathine shortages have driven the need for alternative treatment regimens across stages of syphilis
missedOpportunities: A private, nonjudgmental sexual history despite her initial denial
RPR/VDRL screening for syphilis alongside the dementia workup
Confirmatory treponemal testing (FTA-ABS/TPPA) with reliable follow-up
IV penicillin G treatment appropriate for neurosyphilis
Partner notification and testing for her husband

physicalExam.finding: Toxic-appearing, diffusely flushed, tachypneic, and lethargic but arousable. Skin warm early on.

Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Peripheral pulses thready but palpable; extremities remain warm (warm/distributive shock pattern).
Diffusely tender to palpation without rebound or guarding; active bowel sounds; ongoing watery diarrhea on the pad.
Speculum exam reveals a retained tampon high in the vaginal vault with foul, purulent discharge; vaginal mucosa diffusely hyperemic and friable. No lacerations.
Diffuse muscle tenderness to palpation over thighs and calves without focal weakness or joint swelling.
Intermittently disoriented to time, otherwise alert; no focal motor or sensory deficits.

deterioration.trajectory: toxin-mediated distributive shock from a retained tampon — worsens fast without source removal, aggressive resuscitation, and toxin-suppressing antibiotics

stages.event: Progressive distributive shock
stages.nurse: Her pressure just dropped to 66 over 36 and she's more listless — I need a plan right now.
stages.feedback: Finding and removing the retained source and resuscitating aggressively with fluids and vasopressors are time-critical in toxic shock syndrome.

stages.event: Refractory shock and multi-organ collapse
stages.nurse: She's lost her pulse — she's arrested.
stages.feedback: Untreated toxic shock syndrome progresses to refractory shock and multi-organ failure; source removal, resuscitation, and toxin-suppressing antibiotics were the time-critical steps that were missed.

debrief.goodOutcome: Toxic shock syndrome recognized; the retained source was removed and she was resuscitated with fluids, vasopressors, and toxin-suppressing antibiotics.
debrief.badOutcome: The retained tampon was never found and removed, resuscitation lagged, and the toxin-mediated shock progressed to multi-organ failure.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized toxic shock syndrome, found and removed the retained tampon, resuscitated aggressively with fluids and vasopressors, sent cultures before starting vancomycin plus clindamycin, and escalated to IVIG for refractory shock with ICU-level supportive care — the source-driven shock was controlled.
debrief.outcomePoor: Source control and resuscitation were delayed, and the toxin-mediated shock progressed unchecked toward multi-organ failure.
learningPoints: Look for and remove the toxin source (e.g., retained tampon, packing, or wound focus) in toxic shock syndrome.
Draw cultures before starting antibiotics, then give vancomycin (or another anti-MRSA agent) plus clindamycin to suppress toxin production.
Diffuse 'sunburn' erythroderma, mucous membrane hyperemia, and multi-organ involvement (renal, hepatic, muscular, hematologic) are characteristic of TSS.
Resuscitate aggressively with crystalloid and add vasopressors early for refractory shock.
Consider IVIG in severe or refractory cases; avoid NSAIDs given the acute kidney injury and thrombocytopenia.
missedOpportunities: Finding and removing the retained tampon
Aggressive fluid resuscitation and early vasopressors for refractory shock
Cultures drawn before vancomycin plus clindamycin
IVIG for refractory shock and ICU-level multi-organ supportive care

deterioration.trajectory: high-consequence febrile returning traveler — unrecognized risk factors delay isolation/PPE, and unrepleted volume losses and coagulopathy progress toward shock

stages.event: Unrecognized exposure risk, ongoing unprotected contact
stages.nurse: Doctor, her partner's still sitting bedside without so much as a mask and nobody's moved her out of the open bay — are we doing anything about the room situation?
stages.feedback: Without a travel/exposure history flagged at first contact, isolation and infection-control precautions never start — family and staff stay needlessly exposed while she's managed like a routine flu case.

stages.event: Unrepleted volume losses and worsening coagulopathy
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's more lethargic and her pressure keeps dropping — she still hasn't gotten a single bag of fluids. What's the plan?
stages.feedback: Ongoing vomiting and diarrhea without fluid, electrolyte, and coagulopathy-directed supportive care drives her toward hypovolemic shock — supportive care is the mainstay of management here and can't wait.

stages.event: Refractory shock and cardiovascular collapse
stages.nurse: She's lost her pulse — she's arrested.
stages.feedback: Unrepleted volume losses and an uncorrected coagulopathy progressed to refractory shock and cardiovascular collapse — she never received fluid resuscitation or correction of her coagulopathy.

debrief.goodOutcome: Travel and exposure risk factors were caught at first contact; the patient was isolated, staff protected with PPE, public health notified, endemic infections actively ruled out, and she was resuscitated with fluid, electrolyte, and coagulopathy-directed supportive care.
debrief.badOutcome: Travel history was skipped or delayed, so she was examined and had blood drawn before isolation and PPE were in place — exposing staff and delaying recognition — and unrepleted volume losses and coagulopathy progressed toward shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You asked about travel and sick contacts before laying a hand on her, isolated her and got the team into PPE, notified infection control and public health, actively ruled out malaria and typhoid, and resuscitated her with fluids, electrolytes, and correction of her coagulopathy — the exposure risk contained and her physiology supported.
debrief.outcomePoor: The travel and exposure history came late or not at all, so isolation, PPE, and public health notification lagged behind hands-on contact — and without fluid, electrolyte, and coagulopathy-directed supportive care her volume losses progressed toward shock.
learningPoints: Early high-consequence viral illness is clinically nonspecific — fever, myalgia, headache, and GI symptoms mimic malaria, typhoid, and viral gastroenteritis.
Epidemiologic risk-factor screening (travel to an affected region or contact with a symptomatic patient) must happen at prescreening, before exam or blood draw.
These illnesses spread only through direct contact with blood or body fluids of a symptomatic person, but unprotected healthcare workers are at real risk once that contact occurs.
Actively rule out malaria, typhoid, and other endemic febrile illnesses without letting that workup delay isolation.
Supportive care — fluid and electrolyte repletion and correction of coagulopathy — is the mainstay of management while confirmatory testing is arranged.
missedOpportunities: Travel and exposure risk-factor screening at first contact, before exam or blood draw
Immediate isolation with infection-control precautions
Donning appropriate PPE before hands-on exam or phlebotomy
Notifying infection control and public health
Actively ruling out malaria, typhoid, and other endemic febrile illnesses
Fluid, electrolyte, and coagulopathy-directed supportive care

stages.event: Malignant rhythm develops
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive and the monitor just changed — I need you at the bedside right now!
stages.feedback: Unaddressed hyperkalemic membrane toxicity progressed to a malignant wide-complex rhythm; the potassium still hasn't been shifted or removed.

stages.event: Cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: He's lost his pulse — he's in V-fib! Starting compressions and charging the defibrillator!
stages.feedback: Severe hyperkalemia with cardiac toxicity was never definitively treated with calcium, shifting agents, and dialysis; membrane instability progressed to arrest.

debrief.goodOutcome: Severe hyperkalemia with cardiac toxicity recognized and treated in the correct order — membrane stabilized, potassium shifted, and dialysis arranged.
debrief.outcomeGood: You obtained an ECG, recognized the peaked T waves and widening QRS, gave IV calcium first to stabilize the myocardium, shifted potassium with insulin/dextrose and albuterol, kept him on continuous cardiac monitoring, and got nephrology to dialyze him emergently — his rhythm and labs improved.
debrief.outcomePoor: Definitive treatment was delayed or given out of order, so the membrane instability was never corrected.
debrief.badOutcome: Without calcium to stabilize the membrane and dialysis to remove the potassium, the rhythm degenerated into a malignant arrhythmia and cardiac arrest.
learningPoints: With hyperkalemic ECG changes, give calcium first to stabilize the cardiac membrane.
Insulin/dextrose and albuterol only shift potassium temporarily — they buy time, not a cure.
In ESRD, emergent dialysis is the definitive treatment for severe hyperkalemia.
Monitor glucose after insulin administration and keep the patient on continuous cardiac monitoring.
Avoid succinylcholine in hyperkalemia — it can further raise potassium and worsen the rhythm.
missedOpportunities: Giving IV calcium promptly once ECG changes were recognized
Shifting potassium with insulin/dextrose and nebulized albuterol
Arranging emergent dialysis as definitive therapy
Maintaining continuous cardiac monitoring throughout treatment
Rechecking potassium and glucose after treatment

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: I found her about an hour ago, all sweaty and confused, not making sense. She takes a pill for her diabetes — glipizide — and I know she skipped lunch today.

any: how long has she had diabetes

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Type 2 diabetes for about fifteen years. Her doctor mentioned last visit that her kidneys weren't working as well as they used to.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Glipizide for the sugar, lisinopril for her pressure, and a statin. No allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She lives alone, mostly independent. She told me on the phone this morning she wasn't hungry and skipped lunch, but she still took her morning pills like always.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Come to think of it, she started an antibiotic last week — Bactrim, I think — for a bladder infection. Otherwise nothing new.

physicalExam.finding: Diaphoretic and pale, lethargic but arousable to voice. Confused, oriented to person only. No trauma noted.

No facial droop, no focal motor or sensory deficits, pupils equal and reactive to light. Confusion improves transiently with stimulation and sugar-containing fluids offered by the nurse. No witnessed seizure activity at this time.
Cool, clammy, and diaphoretic throughout. No rash, no track marks, no signs of trauma.
Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs or rubs. Pulses full and equal, capillary refill normal.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fingerstick confirms 32 — critically low.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Chem's back — creatinine's up a bit, and the lab glucose confirms 32.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Insulin and C-peptide are both elevated, and the sulfonylurea screen came back positive.

image.query: normal sinus rhythm 12-lead ECG no ST changes no ischemia

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head (Non-contrast)
diagnosticReports.body: No acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, or large territory infarct. Ventricles and sulci are age-appropriate. No midline shift or mass lesion. IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial abnormality.

image.query: non-contrast head CT elderly patient no hemorrhage no infarct

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: D50, 25 grams IV push — pushing it now.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: (stirring) ...mmn...what... what happened?

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Dextrose dose/route not specified — give 1 amp (25 g) of D50W IV push and recheck glucose in 15 minutes.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Glucagon 1 mg IM given — no IV access yet, so this buys us time.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Reasonable, but glucagon depends on glycogen stores and can be less reliable in an elderly, poorly-eating patient — get IV dextrose going as soon as access is in.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Glucagon dose not specified — 1 mg IM (or intranasal) if no IV access is available.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: We haven't corrected her glucose yet — let's push dextrose first before we talk about a maintenance infusion.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Repeat fingerstick is 145. Starting a D10 infusion at 50 mL/hr — sulfonylureas can drop her again for hours.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: She's still too altered to safely swallow anything — let's get her glucose up first.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's alert enough now — turkey sandwich and juice given, she's eating on her own.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Octreotide 50 mcg subcutaneous given — should blunt the ongoing insulin release from the glipizide.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Octreotide dose not specified — 50-100 mcg subcutaneously, may repeat every 6 hours for recurrent hypoglycemia.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Good thought — her creatinine's up so the glipizide isn't clearing well, she skipped lunch but still took her pill, and the Bactrim she started last week potentiates sulfonylureas.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Poison Control here — glipizide effect can cause recurrent hypoglycemia for 24 hours or more, especially with reduced renal clearance. Recommend octreotide for refractory episodes, admission with serial glucose checks, and holding the sulfonylurea.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Admission to observation arranged — hourly glucose checks and the D10 infusion continue overnight given the recurrence risk.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — she's on a sulfonylurea. This can rebound into severe hypoglycemia for the next 24 hours even after she looks fine. She needs admission and observation, not discharge.

deterioration.trajectory: severe sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia — the prolonged, recurrent insulin-secretagogue effect causes neuroglycopenic injury and rebound hypoglycemia without repeated dextrose, feeding, and observation

stages.event: Prolonged untreated severe hypoglycemia
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's twitching now and barely rousable — her fingerstick is still critically low. I need an order!
stages.feedback: Severe hypoglycemia left uncorrected causes worsening neuroglycopenia, seizures, and risk of permanent brain injury — the critically low glucose needs immediate correction.

stages.event: Rebound hypoglycemia after a single correction
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's sweating and unresponsive again out of nowhere — I thought we already fixed this!
stages.feedback: Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia recurs for many hours after a single dextrose dose; ongoing rechecks and a maintenance dextrose infusion are needed, not a one-time bolus.

stages.event: Refractory hypoglycemic seizure and cardiorespiratory arrest
stages.nurse: She's seizing and now she's lost her pulse — starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Prolonged, uncorrected hypoglycemia progressed to refractory seizures and cardiac arrest; IV dextrose (or IM glucagon) must be given immediately and glucose rechecked and maintained.

debrief.goodOutcome: Severe hypoglycemia corrected, recurrence anticipated and covered with a dextrose infusion and octreotide, contributors identified, and the patient admitted for observation.
debrief.badOutcome: The patient was corrected once with dextrose and discharged, then crashed again hours later from recurrent sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia.
debrief.outcomeGood: You treated the critical glucose with dextrose, rechecked and covered her with a D10 infusion, fed her once alert, gave octreotide to blunt further insulin release, identified renal impairment and a drug interaction as contributors, and admitted her for observation instead of discharging after a single correction.
debrief.outcomePoor: Glucose was corrected once and the recurrence risk was not managed — either the recheck/infusion was skipped, or she was discharged prematurely — leaving her exposed to rebound hypoglycemia.
learningPoints: Sulfonylurea hypoglycemia is prolonged and recurrent — a single amp of D50 is not enough.
Octreotide suppresses insulin release and is the antidote for refractory or recurrent sulfonylurea hypoglycemia.
Feed the patient once alert and admit/observe rather than discharging after one correction.
Glucagon works with no IV access but is less reliable in a glycogen-depleted patient.
Look for contributors — renal impairment, missed meals, and interacting drugs (e.g., TMP-SMX) prolong sulfonylurea effect.
missedOpportunities: Rechecking glucose and starting a dextrose (D10) infusion after the initial correction
Feeding the patient and admitting/observing rather than discharging after a single correction
Giving octreotide for recurrent or refractory hypoglycemia
Identifying contributors such as renal impairment and the interacting antibiotic

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was totally fine yesterday. This morning he said his head was pounding and the light was killing his eyes, then his neck got stiff and he stopped making sense.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Roommates say he's gotten more confused just in the last hour.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's healthy. No hospitalizations, no chronic conditions that we know of.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: No regular medications, no known allergies.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He got all his childhood vaccines, but I don't think he's had anything since he started college two years ago.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He lives in the dorms, shares a suite with three other guys. Nobody else is sick that we know of, but they're all in tight quarters.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: (mumbling) No... I'm healthy. No HIV, no steroids. Still got my spleen.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, lethargic but arousable to voice. Shielding his eyes from overhead light. Diaphoretic.
Alert to name only, oriented to person only, slow and confused responses. Pupils equal and reactive to light. No focal motor or sensory deficits. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam.
Marked nuchal rigidity with pain on neck flexion. Positive Kernig's sign and positive Brudzinski's sign bilaterally.
Scattered petechiae on the bilateral lower extremities and trunk with a few early purpuric lesions; several are non-blanching and appear to be increasing in number since arrival.
Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Peripheral pulses thready, capillary refill 3 seconds, extremities cool and mottled distally.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Blood cultures times two drawn from separate sites, sent before the first antibiotic dose.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head without contrast
diagnosticReports.body: No acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, midline shift, or hydrocephalus. Ventricles and sulci are age-appropriate with no effacement. No findings suggesting impending herniation. IMPRESSION: No contraindication to lumbar puncture identified on this study.

image.query: non-contrast axial head CT normal ventricles no mass effect young adult
normal portable AP chest x-ray young adult male

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: Let's get the antibiotics running first, Doctor — you can tap right after, but treatment can't wait on a needle.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: LP performed at the bedside without difficulty, sent for cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, and culture.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Ceftriaxone and vancomycin dose/route not specified — give ceftriaxone 2 g IV plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV, guided by weight and renal function.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ceftriaxone 2 grams IV and a vancomycin loading dose are running now — pushed the moment you gave the order.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: ID here — he's 22 and immunocompetent, so ampicillin adds nothing. That's reserved for neonates, patients over 50, pregnancy, or immunocompromise.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Dexamethasone dose not specified — 10 mg IV, timed with or just before the first antibiotic dose.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Dexamethasone 10 milligrams IV given just ahead of the antibiotics.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Droplet precautions instituted — masks for staff and visitors, and he's moved to a private room.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Public health has been notified — this is reportable. Close contacts, including his roommates, will be offered prophylactic antibiotics.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: ID here — good, you're already on empiric coverage. We'll narrow once the gram stain and cultures speciate.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neuro and critical care are on board — we'll trend serial exams for rising ICP or seizure and help manage the shock.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ICU bed confirmed, report called — ready for transfer whenever you are.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity — meningococcemia with early septic shock; empiric antibiotics are the single highest-yield intervention and must not be delayed

stages.event: Meningococcemia progressing — rash spreading rapidly up the trunk, patient more somnolent, pressure trending down.
stages.nurse: Doctor, the rash is spreading up his abdomen and he's harder to rouse — what do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Empiric antibiotics were never started. Every hour of delay in bacterial meningitis measurably increases mortality — don't wait for CT or LP results to treat.

stages.event: Septic shock — hypotensive, mottled extremities, rash now confluent purpura.
stages.nurse: He's cold and mottled to the knees and his pressure's dropping fast — I need orders now.
stages.feedback: Untreated meningococcemia progresses to fulminant septic shock and DIC. Empiric antibiotics should have been given within minutes of arrival, not held for confirmatory testing.

stages.event: Cardiovascular collapse — pulseless, code called.
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive, no pulse — starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Fulminant meningococcemia was fatal without early empiric antibiotics — the single highest-yield intervention in this case was withheld too long.

debrief.goodOutcome: Empiric antibiotics and dexamethasone given without delay; LP performed after treatment started; meningococcemia recognized and public health notified.
debrief.badOutcome: Antibiotics were delayed while awaiting CT or LP results, and the patient progressed to fulminant septic shock from meningococcemia.
debrief.outcomeGood: You treated empirically within minutes of arrival — ceftriaxone, vancomycin, and dexamethasone were running before the head CT or LP even resulted. Blood cultures were drawn first, droplet isolation went up, and public health was notified for contact prophylaxis. He stabilized and went to the ICU.
debrief.outcomePoor: Antibiotics were withheld while waiting on imaging or spinal fluid results. The rash spread, he became hypotensive, and the meningococcemia progressed to septic shock and DIC.
learningPoints: Never delay antibiotics for imaging or LP in suspected bacterial meningitis — draw blood cultures, then treat immediately.
Empiric therapy is ceftriaxone plus vancomycin; add ampicillin only for Listeria risk (age over 50, immunocompromise, pregnancy, neonates).
Give dexamethasone with or just before the first antibiotic dose — benefit is greatest for pneumococcal meningitis.
Head CT before LP is reserved for focal deficits, papilledema, immunocompromise, seizure, or altered mental status — otherwise proceed straight to LP.
A spreading petechial or purpuric rash with fever signals meningococcemia — start droplet precautions and notify public health for contact prophylaxis.
missedOpportunities: Empiric IV ceftriaxone and vancomycin given immediately, without waiting for CT or LP
Blood cultures drawn before the first antibiotic dose
Dexamethasone given with the first antibiotic dose
Droplet isolation instituted
Public health notified with prophylaxis arranged for close contacts

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I was hauling pallets at the warehouse three days ago and my back's been killing me since — now it shoots down both my legs, way worse than any strain I've had before.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm healthy, doc. No real medical problems, never had surgery.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I don't take anything regular, just ibuprofen when my back acts up. No allergies.

any: what do you do for work

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Warehouse work, lots of heavy lifting. Smoke maybe half a pack a week, couple beers on weekends. No other drugs.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've thrown my back out before, comes and goes, always better in a few days with rest and ibuprofen. This is different — way worse.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's like an 8 out of 10, worse when I stand or walk, a little better lying flat. The shooting pain down both legs is the worst part.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Both legs, yeah. They feel heavy and kind of numb in spots — I stumbled once trying to get up this morning.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: (pauses, looks away) ...Yeah, actually — I haven't been able to fully empty my bladder since this morning, and it feels numb down between my legs, like sitting on a foam pad. I didn't want to bring it up.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Not really, just the back and leg pain. That's really all that's bothering me.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable, shifting positions on the stretcher, antalgic posture. Alert, oriented, non-toxic, afebrile.
Midline tenderness over the L4-L5 region, no stepoff or deformity, paraspinal muscle spasm, no overlying erythema or wound.
Bilateral positive straight-leg raise, reproducing radiating leg pain at roughly 30-40 degrees on both sides.
Symmetric 4/5 weakness of hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors bilaterally; other lower extremity muscle groups grossly 5/5.
Decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the posteromedial thighs and calves bilaterally.
Patellar and Achilles reflexes symmetrically diminished (1+) bilaterally; toes downgoing, no clonus.
Markedly decreased anal sphincter tone on digital rectal exam; decreased sensation to light touch over the perianal/saddle region bilaterally. No mass, no blood, normal prostate contour.
Antalgic, cautious gait, favoring trunk flexion; unable to walk on heels without difficulty.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Bladder scan after he tried to void — 450 mL left behind.

labResults.name: Urinalysis
labResults.value: No leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, 0-2 WBC/hpf, no bacteria

diagnosticReports.title: Lumbar Spine X-ray
diagnosticReports.body: Vertebral body heights and alignment are preserved. Mild disc space narrowing and endplate osteophytes at L4-L5 and L5-S1. No fracture, subluxation, or destructive osseous lesion. IMPRESSION: Mild degenerative changes; no acute fracture. Radiographs do not assess disc/canal soft tissue.

image.query: lumbar spine x-ray mild degenerative changes no fracture

diagnosticReports.title: CT Lumbar Spine Without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Moderate central disc bulge/protrusion at L4-L5 with associated facet degenerative change; the central canal appears narrowed but soft-tissue and neural element detail is limited on CT relative to MRI. No fracture, no acute bony abnormality, no epidural gas or fluid collection. IMPRESSION: Degenerative disc disease with likely disc protrusion at L4-L5; given the patient's bilateral neurologic and perineal findings, dedicated MRI is recommended for definitive characterization of the central canal and nerve roots.

image.query: CT lumbar spine degenerative disc disease axial

diagnosticReports.title: MRI Lumbar Spine Without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Large central disc extrusion at L4-L5 producing severe spinal canal stenosis with crowding and compression of the traversing lumbosacral nerve roots; the roots appear clustered and displaced posteriorly with effacement of the surrounding CSF signal at this level. No epidural fluid collection or discitis. IMPRESSION: Large central L4-L5 disc herniation causing severe canal stenosis and compression of the traversing nerve roots, correlating with the patient's bilateral radicular and perineal findings; recommend urgent surgical evaluation.

image.query: large central L4-L5 disc herniation severe canal stenosis sagittal MRI

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fentanyl given — he says the pain's a little more tolerable.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Analgesic ordered without a dose — specify (e.g., fentanyl 50 mcg IV).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Copy — keeping him here, no discharge planned. We're treating this as a surgical spinal emergency despite the normal vitals.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: The bladder retention doesn't look like a straightforward medication or constipation issue given everything else going on with his exam.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Ortho here — with bilateral deficits, saddle numbness, and urinary retention, this needs emergent neurosurgery, not an outpatient referral from us.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neurosurgery — with bladder retention and saddle anesthesia this is time-critical. We're booking the OR now for emergent decompression.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's headed up with neurosurgery for emergent decompression.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Wait — bilateral leg weakness, saddle numbness, and urinary retention are red flags for cauda equina. Sending him home now risks permanent bladder and bowel dysfunction.

deterioration.trajectory: cauda equina syndrome from an evolving central L4-L5 disc herniation — vitals stay reassuring throughout, but delay in recognition, imaging, and surgical decompression risks permanent bladder atony, bowel incontinence, saddle anesthesia, and sexual dysfunction

stages.event: Ongoing unaddressed bladder retention and progressing bilateral leg heaviness
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's still shifting around trying to get comfortable and says his legs feel heavier than when he arrived — is there a plan yet?
stages.feedback: Without directly asking about bladder/bowel and saddle symptoms and performing a rectal exam with a post-void residual, the red flags driving this presentation stay hidden behind a routine-looking back strain.

stages.event: Prolonged unrecognized compression nearing the window for reversible decompression
stages.nurse: Doctor, it's been almost an hour now and he keeps asking what's going on — what do I tell him?
stages.feedback: Emergent MRI and neurosurgical decompression are time-critical; the longer decompression is delayed, the less likely his bladder, bowel, and sensory function fully recover.

stages.event: Prolonged, unrecognized compression likely past the window for full neurologic recovery
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's getting more frustrated lying here this long with no answers — I need something to tell him.
stages.feedback: By this point in a real cauda equina syndrome, compression this prolonged often leaves permanent bladder, bowel, and sensory deficits even after decompression — recognition and surgery are time-critical even when vital signs never change.

debrief.goodOutcome: Cauda equina syndrome recognized despite normal vitals, with emergent MRI and neurosurgical decompression obtained before permanent bladder, bowel, and sensory injury.
debrief.outcomeGood: You directly asked about urinary retention and saddle numbness, performed a rectal exam and post-void residual, obtained an emergent MRI, and got neurosurgery to the bedside for urgent decompression — you didn't let completely normal vital signs talk you out of a time-critical diagnosis.
debrief.outcomePoor: The urinary and saddle symptoms went unasked, or the workup stalled without an emergent MRI and neurosurgical consultation, so the compression continued unrecognized while it looked like routine mechanical back pain.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on a benign-appearing lifting injury with reassuring vitals, the compression progressed, leaving Marcus with permanent bladder atony, bowel incontinence, saddle anesthesia, and sexual dysfunction.
learningPoints: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency where the time-critical threat is neurologic and urinary/bowel disability, not hemodynamic instability — vital signs are often completely normal.
Urinary retention is often the earliest sign of cauda equina compression because the parasympathetic bladder fibers are the smallest and most vulnerable to injury.
Patients rarely volunteer bladder, bowel, or saddle-numbness symptoms out of embarrassment — ask directly and perform a rectal exam in any back pain patient with bilateral leg symptoms.
A benign-appearing radicular back pain presentation after a lifting injury can look identical to cauda equina syndrome until red flags are actively sought.
Delayed decompression risks permanent denervation, while urgent MRI and emergent surgical decompression improve the odds of neurologic and bladder recovery.
missedOpportunities: Directly asking about urinary retention/incontinence and saddle or perineal numbness
A rectal exam to assess anal sphincter tone and perianal sensation
A post-void residual bladder volume
Emergent/STAT MRI of the lumbar spine
Emergent neurosurgical consultation for decompression

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My neck's been sore since I saw my chiropractor Tuesday — figured it was from hitting legs hard at the gym. This headache's been nagging since then too.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He kept saying it was just soreness, but he's never like this. Something's wrong.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm healthy, no real medical history. Maybe some pollen allergies, nothing else.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: No prescription meds, just protein powder and creatine. No drug allergies that I know of.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I bike competitively and do CrossFit, train almost every day. I don't smoke, maybe a beer on weekends — never done cocaine or anything like that.

physicalExam.finding: Alert but anxious-appearing young man, mild left facial asymmetry visible at rest, speaking in short sentences with mild slurring. No acute distress at rest.
Right eyelid shows subtle ptosis (~2 mm) with anisocoria — right pupil smaller than left (miosis) — and no facial anhidrosis. Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus.
Mild left lower facial droop with flattening of the left nasolabial fold; dysarthria noted on formal speech testing, words mildly slurred but understandable. Tongue midline, no diplopia.
Left arm and leg drift downward on pronator drift testing, 4/5 strength on the left versus 5/5 on the right. Subtle left-sided inattention on double simultaneous stimulation.
Mild tenderness to palpation over the right sternocleidomastoid/upper neck, no lymphadenopathy, no crepitus. Faint bruit auscultated over the right carotid region; trachea midline.
Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No obvious cardioembolic source on exam.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: ECG's up — normal sinus rhythm, no ectopy.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Head CT's back, radiology's looking at it now.

diagnosticReports.body: No acute intracranial hemorrhage. Subtle loss of gray-white differentiation and mild sulcal effacement over the right MCA territory. No midline shift, ventricles and cisterns preserved. IMPRESSION: No hemorrhage; findings compatible with early right MCA territory ischemic change. speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: CTA's back — radiology wants to talk to you directly about the right side.

diagnosticReports.title: CTA Head and Neck

The right MCA M2 segment demonstrates abrupt cutoff consistent with distal embolic occlusion. Left carotid system and vertebrobasilar circulation are patent. IMPRESSION: Right internal carotid artery dissection with intramural thrombus and associated right MCA branch occlusion.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Duplex tech says views were limited high in the neck near the skull base — tough to fully assess that segment.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Stroke code activated — neurology, radiology, and the stroke coordinator are all being paged now. Girlfriend's confirming the last time he seemed normal.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Whoa — don't chase that pressure down in acute stroke. Permissive hypertension protects perfusion to the penumbra; aggressive lowering can worsen the infarct.

speech.speaker: nurse

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Stroke unit bed is ready — vascular neurology following, blood pressure goals per stroke protocol rather than aggressive lowering, and repeat neuro checks are ordered.

stages.event: Left arm weakness has progressed and his speech is more slurred; girlfriend is now visibly panicked.
stages.nurse: Doctor, his left side is weaker than it was twenty minutes ago and he's harder to understand — what's the hold up?
stages.feedback: Delaying emergent CT head and CTA head/neck lets the embolic clot burden propagate and the ischemic territory expand — every minute of delay costs viable brain tissue.

stages.event: Level of consciousness is dropping, he no longer follows commands on the left side, and the right pupil is now sluggish to react.
stages.nurse: He's stopped talking to us and that right pupil looks different — we need a plan right now, doctor.
stages.feedback: Without decisive reperfusion therapy — thrombolysis and/or thrombectomy — within the treatment window, the infarct core keeps expanding toward malignant edema.

stages.event: Right pupil is now fixed and dilated, breathing has become irregular, and he is unresponsive — signs of herniation from malignant MCA swelling.
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's not responding to anything and his pupil isn't reacting — I'm calling a code.
stages.feedback: The window for reperfusion therapy closed without action; unchecked infarct swelling caused herniation and death. Cervical artery dissection stroke is time-critical, and age alone is never a reason to take it off the differential.

debrief.goodOutcome: Cervical artery dissection recognized promptly; stroke code activated, CT head and CTA head/neck obtained, reperfusion therapy given, and the patient admitted to a stroke unit with vascular neurology.
debrief.outcomeGood: You kept stroke on the differential despite his age and the tempting muscle-strain story, found the Horner's sign, got CTA head and neck imaging that showed the dissection, treated with reperfusion therapy and antithrombotics once hemorrhage was excluded, and admitted him to a stroke unit — the disabling deficits were caught in time.
debrief.outcomePoor: The neck pain and headache were worked up as musculoskeletal strain, the focal deficits were not pursued with vascular imaging, and the embolic stroke progressed without reperfusion therapy.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on a benign chiropractor-visit story and his young age, the evolving carotid dissection stroke went unrecognized until the deficits became permanent — or fatal.
learningPoints: Cervical artery dissection is among the most common identifiable causes of ischemic stroke in adults under 45, and it is easily missed because early symptoms mimic benign neck strain or migraine.

A trivial-seeming mechanical trigger — chiropractic manipulation, sports with neck rotation/hyperextension, even vigorous coughing — can tear the arterial wall hours to days before stroke symptoms appear.
Ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis without anhidrosis) and pulsatile tinnitus are classic, frequently overlooked clues pointing specifically to carotid dissection.
Age alone should never remove stroke from the differential — a young, previously healthy patient with headache/neck pain plus new focal deficits needs a stroke workup.
CT or MR angiography of the head AND neck, not the head alone, is required to diagnose dissection, and dissection does not preclude standard acute stroke therapies like thrombolysis or thrombectomy.
missedOpportunities: A focused exam for Horner syndrome (ptosis/miosis) and pulsatile tinnitus/carotid bruit
Emergent stroke code activation with noncontrast CT head plus CTA (or MRA) of the head AND neck
Evaluation for IV thrombolysis and/or mechanical thrombectomy once hemorrhage was excluded
Antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) for secondary stroke prevention
Admission to a stroke unit with vascular neurology and blood pressure management per stroke protocol rather than aggressive lowering

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The pain in my back has been getting worse for about five days now, right in the middle. Started as an ache, now it's constant and I've had fevers on and off. My legs started feeling heavy and weak yesterday.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've got diabetes, don't always keep up with my insulin. No surgeries, no other problems that I know of.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Just my insulin, when I remember it. No allergies to any medicine.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Yeah, I inject. Been using for a few years. Last time was maybe three days ago, in my arm.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing and diaphoretic, guarding his back, reluctant to move or reposition on the stretcher. Alert and oriented, in visible discomfort.
Marked midline tenderness and mild fullness to palpation and percussion over the thoracolumbar spine (roughly T10-L2). Paraspinal muscle spasm. No overlying erythema, fluctuance, or draining sinus.

Bilateral lower extremity weakness, 3/5 hip flexion and knee extension symmetrically, with reduced patellar and Achilles reflexes bilaterally. Patchy decreased sensation to light touch below approximately the T10 level. Upper extremity strength and sensation fully intact.
Diminished rectal tone on digital exam. Decreased perianal (saddle) sensation to light touch bilaterally. Bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 650 mL after attempted voiding.

Multiple healed and a few fresh needle track marks over both antecubital fossae and forearms; no surrounding erythema or fluctuance at injection sites currently. No skin breakdown or wound over the back.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Inflammatory markers are back and they're both way up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Two sets of blood cultures drawn from separate sites and sent before any antibiotics.

diagnosticReports.title: Thoracolumbar Spine X-ray
diagnosticReports.body: Thoracolumbar vertebral bodies maintain normal height and alignment. Mild multilevel degenerative endplate spurring and disc space narrowing. No fracture, listhesis, or paraspinal soft tissue mass on radiographs. IMPRESSION: Mild degenerative changes, no acute fracture; MRI recommended if clinically indicated.

image.query: lumbar spine x-ray mild degenerative changes no acute fracture

diagnosticReports.title: MRI Spine, Whole Spine With and Without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Loculated, rim-enhancing fluid collection in the posterior epidural space extending from approximately T9 through L2, producing significant mass effect on the thecal sac with abnormal cord signal at T11-T12 concerning for early cord compromise. Adjacent T10-T11 vertebral body and disc space enhancement suggesting associated discitis-osteomyelitis. No paraspinal extension identified. IMPRESSION: Posterior epidural fluid collection causing cord compression with associated discitis-osteomyelitis, findings worrisome for an infectious/abscess process and representing a surgical urgency.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Dexamethasone ordered without a dose — specify (e.g., 10 mg IV).

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — fever, IV drug use, and new leg weakness on top of the back pain. Treating this as a spinal emergency, not routine back pain.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Copy — no discharge, no muscle relaxants. We're keeping him here and working this up as an emergent infectious/surgical process.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neurosurgery — with progressive weakness and a full bladder this is time-critical. We're booking the OR now for emergent decompression.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: ID here. Continue vancomycin plus the antipseudomonal empirically — we'll narrow once cultures and any surgical specimens speciate.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's headed up with neurosurgery for emergent decompression — antibiotics continuing en route.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Wait — fever, IV drug use, and new bilateral leg weakness with urinary retention are red flags. Sending him home now risks permanent paralysis.

deterioration.trajectory: spinal epidural abscess with early cord compression — untreated, the neurologic deficit progresses toward permanent paraplegia and the underlying infection can progress to sepsis and death without emergent MRI, antibiotics, and surgical decompression

stages.event: Progressive lower extremity weakness and spreading numbness
stages.nurse: Doctor, his legs are getting weaker by the minute and the numbness is spreading up his body — what's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Delaying emergent imaging lets the compression progress; the window to reverse the neurologic deficit is closing fast.

stages.event: Worsening sepsis with progression toward complete paraplegia
stages.nurse: He's spiking again and now he can't move his legs at all — I need orders right now.
stages.feedback: Without source control — empiric antibiotics and urgent surgical decompression — both the infection and the neurologic deficit progress toward permanent injury.

stages.event: Septic shock and cardiovascular collapse
stages.nurse: He's unresponsive — no pulse, he's arrested.
stages.feedback: The untreated spinal epidural abscess progressed to septic shock and cardiovascular collapse; emergent antibiotics and surgical decompression were the only path to survival.

debrief.goodOutcome: Spinal epidural abscess recognized and treated before permanent neurologic injury.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the red-flag back pain, performed a focused neuro exam including saddle sensation, rectal tone, and post-void residual, obtained an emergent MRI, sent ESR/CRP and blood cultures, started empiric vancomycin plus antipseudomonal coverage after cultures, and got neurosurgery emergently for decompression.
debrief.outcomePoor: Recognition, emergent imaging, empiric antibiotics, or urgent neurosurgical decompression was delayed, or the pain was treated as mechanical and he was sent home.
debrief.badOutcome: The cord compression progressed to permanent paraplegia, and the untreated infection advanced to septic shock and death.
learningPoints: Think spinal epidural abscess with back pain plus fever and/or a neurologic deficit, especially in IV drug use or diabetes.
The classic triad is often incomplete — check ESR/CRP and get an MRI rather than waiting for all three findings.
Neurologic deficits are time-critical: emergent surgical decompression is what preserves function.
Empiric coverage includes MRSA (vancomycin) plus gram-negative/antipseudomonal coverage, drawn after blood cultures.
missedOpportunities: A focused neurologic exam with saddle sensation, rectal tone, and post-void residual
Emergent MRI of the spine
Empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin plus antipseudomonal coverage) sent after blood cultures
Urgent neurosurgery consultation for decompression

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I was skating and my board slid out on a curb — I guess I hit my head on the side of it. I don't really remember that part.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Nothing. I'm healthy, never had surgery, never had a head injury before this.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I don't take anything regularly, no allergies, definitely not on any blood thinners.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I had two beers earlier tonight before we went skating, that's it. I'm not drunk, I promise.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Bystanders said he was out cold maybe twenty, thirty seconds, then he sat up on his own and started talking — a little confused for a second but then he was fine.

speech.text: Yeah, it was quick. He was joking with us within a minute or two.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I threw up once right after it happened but I've been fine since. Headache's like a 3 out of 10, dull, right side.

physicalExam.finding: Alert, oriented to person/place/time, GCS 15 (E4V5M6). Interactive, joking with his roommate, sitting up on the stretcher without distress.

Boggy, tender scalp hematoma over the right temporoparietal region with a small overlying abrasion; no active bleeding. No Battle sign, no raccoon eyes, no hemotympanum, TMs clear, no CSF rhinorrhea or otorrhea.

Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal, round, and briskly reactive to light. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor 5/5 in all four extremities, sensation intact, no pronator drift, gait steady and coordinated.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Repeating his GCS and pupil checks now — I'll keep reassessing him at frequent intervals rather than just charting the one normal exam.

diagnosticReports.title: CT Head without Contrast
diagnosticReports.body: Biconvex, lens-shaped hyperdense extra-axial collection along the right temporoparietal convexity measuring approximately 3.5 x 2.2 cm, not crossing the coronal or squamosal suture lines, with mild leftward mass effect on the underlying cortex and no midline shift. Overlying nondisplaced linear fracture through the right squamous temporal bone. No intraventricular extension, no additional intracranial hemorrhage. IMPRESSION: Findings consistent with an acute right temporoparietal epidural hematoma with an associated temporal bone fracture and mild mass effect; recommend emergent neurosurgical evaluation.

image.query: noncontrast axial head CT showing biconvex right temporoparietal epidural hematoma with temporal bone fracture

diagnosticReports.body: No fracture, subluxation, or malalignment of the cervical spine. Prevertebral soft tissues are normal in thickness. Vertebral body heights and disc spaces are preserved. IMPRESSION: No acute cervical spine injury.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Agreed — a normal exam right now doesn't rule this out. We'll keep him monitored on frequent checks rather than sending him home.

elseSpeech.speaker: nurse
elseSpeech.text: They're going to want imaging in hand before they'll come down — let's get the head CT first and call them back.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neurosurgery here — we see the scan. Biconvex bleed with the temporal fracture, this needs the OR before it expands any further. We're coming down now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Head of bed up to 30 degrees and hypertonic saline running now while we wait on the OR.

dose.flagIfUnspecified: Hyperosmolar dosing not specified — mannitol 1 g/kg IV bolus or 3% hypertonic saline, plus head of bed elevated to 30 degrees.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — no corticosteroids on the order set. I'll flag it if anyone reaches for them.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Hold on — corticosteroids haven't shown benefit in traumatic intracranial hemorrhage and can raise his glucose without lowering ICP here. Focus on getting him to the OR instead.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Acetaminophen given for the headache — holding NSAIDs and opioids for now given the head injury.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: OR's ready and anesthesia's aware — he's headed up now for emergent evacuation.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity, lucid-interval trap — a normal GCS 15 on arrival masks an expanding arterial epidural bleed; without early CT, serial reassessment, and emergent surgical evacuation the patient herniates and dies

stages.event: Headache worsening and repeat vomiting despite reassuring interaction
stages.nurse: Doctor, his headache's climbing and he just vomited again, but he's still cracking jokes with his roommate — are we any closer to a plan?
stages.feedback: Without an early head CT despite the reassuring exam, the lucid interval keeps masking an expanding bleed and the warning signs are dismissed as 'he's fine.'

stages.event: New right-sided pupillary asymmetry and left-sided weakness — early herniation physiology
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's getting harder to keep awake and his right pupil looks different than it did an hour ago — where are we on this?
stages.feedback: Without imaging already in hand and neurosurgery already engaged, the hematoma keeps expanding and early signs of uncal herniation go unrecognized.

stages.event: Fixed and dilated right pupil, Cushing's triad, and agonal respirations — brainstem herniation
stages.nurse: Doctor, his breathing just went irregular and I can't get a pressure I trust anymore — I need help in here now!
stages.feedback: Without emergent surgical evacuation and ICP-directed therapy while awaiting the OR, the expanding epidural hematoma progressed to uncal and brainstem herniation.

debrief.goodOutcome: Epidural hematoma identified during the lucid interval and evacuated before herniation.
debrief.outcomeGood: You didn't let a reassuring GCS 15 and his joking, ambulatory presentation talk you out of imaging — you obtained an early head CT, kept reassessing him on frequent neuro checks, recognized the lucid interval for what it was, got neurosurgery involved emergently, and covered rising ICP while awaiting the OR. He went to evacuation before herniating.
debrief.outcomePoor: The reassuring initial exam and his own insistence that he felt fine delayed imaging and neurosurgical involvement, and the hematoma expanded to herniation.
debrief.badOutcome: A normal single neuro exam was mistaken for a benign injury, delaying the CT and neurosurgery until the expanding epidural hematoma herniated.
learningPoints: A lucid interval — transient normal or near-normal neurologic status after head trauma — is a classic and dangerous feature of epidural hematoma and must never be mistaken for a benign injury.
Roughly half of patients with a GCS of 14 or less after head trauma have a traumatic intracranial hemorrhage, so a reassuring initial exam does not exclude a surgical bleed.
An expanding epidural hematoma raises intracranial pressure per the Monro-Kellie hypothesis, and once compensatory mechanisms are exhausted, deterioration to herniation can be abrupt and rapid.
A blown pupil and Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) are late signs of herniation, not early warnings — don't wait for them before acting.
Because epidural hematomas cause mass effect with relatively little primary brain injury, prompt neurosurgical evacuation before herniation offers an excellent prognosis.
missedOpportunities: Non-contrast head CT despite a reassuring GCS 15
Serial neurologic reassessments (GCS and pupils) rather than a single normal exam
Recognizing the lucid interval instead of being falsely reassured by interval improvement
Emergent neurosurgery consultation and surgical evacuation
Head-of-bed elevation and hyperosmolar therapy for rising ICP while awaiting the OR

Left-sided hemisensory loss and extinction to double simultaneous stimulation. Dysarthric but names objects and follows midline commands correctly — language intact. Pupils equal and reactive.
Irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses intact and symmetric. No carotid bruits.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Understood — holding all antiplatelets until the head CT is back and the thrombolysis decision is finalized.

speech.speaker: consultant
speech.text: Neurology here — right M1 occlusion, she's a thrombectomy candidate. Keep pushing the lytic decision and get interventional on the phone.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's admitted to the neuro ICU, post-reperfusion stroke protocol running — hourly neuro checks.

stages.event: Delay to imaging while the infarct core grows
stages.nurse: Doctor, her left side looks even weaker than when she rolled in and she's more agitated — are we doing anything else right now?
stages.feedback: Every minute without the noncontrast head CT and CTA delays the reperfusion decision — infarct core grows while imaging waits.

stages.event: Expanding right MCA infarct with rising mass effect
stages.nurse: Her right pupil looks sluggish now and she's harder to arouse by the minute — what's the hold-up?
stages.feedback: Without thrombolysis or thrombectomy the M1 occlusion persists — the completed infarct is swelling enough to raise intracranial pressure.

stages.event: Uncal herniation from malignant MCA infarction
stages.nurse: She's unresponsive, her right pupil's blown, and she's barely breathing — she's herniating right now!
stages.feedback: Reperfusion was never achieved — the completed right MCA infarct swelled enough to herniate. Time is brain; the delay was fatal.

debrief.goodOutcome: LVO stroke recognized and reperfused within window; she is admitted to the neuro ICU with the vessel open.
debrief.badOutcome: Reperfusion (thrombolysis and/or thrombectomy) was never achieved, and the completed right MCA infarct swelled enough to herniate.
debrief.outcomeGood: You activated a stroke alert with a clear last-known-well, excluded hypoglycemia, scored the NIHSS, obtained emergent noncontrast CT plus CTA/perfusion, gave IV thrombolysis and pursued thrombectomy for the M1 occlusion, kept blood pressure permissive rather than over-treated, and withheld aspirin until hemorrhage was excluded and the lytic decision was made.
debrief.outcomePoor: Imaging or reperfusion was delayed, blood pressure was over-lowered pre-lytic, or aspirin was given before hemorrhage was excluded and the lytic decision was finalized.
learningPoints: Time is brain — expedite imaging and reperfusion; establish last-known-well early.
Always check glucose; hypoglycemia is a classic stroke mimic.
Thrombolysis plus thrombectomy for eligible LVO; don't over-lower BP and starve the penumbra.
Withhold antiplatelets until hemorrhage is excluded and the thrombolysis decision is finalized.
missedOpportunities: Fingerstick glucose to exclude a hypoglycemic mimic
Emergent noncontrast head CT plus CTA/CT perfusion
IV thrombolysis and endovascular thrombectomy for the M1 occlusion
Permissive blood pressure management instead of aggressive pre-lytic lowering

deterioration.trajectory: known metabolic disease decompensating under a catabolic trigger — without dextrose to halt metabolite production, ammonia climbs and mental status deteriorates toward coma

stages.event: Lily stops responding to her name and just moans; her breathing looks more labored than on arrival.
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's more out of it than when we walked in and her breathing's worse — what's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Without confirming the metabolic crisis (ammonia, blood gas) and starting dextrose-containing fluids to reverse the catabolic state, she keeps decompensating.

stages.event: She's no longer responding to voice, only withdrawing to painful stimuli; repeat labs show ammonia climbing further.
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's completely unresponsive now, I need direction immediately.
stages.feedback: Ongoing catabolism without dextrose, infection control, and metabolic/genetics guidance let toxic metabolites and ammonia accumulate unchecked, producing cerebral edema and cardiorespiratory collapse.

debrief.goodOutcome: Metabolic decompensation recognized and reversed with dextrose, infection sought and treated, genetics/metabolic engaged, and Lily transferred to the PICU stable and improving.
debrief.badOutcome: Treated as routine dehydration from gastroenteritis, the underlying metabolic decompensation went unaddressed and she progressed to unresponsiveness and cardiorespiratory collapse.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized her known metabolic disease was decompensating, sent ammonia and a blood gas confirming the crisis, started dextrose-containing fluids to halt the catabolism, sent cultures before covering empirically for a precipitating infection, and looped in metabolic/genetics early — she stabilized and went to the PICU improving.
debrief.outcomePoor: The vomiting and tachycardia were managed as simple dehydration; without dextrose to reverse the catabolic state or a search for a precipitant, ammonia climbed unchecked and she deteriorated to unresponsiveness.
learningPoints: A known inborn error of metabolism (e.g., propionic acidemia) presenting with vomiting and lethargy is a distinct, can't-miss entry in the ALOC differential (MOVESTUPID) — treat it as a metabolic emergency, not routine dehydration.
Send glucose, ammonia, venous blood gas, and lactate immediately in a known metabolic patient with altered mental status; an elevated anion gap and hyperammonemia confirm decompensation.
IV dextrose-containing fluids reverse the catabolic state driving toxic metabolite accumulation — plain maintenance or bolus fluids alone do not.
Infection is the most common precipitant of a metabolic crisis; send cultures and cover empirically while the metabolic workup proceeds in parallel.
Early metabolic/genetics involvement guides protein restriction and disease-specific therapy that general resuscitation alone will miss.
missedOpportunities: Sending an ammonia level and venous blood gas/lactate on a known metabolic-disease patient with vomiting and lethargy
Starting IV dextrose-containing fluids rather than plain maintenance fluids
Drawing cultures and covering empirically for a precipitating infection
Consulting metabolic/genetics early to guide protein restriction

deterioration.trajectory: abrupt-onset posterior circulation ischemia with risk of progressive cerebellar edema and basilar occlusion — the risk is anchoring on 'benign vertigo' and losing the thrombolysis/thrombectomy window

stages.event: Anchoring on 'benign vertigo' — the chart still just reads dizzy.
stages.nurse: He's still complaining the room's spinning and Mom's asking about discharge — where are we on a plan, doctor?
stages.feedback: A rushed exam anchored on inner-ear vertigo misses the diplopia, slurred speech, and limb ataxia that separate a benign cause from something more dangerous in the brainstem.

stages.event: The initial head CT came back 'unremarkable' and no one has pushed further imaging or worked through his bleeding risk.
stages.nurse: He's getting harder to rouse and the right side of his face looks off now, doctor — Mom wants to know what the actual plan is.
stages.feedback: A normal early CT does not rule out a posterior fossa problem — the window to find the vascular lesion behind his symptoms and confirm he's safe for thrombolysis is closing.

stages.event: Brainstem compression progresses and his respiratory drive fails.
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's not protecting his airway and his breathing's gone erratic — I need help in here right now!
stages.feedback: Without emergent transfer to a center capable of definitive vascular treatment and close neurosurgical/ICU monitoring, expanding posterior fossa pressure progressed to brainstem failure.

debrief.goodOutcome: Posterior circulation stroke recognized despite a reassuring early CT; thrombolysis eligibility calculated and contraindications screened, neurology and neurosurgery engaged, and emergent transfer to an endovascular-capable center arranged.
debrief.outcomeGood: You looked past 'just vertigo,' found the diplopia, dysarthria, and ataxia on exam, pushed past the falsely reassuring CT to get vascular imaging, worked the thrombolysis window correctly, and got him to definitive care before he decompensated.
debrief.outcomePoor: The workup stayed anchored on benign vertigo, the falsely reassuring CT was accepted at face value, and the window for thrombolysis and transfer to an endovascular-capable center closed as his posterior fossa pressure climbed.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on inner-ear vertigo and a normal early CT, the vertebrobasilar occlusion progressed unrecognized to brainstem compression and respiratory failure.
learningPoints: Vertigo is one of the hardest symptoms to source in the ED — keep posterior circulation ischemia on the differential, especially with accompanying diplopia, dysarthria, or limb/gait incoordination.
A reassuring exam and a normal early noncontrast CT do not exclude posterior circulation stroke; cerebellar and brainstem infarcts are easy to miss on initial imaging.
Extended IV rt-PA eligibility (up to 4.5 hours from last-known-well) applies to posterior circulation strokes just as it does to anterior circulation strokes.
Vertebrobasilar large-vessel occlusion is a time-critical endovascular thrombectomy target — delayed transfer to a capable center is a preventable, named pitfall.
Untreated posterior circulation stroke can progress to cerebellar edema with obstructive hydrocephalus or basilar occlusion with brainstem infarction and locked-in syndrome.
missedOpportunities: A focused neuro exam checking for diplopia, dysarthria, and limb/gait ataxia before anchoring on benign vertigo
Emergent CT angiography or MRI after a falsely reassuring noncontrast CT
Pinning down the exact last-known-well time to calculate IV rt-PA eligibility
Screening thrombolytic contraindications rather than withholding treatment out of generalized fear of bleeding
Immediate neurology consultation and emergent transfer to an endovascular-capable stroke center

speech.speaker: patient

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable, holding his head with both hands, wincing at overhead lights, guarding against movement. Alert and oriented but visibly distressed, non-toxic-appearing.

Irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with known atrial fibrillation, tachycardic. No murmurs. Pulses intact and symmetric throughout.

Ventricles are top-normal in size without significant enlargement. No midline shift, no extra-axial fluid collection. Calvarium intact. deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity: a missed or delayed diagnosis risks catastrophic rebleed and hydrocephalus; delayed BP control, analgesia, and nimodipine raise the risk of rebleed and vasospasm-related morbidity; delayed aneurysm securing can be fatal

stages.event: Repeat vomiting with worsening headache and decreasing responsiveness
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's vomited again and he's drowsier than when he came in — are we getting any imaging on him?
stages.feedback: A thunderclap headache maximal at onset is a rupture until proven otherwise — emergent noncontrast head CT should not be delayed.

stages.event: Uncontrolled hypertension with worsening agitation and pain
stages.nurse: His pressure keeps climbing and he's thrashing around in pain — can we get something on board for that?
stages.feedback: Uncontrolled hypertension and untreated vasospasm risk raise the chance of rebleeding and poor outcome; BP control, analgesia, and nimodipine are time-critical.

stages.event: Sudden loss of consciousness with a blown right pupil and decerebrate posturing — catastrophic rebleed with herniation
stages.nurse: Doctor, he just seized and stopped responding! His right pupil's blown!
stages.feedback: Without emergent imaging and urgent neurosurgical involvement to secure the aneurysm, a catastrophic rebleed with herniation becomes fatal.

debrief.goodOutcome: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage recognized early, confirmed on imaging, and the aneurysm secured before rebleeding.
debrief.outcomeGood: You treated the thunderclap headache as a bleed until proven otherwise, got emergent CT and confirmatory imaging, controlled blood pressure and pain, reversed his supratherapeutic anticoagulation, started nimodipine, and got neurosurgery involved before he rebled — the outcome that matters most in SAH.
debrief.outcomePoor: The headache wasn't imaged and treated urgently enough, and he suffered a catastrophic rebleed with herniation before the aneurysm could be secured.
debrief.badOutcome: Delay in diagnosis or definitive care allowed a preventable rebleed and herniation.
learningPoints: A thunderclap headache maximal at onset is SAH until excluded — image, don't discharge.
Noncontrast CT is highly sensitive early; LP for xanthochromia or CTA is used to confirm or localize the source, including after a positive CT.
Anticoagulated patients with SAH need urgent reversal to prevent hematoma expansion — check coags before reversing.
Nimodipine improves outcomes by mitigating vasospasm; NSAIDs/antiplatelets should be avoided in a fresh hemorrhage.
Definitive treatment is securing the aneurysm (clip or coil) — involve neurosurgery early.
missedOpportunities: Emergent noncontrast head CT
Checking coagulation status and reversing supratherapeutic warfarin
Blood pressure control to a moderate target with analgesia/antiemetics
Nimodipine for vasospasm prophylaxis
Urgent neurosurgery/interventional neuroradiology consultation for aneurysm securing

deterioration.trajectory: generalized convulsive status epilepticus — delayed or under-dosed benzodiazepines allow refractory status and hypoxic injury to progress toward arrest

stages.event: Convulsions ongoing without adequate first-line treatment; desaturating despite airway positioning.
stages.nurse: He's still seizing, eighteen minutes in, and his sats are down in the 80s — I need direction now.
stages.feedback: Convulsive status epilepticus can't wait for a full workup — a prompt, adequately dosed benzodiazepine is the first move.

stages.event: Seizure activity persists despite first-line therapy — refractory status.
stages.nurse: He's been seizing on and off for forty minutes now — this isn't stopping on its own.
stages.feedback: Refractory convulsive status needs a second-line agent loaded promptly — every extra minute deepens the hypoxic injury.

stages.event: Prolonged refractory status leads to cardiorespiratory arrest.
stages.nurse: He's not breathing and I don't have a pulse — I need you right now!
stages.feedback: Refractory status epilepticus left unchecked caused prolonged hypoxia and cardiorespiratory arrest — anesthetic infusion and airway control were needed well before this point.

debrief.goodOutcome: Prompt, adequately dosed benzodiazepines followed by timely second-line therapy controlled the seizure before neuronal injury or arrest.
debrief.outcomeGood: You protected his airway, checked glucose, and gave an adequately dosed benzodiazepine that you didn't hesitate to repeat — when that still wasn't enough, you loaded a second-line agent and had anesthesia ready to escalate. Causes were investigated in parallel. He came through it without arresting.
debrief.outcomePoor: Benzodiazepines were delayed, under-dosed, or never repeated, so the seizure ground on uncontrolled — the escalation ladder to second-line therapy and airway control never happened in time.
debrief.badOutcome: Refractory convulsive status epilepticus went inadequately treated, leading to prolonged hypoxia and cardiorespiratory arrest.
learningPoints: Treat convulsive status early with an adequately dosed benzodiazepine — under-dosing is the most common failure.
Check glucose immediately; hypoglycemia is a reversible cause that must be excluded.
If benzodiazepines fail, promptly load a second-line agent, then escalate to an anesthetic infusion and intubation for refractory status.
Investigate for precipitants in parallel — missed medication, electrolytes, infection, toxins, and intracranial process — without delaying treatment.
missedOpportunities: Giving an adequately dosed (and, if needed, repeated) first-line benzodiazepine promptly
Escalating to a second-line agent when seizures persisted
Escalating to anesthetic infusion and intubation for refractory status
