deterioration.trajectory: hypotensive thunderclap headache from unrecognized pituitary apoplexy — untreated adrenal crisis progresses to refractory shock and death

stages.event: Undetected hypoglycemia/hyponatremia pattern
stages.nurse: Doctor, her pressure keeps sagging and she's more drowsy every time I check on her — I need direction here.
stages.feedback: Without early glucose and electrolyte screening, the endocrine pattern driving her hypotension goes unrecognized.

stages.event: Refractory hypotension and worsening mentation
stages.nurse: Doctor, fluids aren't holding her pressure and she's barely rousable now. What are we doing?
stages.feedback: Untreated secondary adrenal insufficiency progresses to refractory hypotension; empiric stress-dose hydrocortisone and urgent neurosurgical/endocrine involvement cannot wait for imaging confirmation.

stages.event: Circulatory collapse
stages.nurse: Doctor, we're losing her pressure completely and she's not responding to voice anymore!
stages.feedback: Unrecognized and untreated adrenal crisis proceeded to circulatory collapse and death; stress-dose hydrocortisone plus emergent neurosurgery/endocrinology involvement must be initiated on clinical suspicion, not delayed for confirmatory imaging.

debrief.goodOutcome: Pituitary apoplexy recognized as an endocrine emergency — MRI obtained after appropriately excluding SAH, stress-dose hydrocortisone given, neurosurgery and endocrinology engaged urgently, permanent vision loss and cardiovascular collapse prevented.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on a clean head CT and LP as an atypical migraine, the visual field deficit and endocrine emergency went unrecognized and she deteriorated into worsening vision loss and refractory hypotensive shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You screened for the endocrine emergency early, performed a full neuro-ophthalmologic exam that caught the bitemporal visual field cut, appropriately excluded SAH with CT and LP, recognized the workup wasn't finished, obtained a pituitary-protocol MRI, and treated the adrenal crisis with empiric hydrocortisone while looping in neurosurgery and endocrinology — pituitary apoplexy caught and treated before permanent harm.
debrief.outcomePoor: The negative CT and LP were treated as the end of the workup. Without a formal visual field exam or recognition of the hypoglycemia/hyponatremia pattern, the pituitary apoplexy went unrecognized until she deteriorated.
learningPoints: "Worst headache of my life" and thunderclap onset are sensitive but not specific for SAH — 77% of patients WITHOUT SAH also describe it that way

A negative noncontrast CT and negative LP rule out SAH but do not close the secondary-headache workup — MRI/MRV brain is needed for pituitary apoplexy, venous sinus thrombosis, and hypertensive encephalopathy

Pituitary apoplexy classically presents as thunderclap headache plus visual field deficit plus endocrine collapse (hypotension, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia) — treat it as an endocrine emergency, not just a headache
Response to migraine-specific medication (triptans, NSAIDs, neuroleptics) does not exclude a dangerous secondary cause — SAH and structural lesions can also respond to these drugs (ACEP Level C)
Do not anchor on a benign headache history — patients without a migraine history, or with an old 'incidental' pituitary finding, still need a full red-flag-driven workup
missedOpportunities: Fingerstick glucose and BMP screening for an adrenal-insufficiency pattern early in the visit
Formal confrontation visual field and extraocular movement testing, not just a gross bedside check
Recognizing that a negative CT and LP do not end the workup when visual field deficit, hypotension, and electrolyte derangement persist
Pituitary-protocol MRI/MRV brain once SAH was excluded
Empiric stress-dose IV hydrocortisone and urgent neurosurgery/endocrinology consultation

speech.speaker: consultant

speech.speaker: patient

speech.speaker: nurse

deterioration.trajectory: hemodynamically stable, diagnostic case — the risk is premature stroke-alert activation or an incomplete red-flag screen, not physiologic deterioration

stages.event: Team pressure toward an emergent stroke workup with an incomplete screen
stages.nurse: Doctor, the charge nurse and the stroke team keep asking if this is a stroke alert or not — I need something to tell them.
stages.feedback: Committing to a decision before finishing the focal-deficit exam, the trauma/seizure/psychiatric screen, and a glucose check risks either an unnecessary emergent workup or, worse, missing a genuine stroke or seizure mimic.

debrief.goodOutcome: Classic, red-flag-negative TGA recognized through a witnessed history and a thorough exam; observed safely without unnecessary emergent intervention until the amnesia resolved.
debrief.badOutcome: The workup stalled on the patient's own unreliable account, red flags were never formally excluded, and the encounter drifted toward reflexive stroke-alert activation or premature discharge.
debrief.outcomeGood: You got the witnessed history from her husband, ran a full focal-deficit neuro exam, screened out head trauma, seizure, and psychiatric history, checked her glucose, and watched her amnesia gradually resolve before finalizing the diagnosis — stood down the stroke alert appropriately instead of reflexively escalating.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without a witnessed account and a completed red-flag screen, the team couldn't confidently rule out stroke or seizure, and the case never reached a comfortable, evidence-based disposition.
learningPoints: TGA is acute, isolated anterograde amnesia lasting up to 24 hours (mean 4-6 hours) with preserved orientation to person and place and no other neurologic deficits.
The patient herself is an unreliable historian during the episode — the onset, duration, and trigger must come from a witness.
Head trauma within 72 hours, known epilepsy, any other focal neurologic abnormality, or psychiatric history excludes TGA and should prompt a stroke/seizure workup instead.
The diagnosis cannot be confirmed until the amnesia has fully resolved — serial exams during observation are part of the diagnosis, not an afterthought.
Once red flags are excluded, the evidence-based approach is minimalist: observation, safety, and reassurance, not reflexive stroke-alert activation or thrombolysis.
missedOpportunities: Obtaining a witnessed history of onset, duration, and trigger from the husband
A complete focal-deficit neurologic exam
Screening for head trauma, seizure history, and psychiatric history
Checking a fingerstick glucose
Serial reassessment confirming spontaneous resolution before finalizing the diagnosis

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity: without magnesium the seizures recur; without prompt blood pressure control, sustained severe hypertension risks placental abruption and maternal intracranial hemorrhage, which is fatal if both are neglected

stages.event: Recurrent tonic-clonic convulsion at the bedside
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's shaking again and I can't get a pressure that isn't through the roof — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: Recurrent convulsions without magnesium sulfate on board leave her unprotected against further seizures and rising pressures.

stages.event: Sustained severe hypertension with new vaginal spotting and a dip on the fetal monitor
stages.nurse: Doctor, her pressure's still sky-high and now there's some vaginal bleeding and the fetal monitor's showing a dip — I need a real plan!
stages.feedback: Sustained severe hypertension raises the risk of placental separation and maternal stroke; it needs treatment independent of seizure control.

stages.event: Sudden unresponsiveness with a fixed, dilated pupil and irregular, gasping breathing — a catastrophic intracranial bleed
stages.nurse: Doctor, she just stopped responding and her pupil's blown — I need orders right now!
stages.feedback: Without magnesium to prevent further seizures and prompt control of severe hypertension, uncontrolled pressures progress to a fatal intracranial hemorrhage.

debrief.goodOutcome: The eclamptic seizure was controlled with magnesium sulfate, her severe hypertension was treated, and obstetrics delivered her before a maternal complication occurred.
debrief.badOutcome: Magnesium sulfate and blood pressure control were delayed or skipped, and uncontrolled severe hypertension progressed to a fatal intracranial hemorrhage.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized this as eclampsia, gave magnesium sulfate for seizure control and prophylaxis, treated her severe hypertension, protected her airway and positioned her left lateral, monitored for magnesium toxicity, worked up HELLP, monitored the fetus, and got obstetrics involved emergently for delivery — the definitive treatment.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without magnesium sulfate and timely blood pressure control, the seizures and hypertension went unchecked and she suffered a fatal intracranial hemorrhage before delivery could be arranged.
learningPoints: Magnesium sulfate — not standard antiepileptics — is the first-line treatment and prophylaxis for eclamptic seizures.
Treat severe hypertension (SBP >=160 or DBP >=110) promptly with IV labetalol, hydralazine, or nifedipine to prevent maternal stroke.
Delivery is the definitive cure for eclampsia; involve obstetrics emergently regardless of gestational age.
Watch for magnesium toxicity (loss of reflexes, respiratory depression) and keep calcium gluconate available as the antidote.
Screen for HELLP syndrome (platelets, LFTs, hemolysis markers) — it can coexist with and complicate severe preeclampsia.
missedOpportunities: Giving IV magnesium sulfate for seizure control and prophylaxis
Treating severe hypertension with IV labetalol, hydralazine, or nifedipine
Protecting the airway, giving oxygen, and positioning left lateral
Monitoring for magnesium toxicity and keeping calcium gluconate available
Consulting obstetrics emergently for delivery
Continuous fetal monitoring
Evaluating for HELLP syndrome

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity: without recognition beyond the borderline blood pressure, severe features and end-organ injury advance toward eclampsia, hepatic and hematologic decompensation, and placental abruption

stages.event: New visual disturbance (seeing spots) with a worsening frontal headache
stages.nurse: Doctor, she says her vision's gone spotty and her head's pounding worse than before, and I still don't have orders past the Zofran — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: New visual symptoms and a worsening headache are severe features of preeclampsia-spectrum disease; without recognizing this beyond the borderline blood pressure, the picture keeps sliding toward eclampsia unaddressed.

stages.event: Worsening RUQ pain with new spontaneous bruising and gum bleeding

stages.feedback: Without platelets, LFTs, and LDH with peripheral smear and urine protein sent, the hemolysis-elevated-liver-enzymes-low-platelets picture and proteinuria stay hidden while hepatic and hematologic injury advances toward DIC.

stages.event: Witnessed tonic-clonic seizure at the bedside followed by heavy vaginal bleeding concerning for placental abruption
stages.nurse: Doctor, she just started seizing and now there's heavy vaginal bleeding — I need orders right now!
stages.feedback: Without emergent obstetric involvement and magnesium prophylaxis, severe preeclampsia and HELLP progressed unchecked to eclampsia and placental abruption.

debrief.goodOutcome: Severe preeclampsia/HELLP was recognized despite the borderline blood pressure, the hemolysis/liver/platelet and proteinuria workup confirmed it, magnesium sulfate was started for seizure prophylaxis, and obstetrics was involved emergently for expedited delivery planning.
debrief.badOutcome: The picture was anchored on gallbladder disease or gastritis, the hemolysis/liver/platelet workup was never sent, and severe preeclampsia/HELLP progressed unrecognized toward eclampsia and hepatic/hematologic decompensation.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized severe preeclampsia-spectrum disease despite the borderline pressure, sent CBC with platelets, LFTs, LDH with peripheral smear, and urine protein, avoided anchoring on gallbladder disease, started magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, and got obstetrics involved emergently for delivery planning.
debrief.outcomePoor: The RUQ pain, headache, and malaise were treated as gallbladder disease or a viral illness, the hemolysis and proteinuria workup was never sent, and severe preeclampsia/HELLP progressed unrecognized toward eclampsia and placental abruption.
learningPoints: Severe preeclampsia and HELLP are defined by end-organ findings — RUQ/epigastric pain, headache, visual changes, thrombocytopenia, and hemolysis — not by how dramatic the blood pressure reading is.
A borderline blood pressure does not rule out severe preeclampsia-spectrum disease in a pregnant patient with concerning symptoms.
Send CBC with platelets, LFTs, LDH with peripheral smear, and urine protein whenever severe features are present, even with reassuring vitals.
RUQ/epigastric pain, nausea, and malaise late in pregnancy are easily mistaken for gastritis or gallbladder disease — actively exclude preeclampsia/HELLP first.
Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and emergent obstetric consultation for delivery planning should not wait for a 'classic' severe blood pressure reading.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing severe-preeclampsia-spectrum disease despite the borderline blood pressure
Sending CBC with platelets, LFTs, and LDH with peripheral smear
Checking urine protein for proteinuria
Consulting obstetrics emergently for delivery planning
Starting magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity postpartum hemorrhage from uterine atony — without fundal massage, uterotonics, tranexamic acid, and blood replacement, ongoing bleeding progresses to decompensated hemorrhagic shock and DIC, which is fatal if resuscitation is never started

stages.event: Bleeding remains brisk, fundus still boggy and uterus not contracting
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's still bleeding through everything and her pressure's dropping — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: Without fundal massage and uterotonics on board, uterine atony continues unchecked and blood loss accelerates.

stages.event: Persistent hemorrhage now with cool, mottled extremities and worsening confusion — decompensating hemorrhagic shock
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's getting confused and her hands are ice cold — I need blood and I need it now!
stages.feedback: Without large-bore access, blood products, and tranexamic acid, ongoing hemorrhage progresses toward decompensated shock and coagulopathy.

stages.event: Cardiovascular collapse from exsanguinating postpartum hemorrhage with disseminated intravascular coagulation

stages.feedback: Ongoing uncontrolled hemorrhage without fundal massage, uterotonics, tranexamic acid, and blood replacement progresses to fatal hemorrhagic shock and DIC.

debrief.goodOutcome: Uterine atony recognized and treated with fundal massage, uterotonics, and tranexamic acid, with the 4 Ts worked up and blood replaced — she stabilized and was taken up for definitive care.
debrief.badOutcome: Massage, uterotonics, tranexamic acid, and blood replacement were delayed or skipped, and uncontrolled hemorrhage progressed to fatal hemorrhagic shock and DIC.
debrief.outcomeGood: You placed large-bore IVs and crossmatched blood, performed fundal massage, gave uterotonics while avoiding carboprost in her asthma, gave tranexamic acid, worked up the 4 Ts with a genital tract exam, placenta inspection, coagulation studies, and bedside ultrasound, escalated to balloon tamponade when bleeding persisted, and got obstetrics involved for possible embolization or surgery.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without fundal massage, uterotonics, and tranexamic acid, uterine atony went unchecked and she progressed to hemorrhagic shock and DIC before the bleeding could be controlled.
learningPoints: Uterine atony is the leading cause of postpartum hemorrhage — treat with fundal massage plus uterotonics first.
Remember the 4 Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin — examine for retained placenta, lacerations, and coagulopathy.
Tranexamic acid reduces mortality in postpartum hemorrhage and should be given early.
Mind uterotonic contraindications: methylergonovine in hypertension, carboprost in asthma.
Escalate to balloon tamponade and obstetric consultation for embolization or surgery when bleeding is refractory to first-line measures.
missedOpportunities: Placing large-bore IVs, sending type and crossmatch, and transfusing blood products
Performing fundal massage
Giving uterotonics (oxytocin, methylergonovine, or misoprostol) while avoiding carboprost in asthma
Giving tranexamic acid
Examining for retained tissue, genital tract trauma, and coagulopathy
Escalating to balloon tamponade and obstetric consultation when refractory

deterioration.trajectory: well-appearing neutropenic patient who can decompensate into septic shock if antibiotics are delayed — door-to-antibiotic time within 1 hour is the metric

stages.event: Fever spikes, early tachycardia
stages.nurse: Doctor, her temp just spiked to 39.2 and she says she feels shaky — are we starting anything for this fever?
stages.feedback: Empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics should be running within the hour of a neutropenic fever — every extra minute waiting on labs raises the risk.

stages.event: Hypotension develops — early septic shock
stages.nurse: Her pressure just dropped to 88 over 54 and she's breathing faster — I need orders now.
stages.feedback: A well-appearing neutropenic patient can crash into septic shock quickly once a source goes uncovered by antibiotics.

stages.event: Cardiovascular collapse — septic shock unresponsive to fluids
stages.nurse: She's not responding to fluids and her pressure's in the 60s — we needed antibiotics an hour ago.
stages.feedback: Without empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics in the first hour, neutropenic fever progressed to fatal septic shock.

debrief.goodOutcome: Febrile neutropenia recognized as an oncologic emergency; empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics given within the hour and the patient stabilized for admission.
debrief.badOutcome: Antibiotics were delayed while the well-appearing patient and pending counts created false reassurance, and she progressed into septic shock.
debrief.outcomeGood: You treated the fever as an emergency from the moment she arrived, drew cultures peripherally and from the port, and had cefepime running well within the hour without waiting on the ANC — she stayed stable and went up to the floor on neutropenic precautions.
debrief.outcomePoor: The workup lingered on how well she looked and on pending labs, antibiotics never got started in time, and she deteriorated into septic shock.
learningPoints: Give empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics (cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) within 1 hour — don't wait for the ANC or for the patient to look sick.
Culture peripherally and from any indwelling line/port before antibiotics; avoid a digital rectal exam.
A well-appearing neutropenic patient can crash into septic shock quickly.
Add vancomycin for line infection, skin/soft-tissue source, mucositis, or hemodynamic instability.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing neutropenic fever as an emergency despite a well-appearing patient
Drawing peripheral and port blood cultures before antibiotics
Giving empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics within one hour without waiting on the ANC
Avoiding a digital rectal exam
Adding vancomycin and resuscitating/risk-stratifying for disposition

deterioration.trajectory: untreated migratory polyarthritis with evolving carditis progresses toward decompensated heart failure

stages.event: Increasing respiratory distress from evolving carditis

stages.feedback: Ongoing untreated rheumatic carditis progressed toward decompensated heart failure — anti-inflammatory therapy, penicillin, and cardiology involvement are needed before this point.

debrief.goodOutcome: Acute rheumatic fever recognized and treated before decompensated heart failure set in.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on a migrating joint complaint without recognizing carditis, the rheumatic fever progressed untreated toward heart failure.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized the migratory large-joint polyarthritis and disproportionate tachycardia in a high-risk child, obtained ASO/anti-DNase B titers and a full cardiac workup, treated the arthritis and carditis with anti-inflammatory therapy plus penicillin, and arranged cardiology follow-up with secondary prophylaxis — carditis caught and treated before lasting valve damage.
debrief.outcomePoor: The migratory pattern and cardiac findings went unrecognized or untreated, and the child's carditis and heart failure progressed without anti-inflammatory therapy, penicillin, or cardiology involvement.
learningPoints: Acute rheumatic fever causes migratory large-joint polyarthritis — one joint resolves as the next flares — unlike the additive, persistent pattern of poststreptococcal reactive arthritis
Tachycardia disproportionate to fever plus a new or evolving murmur should raise concern for rheumatic carditis, the manifestation with the greatest long-term risk
Pacific Islander, Maori, and Aboriginal Australian children, and those in Utah or Hawaii, have markedly higher ARF incidence and warrant a lower testing threshold
Diagnosis requires evidence of antecedent GAS infection (ASO or anti-DNase B titers) since throat culture is often negative by the time arthritis or carditis appears

Untreated or undertreated strep pharyngitis can trigger ARF two to three weeks later; missed diagnosis risks progression to valvular damage, underscoring the need for antibiotics plus secondary prophylaxis
missedOpportunities: Obtaining ASO/anti-DNase B titers instead of anchoring on a single negative throat swab
A careful cardiac exam and ECG/echocardiogram before or alongside starting anti-inflammatory therapy
Starting NSAID or aspirin therapy for the arthritis and carditis plus penicillin to eradicate residual GAS
Cardiology consultation, admission for monitoring, and long-term secondary penicillin prophylaxis

deterioration.trajectory: early meningococcemia with septic shock trending toward respiratory failure — worsens fast without early recognition, IV access, fluids, antibiotics, and a calm, well-prepared approach to any airway attempt

stages.event: Progressive septic shock — spreading rash, worsening perfusion
stages.nurse: His hands are ice cold now and he's even harder to rouse — what's the plan, doctor?
stages.feedback: Subtle early shock physiology — cool mottled extremities, weak cry, leg pain — was present from arrival; early recognition, IV access, fluids, and antibiotics couldn't wait for hypotension or desaturation to confirm it.

stages.event: Worsening shock compounded by repeated agitating handling
stages.nurse: He's screaming and thrashing every time we touch him and his pressure just tanked again — I need something now.
stages.feedback: Untreated septic shock plus repeated agitating exams accelerated the decompensation toward a fully crashed picture — fluid/antibiotic resuscitation and a calm, hands-off approach were both needed.

stages.event: Respiratory failure and cardiopulmonary arrest
stages.nurse: He's not breathing and I can't find a pulse — starting compressions!
stages.feedback: Without early resuscitation and a properly prepared, well-supported airway attempt, respiratory failure progressed to arrest — exactly the crash meticulous preparation and early treatment are meant to prevent.

debrief.goodOutcome: Early shock recognized and treated — IV/IO access, fluids, and empiric antibiotics given without delay; child kept calm and upright with a parent nearby; a deliberately prepared, positioned airway (or BVM as a bridge) avoided a crashed intubation; transferred to PICU stable.
debrief.outcomeGood: You caught the subtle early shock physiology instead of waiting for hypotension or desaturation, got IV access with fluids and empiric antibiotics running fast, kept him calm and upright on his parent's lap, and — when the airway needed attention — positioned for his large occiput, fully prepped (or bridged with BVM), and called in the most experienced provider. He stabilized and went to the PICU instead of a code.
debrief.badOutcome: Subtle shock signs were missed or treatment was delayed, agitating exams accelerated decompensation, and/or an unprepared airway attempt led to a complicated, crashed intubation.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter never caught the early shock physiology or delayed fluids and antibiotics, agitating handling pushed him toward decompensation, and the airway — if attempted — was rushed without positioning, preparation, or experienced backup.
learningPoints: Pediatric respiratory failure funnels through only a handful of final common pathways — recognize subtle early shock (grayish/mottled skin, weak cry, leg pain, mild retractions) rather than waiting for hypotension or desaturation.
Obtain IV/IO access and start fluids plus empiric antibiotics immediately once shock is suspected — don't wait for confirmatory labs.
Keep the child calm and upright with a parent nearby; agitating exams or separation can precipitate abrupt decompensation.
The large pediatric occiput passively flexes the neck supine — a shoulder roll or towel aligns the airway axis before any laryngoscopy attempt.

Prepare thoroughly before intubating (preoxygenation, working suction, correct equipment, a failed-airway backup plan) and call the most experienced available provider; bag-valve-mask performs comparably to intubation and is a reasonable temporizing bridge.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing subtle shock/impending respiratory failure signs instead of waiting for hypotension or desaturation
Early IV/IO access with fluid resuscitation and empiric antibiotics
Keeping him calm and upright with his parent, avoiding agitating exams
Shoulder-roll positioning for the large pediatric occiput before any laryngoscopy
Full airway preparation and the most experienced provider present (or BVM as a bridge) before intubating

stages.feedback: Without explicitly walking through and confirming all six AAP low-risk criteria, the workup drifts toward unnecessary imaging and admission instead of a clear, defensible low-risk discharge.

debrief.goodOutcome: Low-risk BRUE correctly identified and managed without unnecessary testing or admission.
debrief.outcomeGood: You took a complete event history, a thorough cardiac and neurologic exam, and methodically confirmed all six AAP low-risk criteria — recognizing that bystander CPR from a panicked parent doesn't count against her. She went home with family education and pediatric follow-up, sparing her an unnecessary workup.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter ended without ever explicitly walking through the six low-risk criteria, so the dramatic history pushed the workup toward CT, extensive labs, or admission she didn't need — or she was sent home before the criteria were actually confirmed.
debrief.badOutcome: The infant was over-tested and/or admitted despite meeting every AAP low-risk criterion, or discharged before those criteria were ever confirmed.
learningPoints: BRUE (brief resolved unexplained event) replaced the older, nebulous term ALTE and requires the event to have fully resolved with the infant back to baseline and no explanation found after history and exam

The 2016 AAP guideline defines six explicit criteria for a lower-risk BRUE: age >60 days; born at >=32 weeks' gestation with postconceptional age >=45 weeks; a first, isolated event; duration <1 minute; no CPR performed by a trained medical provider; and no concerning historical or exam findings
CPR performed by a frightened parent or bystander does not by itself upgrade an infant to higher-risk -- only CPR performed by a trained medical provider counts against the low-risk criteria

The AAP guideline gives management recommendations only for lower-risk infants; higher-risk infants (who fail any low-risk criterion or have concerning findings) require individualized further evaluation the guideline does not standardize

Unnecessary extensive testing and hospitalization in a truly low-risk infant carries real risk without a high likelihood of finding a treatable cause or preventing a future event -- the exact problem the old ALTE label caused
missedOpportunities: Confirming who actually performed the resuscitation (untrained parent versus a trained medical provider)
Explicitly walking through and documenting all six 2016 AAP low-risk BRUE criteria
Completing a focused cardiac and neurologic exam before applying the BRUE label
Engaging the family in shared decision-making with BRUE/CPR education and outpatient follow-up instead of admission

deterioration.trajectory: well-appearing postictal toddler, low-acuity diagnostic case — the risk is over-testing or missing a subtle exam change, not hemodynamic collapse

stages.event: Temp rebounds to 39.7C and Maya has a brief 15-second generalized shudder that resolves on its own before anyone reaches the bedside; she's fussy but arousable afterward.
stages.nurse: Doctor, her temp's climbing again and Mom's paged me twice — I need a plan for this kid, now.
stages.feedback: A focused neuro exam confirming she's back to full baseline, and an active search for what's driving the fever, are what keep this contained — leaning on the seizure count alone to justify the next step isn't enough.

debrief.goodOutcome: Complex febrile seizure correctly worked up and safely managed; bacterial meningitis excluded on history and exam, the fever source pursued, no unnecessary antiepileptic prophylaxis or reflexive neuroimaging, and the family counseled before discharge.
debrief.outcomeGood: You elicited exact seizure duration, focality, and recurrence count, confirmed a full return to baseline on serial neuro exam, screened for meningismus, bulging fontanelle, petechiae, immunization status, and antibiotic pretreatment, searched for the fever source instead of reflexively imaging or tapping her, skipped unnecessary antiepileptic prophylaxis, and counseled the family on recurrence risk before a safe discharge.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without a clear seizure history and a confirmed return to baseline, the visit drifted toward reflexive LP or head CT, or missed an exam change that actually mattered — and the family left without recurrence-risk counseling.
debrief.badOutcome: The seizure characteristics were never fully pinned down and a true return to baseline was never confirmed, so the workup either escalated into unnecessary invasive testing or missed a red flag that needed attention.
learningPoints: AAP 2011 criteria: simple febrile seizure is generalized, <15 minutes, once per 24 hours; complex febrile seizure meets any one of duration >=15 minutes, focal features, or recurrence more than once in 24 hours
Febrile seizures occur in children 6-60 months with temperature >38C and no CNS infection; about 25%-40% of children with a febrile seizure will have at least one recurrence
Certain viral illnesses, including HHV-6 (roseola) and influenza A, carry increased risk of triggering febrile seizures
Meeting complex criteria by duration or recurrence alone does not automatically mandate neuroimaging or lumbar puncture in a child who fully returns to neurologic baseline with a reassuring exam
Unvaccinated status and recent antibiotic exposure raise concern for occult or partially masked serious infection and should lower the threshold for further workup
Prophylactic antiepileptic medications are not used to prevent febrile seizure recurrence; family counseling is the mainstay of ED management
missedOpportunities: Eliciting exact seizure duration, focality, and recurrence count
A serial neuro exam confirming full return to baseline
Screening for meningismus, bulging fontanelle, petechiae, immunization status, and antibiotic pretreatment
Searching for the fever source (urinalysis, cultures) instead of reflexive neuroimaging or LP
Declining prophylactic antiepileptic medication
Counseling the family on recurrence risk and return precautions before discharge

physicalExam.finding: Crying but consolable in his mother's lap, non-toxic appearing, moving all extremities spontaneously. Bloodstained washcloth removed from the cheek to reveal the wound.

Buccal mucosa intact opposite the wound, no through-and-through intraoral laceration. Dentition intact, occlusion normal, no loose or avulsed teeth.

Symmetric facial movement — forehead wrinkle, eye closure, and smile intact bilaterally, no facial droop. No clear salivary leak from the wound on gentle palpation over the parotid region; duct appears uninvolved.
No other bite marks, scratches, or puncture wounds on the scalp, trunk, or extremities on full skin survey.
No bony step-off, crepitus, or malocclusion on palpation of the orbital rims, zygoma, maxilla, and mandible. Globes intact bilaterally, extraocular movements full.

IMPRESSION: No acute facial or skull fracture; soft tissue laceration as clinically evident, no foreign body seen on CT.

deterioration.trajectory: stable, low-acuity wound-care case — the risk is delayed/incorrect wound management or overtreatment, not hemodynamic collapse

stages.event: Wound care delay — tissue drying, family growing anxious
stages.nurse: Doctor, that dressing's been sitting for half an hour now and Mom keeps asking why nobody's actually touched the wound yet. Are we doing something soon?
stages.feedback: Delaying irrigation and debridement lets the wound edges dry out and devitalized tissue set in, narrowing the window for a good primary closure.

debrief.goodOutcome: The wound was irrigated and debrided, closed primarily, and covered with appropriate oral antibiotics; tetanus status was confirmed and a structured rabies risk assessment led to observation of the known dog instead of empiric prophylaxis.
debrief.badOutcome: The wound was left open or closure was delayed, antibiotics were withheld or unnecessarily escalated to IV/admission, and the family's anxiety about rabies drove reflexive prophylaxis instead of a structured risk assessment.
debrief.outcomeGood: You irrigated and debrided before closing, treated with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate, confirmed his tetanus status was current, and worked through the rabies risk assessment to arrange observation of the known dog — the correct, evidence-based plan.
debrief.outcomePoor: Wound management stalled or the rabies conversation short-circuited into reflexive prophylaxis, missing the structured assessment that would have shown observation was the right call.
learningPoints: Facial bite wounds have low infection risk from excellent vascularity and are generally closed primarily after thorough irrigation and debridement, unlike hand or puncture-heavy bites.
Children take a disproportionate share of dog bites to the head and face because of their smaller stature — up to 73% of bites in children under 9 are facial.

Prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) are reserved for higher-risk wounds — crush/devitalized tissue, deep punctures, hand/foot/joint location, or delayed presentation — not given reflexively to every bite.

Rabies PEP is unnecessary for a bite from a known, apparently healthy domestic dog that can be confined and observed for 10 days; reflexive prophylaxis driven by parental anxiety should be replaced with a structured risk assessment.

A dog's crushing bite force can fracture facial bones or the skull even when the visible wound looks minor, so head and face bites in small children warrant careful exam and imaging when the exam is at all suspicious.
missedOpportunities: Irrigating and debriding devitalized tissue before deciding on closure
Closing the facial wound primarily rather than leaving it open
Prescribing oral amoxicillin-clavulanate rather than withholding antibiotics or escalating to IV therapy
Confirming tetanus immunization status
Performing a structured rabies risk assessment instead of reflexively starting PEP
Arranging 10-day observation of the known, healthy-appearing dog

deterioration.trajectory: unrecognized or untreated severe symptomatic hyponatremia — recurrent seizures and cerebral edema progress toward herniation and respiratory arrest without hypertonic saline and airway/access support

stages.event: Recurrent seizure activity with worsening lethargy as cerebral edema progresses untreated.
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's jerking again and even floppier than before — what do you want to give her?
stages.feedback: Recurrent seizures from unchecked severe hyponatremia — this can't wait for a full sepsis workup to result before acting.

stages.event: Bradycardia and widening pulse pressure develop as cerebral edema progresses toward herniation.
stages.nurse: Her heart rate just crashed into the 50s and she's barely breathing on her own — I need a plan right now.
stages.feedback: Bradycardia with a widening pulse pressure and irregular respirations signals impending herniation — she needed sodium correction and airway/access support well before this point.

stages.event: Cardiorespiratory arrest from uncal herniation.
stages.nurse: She's not breathing and I don't have a pulse — starting compressions, I need you right now!
stages.feedback: Fatal herniation and respiratory arrest from untreated severe symptomatic hyponatremia — hypertonic saline and airway/access support could not be delayed this long.

debrief.goodOutcome: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia recognized and treated with hypertonic saline, airway and access secured, sodium corrected cautiously, the over-diluted formula uncovered and corrected, and the infant admitted to the PICU.
debrief.outcomeGood: You checked a stat glucose and electrolytes instead of anchoring on sepsis, recognized her seizures as severe symptomatic hyponatremia, gave hypertonic saline to stop them, suctioned her airway and got IV access despite difficult neonatal veins, corrected her sodium cautiously with serial rechecks, uncovered the over-diluted formula and counseled her father on safe mixing, and admitted her to the PICU.
debrief.outcomePoor: Hypertonic saline was delayed or never given, and recurrent seizures from worsening cerebral edema progressed to herniation and cardiorespiratory arrest.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchoring on a routine neonatal sepsis workup, the critically low sodium went untreated and her seizures progressed to herniation and arrest.
learningPoints: Seizures in a neonate are not always febrile seizures or sepsis — always check a stat glucose and sodium.
Improperly (over-)diluted powdered infant formula is a classic, preventable cause of acute hyponatremic seizures in young infants.
GI losses replaced with more hypotonic formula worsen hyponatremia and can precipitate cerebral edema and seizures.
Severe symptomatic hyponatremia is treated with hypertonic (3%) saline to acutely raise sodium and reverse cerebral edema, then corrected slowly to avoid osmotic demyelination.
Tachycardia is the primary compensatory sign of shock in infants — hypotension is a late, ominous finding.
missedOpportunities: Sending a stat glucose and basic metabolic panel/sodium instead of anchoring on a sepsis workup alone
Giving hypertonic (3%) saline promptly for the seizing, severely hyponatremic neonate
Suctioning the airway and obtaining IV/IO access early given difficult dehydrated neonatal veins
Rechecking sodium and correcting the deficit cautiously to avoid osmotic demyelination
Taking a careful feeding-preparation history and counseling the father on correct formula reconstitution
Admitting for PICU-level monitoring rather than a routine floor disposition

stages.feedback: Every calm, unagitated minute matters, but the window for a controlled intubation narrows the longer the airway team isn't mobilized.

stages.event: Worsening stridor, accessory muscle use, falling saturations
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's really struggling now and going quiet on me — I need something done immediately.
stages.feedback: Fatigue and a falling heart rate in a child working this hard to breathe are pre-arrest signs — the airway is running out of time.

stages.event: Complete airway obstruction and cardiopulmonary arrest
stages.nurse: Doctor — he's not breathing, he's unresponsive!
stages.feedback: Without a mobilized, experienced airway team to secure the airway under controlled conditions, the airway obstructed completely.

debrief.goodOutcome: Airway secured via controlled intubation in the OR by ENT/anesthesia with a surgical airway backup ready; antibiotics and steroids given only after; child transferred to PICU stable.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized this as an airway emergency, kept him calm and upright, avoided any ED throat exam, IV attempt, or supine positioning, gave blow-by oxygen, and mobilized ENT/anesthesia for a controlled intubation in the OR with a surgical airway backup — then treated with ceftriaxone and dexamethasone once the airway was secured.
debrief.badOutcome: Instrumentation, an IV attempt, or supine positioning in the ED agitated him before a controlled airway could be secured, and he progressed to complete obstruction and arrest.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter never achieved a secured airway before he decompensated — attempts at exam, IV access, or laying him flat (or simple delay) let him tire out and obstruct.
learningPoints: Keep the child calm and upright — agitation or airway instrumentation can precipitate sudden, complete obstruction.
Definitive airway management is a controlled intubation in the OR with ENT/anesthesia and a surgical airway backup ready.
Defer IV access, bloodwork, throat exam, and supine positioning until the airway is secured.
Classic picture: rapid onset, drooling, tripod position, muffled voice, toxic appearance, often in an incompletely immunized child.
missedOpportunities: Keeping the child calm and upright instead of attempting an ED throat exam, IV, or supine positioning
Mobilizing ENT/anesthesia early for a controlled intubation in the OR with surgical backup
Giving blow-by humidified oxygen without touching or agitating the child
Holding IV access, labs, and antibiotics until after the airway was secured

stages.event: Blood pressure climbs further on recheck and his headache worsens

stages.event: Sudden generalized seizure at the bedside — hypertensive encephalopathy

stages.feedback: Nearly an hour of untreated severe hypertension progressed to hypertensive encephalopathy with seizure — the outcome urgent blood pressure control was meant to prevent.

debrief.goodOutcome: Hypertension recognized as a high-risk finding and treated urgently, glomerular hematuria confirmed on urinalysis with microscopy, postinfectious glomerulonephritis confirmed with a low C3 and elevated strep serologies, and nephrology involved with admission for monitoring — hypertensive emergency averted.
debrief.badOutcome: The elevated blood pressure was never treated, and possibly worsened with aggressive IV fluids, so it progressed unchecked toward encephalopathy and seizure.
debrief.outcomeGood: You caught the elevated blood pressure as a high-risk finding on recheck, sent urinalysis with microscopy confirming glomerular bleeding with RBC casts, asked specifically about the timing of his preceding throat and skin infections, and confirmed postinfectious glomerulonephritis with a low C3 and elevated strep serologies. You treated his hypertension urgently with a diuretic and avoided aggressive IV fluids, then got nephrology on board and admitted him for close monitoring — he did well.
debrief.outcomePoor: The elevated blood pressure was noted but never treated, and his headache and hypertension kept worsening until he had a seizure.
learningPoints: Cola-colored macroscopic hematuria suggests upper urinary tract/glomerular bleeding; confirm with urinalysis and microscopy rather than assuming from color alone.
Hypertension in a child with hematuria is a high-risk feature that must prompt urgent evaluation and treatment, never reassurance.

Postinfectious glomerulonephritis follows a streptococcal throat or skin infection by one to three weeks via molecular mimicry, unlike IgA nephropathy's gross hematuria within one to two days of a URI.
A low C3 with a normal C4 supports a postinfectious process; RBC casts on urine microscopy confirm glomerular, not lower-tract, bleeding.
PSGN-associated hypertension is driven by sodium and water retention — treat with diuresis and fluid/sodium restriction rather than aggressive IV fluids, which can worsen volume overload.
missedOpportunities: Rechecking and recognizing the elevated blood pressure as a high-risk finding requiring urgent treatment
Sending urinalysis with microscopy to confirm RBCs, protein, and RBC casts
Asking specifically about the timing of a preceding throat or skin infection
Checking renal function, strep serologies, and complement C3
Treating hypertension urgently with a diuretic/antihypertensive while avoiding aggressive IV fluids
Consulting nephrology and admitting for monitoring of blood pressure, renal function, and fluid status

stages.event: Worsening headache with a single episode of vomiting
stages.nurse: Doctor, he just threw up and says his head's pounding worse than before — are we treating him or just waiting on the CT?
stages.feedback: Empiric factor IX should be given the moment head trauma is recognized in a child with hemophilia, not held for imaging results.

stages.event: Increasing lethargy with one pupil now sluggish and larger than the other

stages.feedback: The subdural is expanding while factor replacement is delayed — the window to prevent this closes fast without treatment.

stages.event: Cushing triad with herniation
stages.nurse: Doctor, he's bradycardic with his pressure through the roof and barely responding — we need to act right now!
stages.feedback: Without empiric factor IX replacement, the expanding subdural hematoma progressed to herniation — treat first, image second is the rule for head trauma in hemophilia.

debrief.goodOutcome: Empiric factor IX replacement given immediately, CT confirmed a small stable subdural hematoma without midline shift, and Ethan was admitted neurologically intact for observation.
debrief.badOutcome: Treatment was delayed pending CT confirmation, and the subdural hematoma expanded to herniation and death.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized that a normal exam doesn't rule out intracranial hemorrhage in a child with severe hemophilia, gave empiric factor IX immediately without waiting for CT, confirmed the timing of his last dose, obtained CT alongside treatment, and reassessed him serially — Ethan stayed neurologically intact and was admitted for observation.
debrief.outcomePoor: The case ended without empiric factor IX replacement ever being given — treatment was delayed pending imaging, and the evolving subdural hematoma progressed unchecked.
learningPoints: The foundation of hemophilia treatment is urgent clotting factor replacement guided by the presenting complaint, not by diagnostic imaging.
Head trauma in a child with hemophilia requires empiric factor replacement even with a normal neurologic exam and no scalp hematoma.
Standard 'low-risk' minor head injury observation criteria assume normal coagulation and do not apply to patients with bleeding disorders.
Trough factor levels between scheduled prophylactic doses can leave an otherwise well-controlled hemophilia patient transiently vulnerable to bleeding after trauma.
A mismatch between the ED's stocked factor product and the patient's home brand should never delay treatment — dose the equivalent product on hand.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing that a normal exam does not exclude intracranial hemorrhage in a child with hemophilia
Giving empiric factor IX replacement immediately, without waiting for CT confirmation
Confirming the timing of the last prophylactic factor dose to recognize trough vulnerability
Rejecting standard low-risk minor head injury observation-only pathways for this patient
Obtaining CT head alongside, not instead of, empiric factor administration
Serial neurologic reassessment for signs of an expanding intracranial hemorrhage

debrief.goodOutcome: Ileocolic intussusception recognized from the colic-with-lethargy pattern, confirmed on ultrasound, and successfully reduced with an air enema.
debrief.badOutcome: The diagnosis was missed or delayed, and an unrecognized intussusception progressed to bowel ischemia, perforation, and shock.

learningPoints: Intussusception classically presents with intermittent colic, legs drawn up, and lethargy between episodes — a leading cause of bowel obstruction in infants.
Lethargy can be the dominant or only presenting feature and is easily mistaken for sepsis or a neurologic process.
Currant-jelly stool is a late finding — don't wait for it to raise concern or start the workup.
Abdominal ultrasound (target/donut sign) is the diagnostic test of choice; air or contrast enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic in the stable child.
Surgery is reserved for failed reduction, perforation, peritonitis, or an unstable child.
missedOpportunities: Recognizing the intermittent colic-with-lethargy pattern without waiting for currant-jelly stool
Fluid resuscitation and keeping the child NPO
Abdominal ultrasound to confirm the diagnosis

learningPoints: Kawasaki disease: fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 clinical criteria (conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy) — the danger is coronary artery aneurysms.

learningPoints: Painless rectal bleeding in a toddler is itself a red flag for Meckel's diverticulum, not a reassuring finding — a well-appearing child does not rule out serious pathology.
The 'rule of 2s' — about 2% prevalence, roughly 2 feet from the ileocecal valve, about 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue, usually symptomatic before age 2, and about twice as common in boys.
Ectopic gastric mucosa within the diverticulum secretes acid that ulcerates the adjacent normal ileal mucosa, producing painless lower GI hemorrhage.

Technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel's) scan is the diagnostic study of choice, taken up by ectopic gastric mucosa; definitive management is surgical resection.

learningPoints: Medical child abuse should enter the differential when reported symptoms are out of proportion to exam findings, follow an atypical course, or fail to respond to standard treatment.
Perpetrators are the biological mother in about 90% of cases, often with a medical background, and are frequently described by staff as devoted, calm, and likeable even during dramatic 'emergencies.'
A hallmark clue is that events are witnessed only when the caregiver is alone with the child and resolve rapidly once the child is separated, stimulated, or observed by others.
Findings that are physiologically implausible or mismatched with the history should prompt suspicion of fabrication rather than escalation of workup.
Emergency clinicians are mandated reporters; suspected medical child abuse requires involving social work and child protection teams and reporting to CPS, not just ruling out organic disease.

debrief.goodOutcome: Viral myocarditis with early cardiogenic shock recognized early via ECG and echocardiography; fluids managed cautiously and pediatric cardiology/PICU engaged for ICU-level admission.

learningPoints: Pediatric myocarditis often presents with a nonspecific flu-like prodrome — GI symptoms, malaise, poor feeding — rather than obvious cardiac complaints

Coxsackievirus, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, and human herpesvirus 6 are among the most commonly implicated pathogens in pediatric myocarditis

stages.feedback: This case turns on speed of recognition and consultation, not on medications or fluids; a delay of this length converts a salvageable torsion into testicular loss.

debrief.goodOutcome: Left neonatal testicular torsion recognized emergently and treated with urgent urology consultation and surgical exploration while the testicle was still likely salvageable.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized a firm, discolored, minimally tender neonatal scrotal mass as testicular torsion rather than anchoring on hydrocele or hernia, clarified that the finding was new since a normal two-week checkup, consulted urology emergently, and moved to surgical exploration with contralateral fixation — the commonly-missed diagnosis, caught in time.
debrief.outcomePoor: The scrotal finding was never clearly worked up as torsion — urology was not consulted emergently and no surgical exploration was arranged, so the window for testicular salvage closed.
debrief.badOutcome: Dismissed as a hydrocele or hernia, the torsion went unrecognized until the window for testicular salvage had passed.
learningPoints: Testicular torsion has a bimodal age distribution — one peak in the first year of life, the other around puberty — and the neonatal form is a distinct entity from adolescent torsion.
Neonatal torsion is extravaginal: the spermatic cord and tunica vaginalis twist together above an unfixed tunica, unlike the intravaginal 'bell-clapper' torsion of older boys.
A firm, non-tender or minimally tender, discolored scrotal mass in a newborn is the classic presentation and is easily mistaken for a hydrocele or incarcerated hernia.

Prenatal (in-utero) torsion usually presents with an already-infarcted, non-salvageable testis, while postnatal-onset neonatal torsion is a true surgical emergency with a real chance of salvage if acted on urgently.
The cremasteric reflex is physiologically unreliable in neonates and should not be used to reassure against torsion the way it can be in adolescents.
missedOpportunities: Emergent urology or pediatric surgery consultation
History clarifying the finding was new since a normal two-week checkup rather than present at birth
Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler ordered without delaying surgical consultation
Counseling the family that testicular salvage is time-dependent
Surgical exploration with consideration of contralateral testicular fixation

learningPoints: Papilledema is the key exam finding that separates a secondary, vision-threatening headache from a primary headache disorder
Positional headache worse lying flat, transient visual obscurations, and pulsatile tinnitus are hallmark features of raised intracranial pressure

stages.nurse: Doctor, she says her tummy's bothering her more and she's stopped asking about her tablet — are we doing anything else for her right now?

stages.event: Her abdomen is now rigid and diffusely distended; she becomes tachycardic and hypotensive as septic shock sets in from the unrecognized bowel perforation.
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's barely responding and her pressure just crashed — her belly's rock hard!

debrief.goodOutcome: Seatbelt-sign triad recognized despite a reassuring exam; CT abdomen/pelvis and lumbar spine imaging obtained, spinal precautions maintained, serial abdominal exams performed, and trauma surgery consulted early.
debrief.outcomeGood: You stayed suspicious despite her benign-looking exam and near-normal vitals, imaged her abdomen and spine, kept her immobilized, reassessed her serially, and got trauma surgery on board early — the bowel/mesenteric injury and unstable L2 fracture were caught before they could progress.
debrief.outcomePoor: Reassured by her calm affect and near-normal vitals, the abdominal and spine imaging were deferred, and the occult injuries were left to progress unrecognized.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on a benign initial exam and stable vitals, the bowel/mesenteric injury under the seatbelt sign went unrecognized and progressed to peritonitis and septic shock, while the unstable L2 fracture remained unprotected.
learningPoints: A seatbelt sign after a pediatric MVC is a marker for the classic triad of Chance-type spine fracture, hollow viscus injury, and intra-abdominal solid organ/vascular injury.

Lap-belt-only or improperly positioned restraint (common once children outgrow booster seats but are still too small for adult belt fit) transmits flexion-distraction forces across the abdomen and lumbar spine.
A reassuring initial exam and near-normal vitals do not rule out serious injury in this population — just as a normal neuro exam does not exclude a basilar skull fracture or intracranial hemorrhage.
Restrained children have far lower overall injury risk than unrestrained children, but improper positioning of the restraint itself creates a distinct, easily missed injury pattern.

High suspicion plus CT abdomen/pelvis, dedicated spine imaging, spinal precautions, serial exams, and early surgical consultation prevent missed bowel perforation/peritonitis and missed unstable spine fracture with neurologic injury.
missedOpportunities: Maintaining high suspicion for intra-abdominal and spinal injury despite a reassuring initial exam and near-normal vitals

Dedicated lumbar spine imaging to evaluate for a flexion-distraction fracture
Maintaining spinal precautions until the spine is definitively cleared
Serial abdominal examinations and reassessment
Early trauma/pediatric surgery consultation given the seatbelt-sign triad

deterioration.trajectory: hemodynamically stable pediatric orthopedic injury — the risk is a missed growth-plate fracture from anchoring on 'ankle sprain,' not physiologic deterioration

stages.event: Diagnostic anchoring — the exam and workup are stuck on a routine sprain narrative
stages.nurse: Doctor, Mom's asking again why we can't just wrap this and send her home — she's getting more uncomfortable waiting. What's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Without localizing tenderness to the joint line and getting dedicated ankle films, the workup drifts toward a routine sprain and the growth-plate injury risks being missed entirely.

debrief.goodOutcome: Growth-plate fracture localized on exam, characterized with CT, splinted non-weight-bearing, and referred to orthopedics for definitive management.
debrief.outcomeGood: You localized tenderness to the anterolateral joint line, obtained dedicated ankle films and CT to characterize the fracture, kept her strictly non-weight-bearing, and looped in orthopedics — the growth-plate fracture hiding behind a 'sprain' presentation was caught and managed appropriately.
debrief.outcomePoor: The exam stayed anchored on the lateral ligaments, and Maya was treated and discharged as a routine ankle sprain without dedicated imaging or orthopedic involvement.
debrief.badOutcome: Anchored on the story of a typical ankle sprain, the juvenile growth-plate fracture went unrecognized, unsplinted appropriately, and unreferred to orthopedics — risking articular displacement and long-term ankle dysfunction.
learningPoints: In younger children, growth plates and bones are weaker than ligaments, so ankle trauma more often fractures the physis than sprains it; as adolescents approach skeletal maturity, ligamentous sprains become relatively more common.

Juvenile Tillaux and triplane fractures occur only in the narrow adolescent window when the distal tibial physis is fusing asymmetrically, because the anterolateral segment is the last part to close and stays vulnerable to external rotation.
These fractures classically mimic a routine ankle sprain and are easy to miss on cursory exam and even on standard plain films.
Point tenderness over the anterolateral joint line/distal tibial physis, not the lateral ligaments, is the exam clue that should redirect suspicion away from a simple sprain.

Because these fractures involve the articular surface, they need orthopedic evaluation, often CT for surgical planning, and possible operative fixation to avoid malunion and chronic ankle dysfunction.
missedOpportunities: Palpating specifically over the anterolateral joint line/distal tibial physis instead of just the lateral ligaments
Obtaining a dedicated 3-view ankle series and CT when plain films were equivocal
Keeping the patient strictly non-weight-bearing instead of dispensing a walking boot
Consulting orthopedics for an articular growth-plate fracture

stages.nurse: Doctor, she just about walked herself dizzy trying to get back out to the game — are we doing anything more here, or can I let her go?
stages.feedback: Ambulatory desaturation and climbing tachycardia are early clues to pulmonary embolism that resting vitals alone miss — risk-factor recognition on history should be driving the workup by now.

stages.event: She's now short of breath even lying still, and her neck veins look more prominent than before.
stages.nurse: Doctor, her oxygen keeps dropping and she looks worse by the minute — I need a plan from you now.
stages.feedback: Without confirmatory imaging, the clot burden is propagating toward right heart strain and obstructive shock — CT pulmonary angiography should not be withheld for lack of a validated pediatric decision rule.

stages.event: Massive clot burden causes sudden loss of pulses — the room moves into cardiac arrest.
stages.nurse: Doctor, she's unresponsive and I can't feel a pulse — I need you at the bedside right now!
stages.feedback: Untreated pulmonary embolism with progressive right heart strain culminated in obstructive cardiac arrest — prompt anticoagulation once the clot was confirmed would have halted propagation before this point.

debrief.goodOutcome: Pediatric PE recognized from combined provoked risk factors, confirmed on CT pulmonary angiography, and treated with prompt anticoagulation.
debrief.badOutcome: The combined risk factors went unrecognized, imaging was never obtained, and the clot propagated to obstructive shock and arrest.
debrief.outcomeGood: You elicited both risk factors, didn't let reassuring resting vitals or the musculoskeletal/anxiety narrative distract you, confirmed pulmonary embolism on CTPA, started anticoagulation, and looped in pediatric hematology — Maya was stabilized and admitted with a clear treatment plan.
debrief.outcomePoor: The encounter ended without imaging or anticoagulation in place. The combined risk factors — recent post-surgical immobility and estrogen-containing contraception — went unrecognized, and the pulmonary embolism was never treated.
learningPoints: Pediatric VTE is almost always provoked by an identifiable risk factor, unlike adult VTE, which is frequently idiopathic.

Adolescents are more likely than younger children to have two or more simultaneous VTE risk factors present at diagnosis — ask specifically about both recent immobility/surgery and hormonal contraception.
No validated clinical decision rule (no pediatric Wells or PERC equivalent) exists for pediatric VTE, so risk-factor recognition on history is the primary diagnostic tool.
Reassuring resting vitals, including a normal room-air SpO2, do not exclude pediatric PE — exertional or ambulatory desaturation can unmask it.

Once VTE is confirmed, anticoagulation should start promptly to halt clot propagation and prevent obstructive shock; involve pediatric hematology early given the lack of pediatric-specific risk-stratification protocols.
missedOpportunities: Asking specifically about recent surgery/immobility and hormonal contraceptive use
Rechecking vitals with ambulation/exertion rather than relying on reassuring resting numbers
Obtaining CT pulmonary angiography despite the absence of a validated pediatric decision rule
Starting anticoagulation promptly once the clot was confirmed
Consulting pediatric hematology early

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity — an unrecognized shunt malfunction progresses through worsening bradycardia and widening pulse pressure to Cushing physiology, anisocoria, herniation, and death if neurosurgery and ICP management are never engaged.

stages.event: Worsening lethargy with early Cushing physiology
stages.nurse: He's harder to wake than he was twenty minutes ago, and his heart rate keeps trending down — what do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Early signs of rising intracranial pressure — worsening bradycardia and a widening pulse pressure — are being missed because no imaging or neurosurgery consult has been initiated.

stages.event: Anisocoria and Cushing triad — impending herniation
stages.nurse: His right pupil looks bigger than the left now and his breathing's gone irregular — I need you at the bedside right now.
stages.feedback: This is the Cushing triad of impending herniation — bradycardia, widening pulse pressure, and irregular respirations, with new anisocoria. Neurosurgery should already be involved and ICP/airway management ready.

stages.event: Herniation, respiratory arrest
stages.nurse: He's stopped breathing on his own — I need help in here right now!
stages.feedback: Without neurosurgical intervention and emergent airway/ICP management, uncal herniation progressed to brainstem compression, apnea, and death.

debrief.goodOutcome: Shunt malfunction recognized despite a deceptively benign early exam; head CT and shunt series obtained and specifically compared to Eli's baseline studies; neurosurgery consulted emergently and in parallel with imaging; lumbar puncture avoided; Eli taken to the OR for shunt revision.
debrief.badOutcome: The evaluation anchored on gastroenteritis, constipation, or migraine; imaging was interpreted in isolation or never compared to baseline; neurosurgery was consulted late or not at all — Eli progressed through worsening bradycardia, widening pulse pressure, anisocoria, and herniation.
debrief.outcomeGood: You kept shunt malfunction on the differential despite a reassuring initial exam, obtained a head CT and shunt series and specifically compared them to his baseline studies rather than reading them in isolation, consulted neurosurgery emergently and in parallel with imaging, and avoided lumbar puncture given his shunt and possible elevated ICP — he went to the OR for shunt revision and recovered fully.
debrief.outcomePoor: The workup drifted toward gastroenteritis, constipation, or migraine; imaging was obtained but never compared to his baseline (or not obtained at all), and neurosurgery was engaged late — his mental status, pupillary exam, and vital signs progressively worsened toward herniation.
learningPoints: VP shunt malfunction has an annual failure rate as high as 5%, and mechanical failure can present with nonspecific vomiting, headache, and lethargy that mimic viral gastroenteritis, migraine, or constipation
A normal-appearing CT or shunt series does not reliably exclude shunt malfunction; imaging must always be compared to the patient's own prior baseline study, not judged in isolation
Vomiting with a paradoxically normal-to-low heart rate in a shunted child, rather than the tachycardia expected from dehydration, can be an early sign of rising intracranial pressure
Risk factors for shunt complications include younger age, prematurity, more prior revisions, and a shorter interval from placement to first revision

Untreated shunt malfunction can progress to herniation and death, with malfunction-related mortality reported as high as 1% to 2%, so neurosurgery should be involved early rather than only after imaging 'proves' the diagnosis
missedOpportunities: Maintaining suspicion for shunt malfunction despite a reassuring initial exam
Comparing head CT and shunt series directly to Eli's baseline imaging rather than reading them in isolation
Consulting neurosurgery emergently and in parallel with imaging
Recognizing early Cushing physiology (bradycardia, widening pulse pressure, irregular respirations, anisocoria) and preparing for emergent ICP/airway management
Avoiding lumbar puncture in a shunted child with possible elevated intracranial pressure

deterioration.trajectory: ACE-inhibitor angioedema causing progressive upper-airway swelling — untreated, it advances from voice change and drooling to complete obstruction; epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids rarely reverse it, so timely, controlled airway management is the only reliable intervention.

stages.event: Progressive upper-airway swelling
stages.nurse: Doctor, his voice is barely a whisper now and he's drooling more than when he came in — what's the plan here?
stages.feedback: Without continuous airway reassessment and a difficult-airway setup in place, the swelling is progressing toward a point where a controlled intubation becomes far harder.

stages.event: Complete airway obstruction — can't intubate, can't oxygenate
stages.nurse: He's not moving air at all — I can't get a breath into him! Starting compressions!
stages.feedback: The airway wasn't secured while it was still manageable. By the time it was completely obstructed, it became an unrecoverable can't-intubate-can't-oxygenate scenario.

debrief.goodOutcome: ACE-inhibitor angioedema recognized, the ACE inhibitor stopped, and the airway secured early and safely.
debrief.badOutcome: The airway was managed as if this were allergic anaphylaxis and treated expectantly until it obstructed completely.
debrief.outcomeGood: You recognized this as bradykinin-mediated ACE-inhibitor angioedema, stopped the lisinopril, reassessed the airway, staged a difficult-airway double setup with anesthesia and ENT, and secured the airway with an awake fiberoptic technique before it closed — while giving epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids and specific bradykinin-pathway therapy empirically.
debrief.outcomePoor: The airway wasn't secured while it was still manageable, or the case was treated purely as allergic anaphylaxis while waiting on medications to work as the swelling advanced to complete obstruction.
learningPoints: ACE-inhibitor angioedema is bradykinin-mediated — it often doesn't respond to epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids.
The airway is the threat; manage it early and in a controlled way with a difficult-airway plan.
No hives/itching and an ACE-inhibitor history point away from allergic anaphylaxis.
Have a double setup with a surgical airway ready before the airway closes — avoid standard paralytic RSI on a distorted, swollen airway.
Involve anesthesia and ENT early; a controlled awake intubation is far safer than a crash airway.
missedOpportunities: Continuous airway reassessment for voice change, drooling, and stridor
Preparing a difficult-airway double setup (awake fiberoptic + surgical airway ready) before attempting intubation
Securing the airway early, before complete obstruction
Stopping the ACE inhibitor and involving anesthesia/ENT early

deterioration.trajectory: untreated anaphylaxis progresses from airway/breathing compromise to cardiovascular collapse and arrest

stages.event: Progressive angioedema and bronchospasm without epinephrine
stages.nurse: Doctor, her voice is getting more muffled and her sats are dropping into the 80s — she's really struggling now. What do you want to do?
stages.feedback: Epinephrine is first-line and time-critical in anaphylaxis — antihistamines and steroids don't reverse airway swelling or shock, and delay lets both keep progressing.

stages.event: Complete airway obstruction and cardiovascular collapse
stages.nurse: She's unresponsive, I can't feel a pulse!
stages.feedback: Without IM epinephrine, oxygen and positioning, and aggressive fluids, anaphylactic shock progressed to airway obstruction and cardiac arrest — delayed epinephrine is the single most common preventable cause of death.

debrief.goodOutcome: Anaphylaxis recognized and treated with immediate IM epinephrine, oxygen, positioning, and fluids; bronchospasm and refractory hypotension escalated appropriately, airway anticipated, and the patient stabilized and admitted for monitored observation.
debrief.outcomeGood: You gave IM epinephrine right away, positioned her supine with high-flow oxygen, resuscitated with IV fluids, treated bronchospasm, escalated to an epinephrine infusion when she stayed hypotensive, prepared for a difficult airway, and watched for a biphasic reaction — she stabilized and moved to a monitored bed.
debrief.badOutcome: Epinephrine was delayed or withheld in favor of antihistamines and steroids alone, and anaphylactic shock progressed to airway obstruction and cardiac arrest.
debrief.outcomePoor: Without prompt IM epinephrine, the airway swelling and shock kept progressing until she arrested.
learningPoints: Epinephrine IM is first-line and life-saving in anaphylaxis — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts, never substitutes.
Give epinephrine immediately, 0.3-0.5 mg IM in the lateral thigh, and repeat every 5-15 minutes as needed.
Refractory hypotension needs an epinephrine infusion; ask about beta-blocker use and add glucagon if so.
Anticipate airway compromise from progressive angioedema and prepare early for a difficult airway.
Observe for biphasic reactions for several hours after apparent resolution.
missedOpportunities: Immediate IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg, lateral thigh), repeated as needed
Supine positioning with legs elevated and high-flow oxygen
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for hypotension
Epinephrine infusion for refractory hypotension
Preparation for a difficult airway and extended observation for a biphasic reaction

speech.text: ICU bed is ready — he's on the ventilator with a confirmed airway, fluids and pressors running, antibiotics on board.

deterioration.trajectory: high-acuity unrecognized esophageal intubation — transient preoxygenation reserve and falsely reassuring breath sounds/colorimetric change mask the misplaced tube until hypoxia collapses into bradycardic pre-arrest and cardiac arrest

stages.event: Preoxygenation reserve exhausted — true hypoxia unmasked
stages.nurse: Doctor, his sat's crashing and he's going dusky around the lips — I need direction right now!
stages.feedback: The falsely reassuring breath sounds, color change, and a few minutes of preoxygenation reserve bought time, but with no confirmed airway the true hypoxia is now unmasked — continuous waveform capnography checked immediately after intubation would have caught this before it got here.

stages.event: Hypoxic bradycardia — pre-arrest
stages.nurse: He's bradying down hard and I can barely get a sat reading — what do you want me to do?
stages.feedback: Unrecognized esophageal placement has progressed to hypoxic bradycardia on the edge of arrest — the airway still hasn't been confirmed or corrected.

stages.event: Hypoxic cardiac arrest
stages.nurse: He's lost his pulse — starting compressions, I need you here now!
stages.feedback: Uncorrected esophageal intubation progressed to hypoxic cardiac arrest — mortality approaches 100% when an apneic patient's airway is never confirmed. Continuous waveform capnography checked immediately after intubation, with prompt removal and reintubation if the trace isn't sustained, is what prevents this.

debrief.goodOutcome: The esophageal intubation was caught within minutes by continuous waveform capnography, corrected before arrest, and the underlying septic pneumonia was treated with fluids, pressors, and antibiotics.
debrief.badOutcome: Reassured by 'equal' breath sounds and an early colorimetric color change, the misplaced tube went unrecognized until hypoxia progressed to cardiac arrest.
debrief.outcomeGood: You placed continuous waveform capnography immediately after intubation, recognized the flat trace as esophageal placement despite falsely reassuring breath sounds and pulse oximetry, pulled and replaced the tube before arrest, correctly read the persistently low ETCO2 afterward as shock-driven dead space rather than under-ventilation, and treated the septic pneumonia with fluids, pressors, and antibiotics — he went to the ICU intact.
debrief.outcomePoor: The tube was never re-checked with continuous waveform capnography, so the esophageal placement went unrecognized. Falsely reassuring breath sounds, an early colorimetric color change, and a pulse oximeter still running on preoxygenation reserve delayed detection until hypoxia was irreversible.
learningPoints: Continuous waveform capnography — a sustained rising-and-falling trace over several breaths — is the most reliable bedside confirmation of endotracheal tube placement, more reliable than auscultation or a single colorimetric check.
Colorimetric capnometry can show a transient false-positive color change from residual gastric CO2 immediately after an esophageal intubation.
Unrecognized esophageal intubation is highly lethal — roughly 69% mortality overall, approaching 100% if the patient was apneic on arrival.
Pulse oximetry reflects oxygenation, not airway position, and can stay falsely reassuring for several minutes after a misplaced tube due to preoxygenation reserve.

A widened PaCO2–ETCO2 gradient (persistently low ETCO2 despite a confirmed, correctly placed tube) reflects reduced pulmonary blood flow from shock, hypovolemia, or PE — treat the perfusion problem with fluids and pressors, not by increasing minute ventilation.
missedOpportunities: Placing continuous waveform capnography immediately after intubation instead of relying on breath sounds and a single colorimetric check
Recognizing a flat or non-sustaining ETCO2 waveform as esophageal intubation and immediately pulling and replacing the tube
Not trusting a falsely reassuring pulse oximeter reading to confirm tube position
Treating a persistently low ETCO2 after correct placement as shock-driven dead space with fluids and pressors rather than increasing ventilation
Starting antibiotics and hemodynamic support promptly for the underlying septic pneumonia
